target.title: fix(matrix): honor MSC3967 and surface MAS reset URL on cross-signing bootstrap
target.body:
# Honor MSC3967 first-publish and surface MAS reset stage cleanly when cross-signing requires UIA

## What this PR does

Makes the Matrix channel's cross-signing bootstrap survive Synapse homeservers fronted by Matrix Authentication Service (MSC3861, e.g. ESS deployments) when the bot logs in with a Personal Access Token. Today those bots fail with a misleading "provide `matrix.password` for `m.login.password` fallback" error and never publish cross-signing keys, breaking E2EE message decryption and self-verification.

After this change:

- A bot with **no** server-side master cross-signing key publishes a fresh identity using the [MSC3967](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-spec-proposals/pull/3967) first-publish path (no UIA, no auth callback round trip).
- A bot whose local secret-storage state is stale relative to the server gets a typed `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` carrying the MAS reset URL, so the operator can complete the OAuth re-auth in a browser and re-run the bootstrap inside the MAS approval window.
- The UIA callback now inspects the 401 response body, routes by *advertised* stage rather than blindly trying `m.login.dummy` then `m.login.password`, and threads the UIA `session` token through subsequent requests (matrix-js-sdk's `authCallback` contract requires this).

Bug report with full reproduction steps and environment details: [MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md](./MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md) ŌĆö should be filed as the companion issue.

## Changes

### `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/types.ts`

- New exported error classes:
  - `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError(stages, resetUrl?, session?)` ŌĆö thrown when the homeserver demands `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset`. Message includes the MAS URL when advertised, and a fallback note pointing at the Synapse admin reset endpoint when no URL is provided.
  - `MatrixUiaUnsupportedStagesError(stages, hasPassword)` ŌĆö thrown when the homeserver advertises only stages this client cannot satisfy non-interactively (e.g. `m.login.password` on a passwordless PAT-only bot).
- New types: `MatrixHomeserverCapabilities`, `MatrixUiaResponseBody`.

### `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/crypto-bootstrap.ts`

- `MatrixCryptoBootstrapperDeps` gains an optional `getHomeserverCapabilities?: () => Promise<MatrixHomeserverCapabilities>`.
- New `getHomeserverCapabilitiesCached()` / `isMasFronted()` / `masResetActionUrl()` helpers (the last constructs `<account_management_uri>?action=org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` per MSC2965 ┬¦6.4.4).
- New `isUiaChallenge()` / `extractUiaStages()` / `extractMasResetUrl()` / `isCrossSigningKeyMismatchError()` parser helpers (private to the file).
- `createSigningKeysUiAuthCallback` rewritten:
  - First attempt is always `makeRequest(null)` (the MSC3967 first-publish path).
  - If that throws a 401-with-UIA-body, parse the advertised stages.
  - Route by stage: `m.login.dummy` ŌåÆ `{type, session}`; `m.login.password` (with configured password) ŌåÆ `{type, identifier, password, session}`; `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` ŌåÆ `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` carrying URL (from the 401 body if present, else constructed via MSC2965 from the cached `account_management_uri`) + session.
  - On MAS-fronted homeservers without a satisfiable stage, also surface `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError`. On non-MAS, throw `MatrixUiaUnsupportedStagesError`.
  - All other 401 bodies and non-401 errors re-throw unchanged.
- `bootstrapCrossSigning()` keeps the existing first-pass / forced-reset / fallback-reset structure, with two changes:
  - Every catch block now propagates `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` instances as-is rather than wrapping/logging them, so the actionable URL reaches the CLI cleanly.
  - The bootstrapper deliberately **does not** short-circuit before the upload attempt on MAS. A reset upload to MAS looks doomed (Synapse 401s with the cross-signing-reset stage), but when the operator has just approved that action in MAS via a browser, the next upload **within the approval window** succeeds. Letting the upload happen in both states means the same code path serves both the "tell me what to do" and "do it" sides of recovery ŌĆö the 401 turns into a structured error with the URL; the 200 lands cross-signing.

### `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts`

- New `detectHomeserverCapabilities()` method that probes `/_matrix/client/v1/auth_metadata` and returns `{ msAuthService, accountManagementUri }`. `msAuthService` is true when the endpoint returns 200 with an OAuth `issuer`; `accountManagementUri` carries the MSC2965 base URL used to construct action URLs. Result is cached on the instance.
  - Note: an earlier draft used `unstable_features["org.matrix.msc3861"]` from `client.getVersions()`. That doesn't work ŌĆö Synapse 1.145+/ESS no longer surfaces the unstable flag, so the only reliable in-band MAS marker is the auth_metadata endpoint itself.
- Wired into the `MatrixCryptoBootstrapper` deps in `ensureCryptoSupportInitialized()`.

## What this PR explicitly does **not** do

- Does **not** implement an OAuth re-auth flow inside the bot. The `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` stage is designed to require a human in a browser; we surface the URL and stop. Operators who need fully unattended recovery should use Synapse's `POST /_synapse/admin/v1/users/<user>/_reset_cross_signing_keys` admin endpoint and then let the bot do a clean MSC3967 first-publish.
- Does **not** modify matrix-js-sdk. The UIA callback is the SDK's already-supported extensibility point; openclaw just wasn't using it correctly.
- Does **not** touch `account_data` to "reset" cross-signing ŌĆö server-side cross-signing public keys live in a separate keys table queried via `/keys/query`, not in `account_data`, so clearing `m.cross_signing.{master,self_signing,user_signing}` would not unblock MSC3967. (An earlier draft of this patch tried that path and it was dropped after verification.)
- Does **not** change matrix-rust-sdk OTK regeneration behaviour, the `verify list/accept/start/sas/confirm-sas` gateway-RPC routing, or the Bonjour mDNS spam ŌĆö those are separate issues.

## Verified end-to-end on real MAS-fronted Synapse

Confirmed against `matrix.thepolycule.ca` (Synapse 1.145.0+ess.1, MAS-fronted, OAuth issuer `https://auth.matrix.thepolycule.ca/`). With this patch the bot can now:

1. Detect MAS via `/_matrix/client/v1/auth_metadata` (status 200, OAuth issuer present).
2. On `verify bootstrap --force-reset-cross-signing` with no MAS approval current, attempt the reset upload, receive Synapse's 401, and surface `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` with the URL `https://auth.matrix.thepolycule.ca/account/?action=org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` (constructed via MSC2965 from the cached `account_management_uri`).
3. After the operator opens that URL and approves the cross-signing reset action in MAS, the next `verify bootstrap --force-reset-cross-signing` within the MAS approval window has its `/keys/device_signing/upload` succeed (200), and `/_matrix/client/v3/keys/query` thereafter returns the bot's freshly published `master_keys` and `self_signing_keys` for the first time.

Pre-existing matrix-rust-sdk OTK regeneration collisions on `/keys/upload` (`signed_curve25519:AAAAAAAAAA0 already exists`) and incomplete owner-side device verification are observable in the same e2e run but are unrelated to this PR ŌĆö they are filed as separate upstream issues.

## Tests

`extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/crypto-bootstrap.test.ts`:

- 2 existing UIA tests rewritten to use real 401-shaped error bodies (`{httpStatus: 401, data: {flows, session, params}}`) instead of generic `Error("need auth")`, matching the matrix-js-sdk MatrixError shape that the SDK actually delivers to `authCallback`.
- 5 new regression tests:
  - `uses dummy UIA stage when advertised even alongside other stages` ŌĆö verifies stage prioritisation when both dummy and password are offered.
  - `surfaces MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError with the MAS reset URL` ŌĆö asserts the error carries `resetUrl`, `session`, and `stages`.
  - `attempts forced reset on MAS so a freshly approved reset can land within the MAS window` ŌĆö asserts the bootstrapper does NOT short-circuit; it attempts the upload so a current MAS approval can land cross-signing on the same call.
  - `allows MSC3967 first-publish on MAS when the user has no master key yet` ŌĆö asserts no-auth path succeeds.
  - `falls through to a reset upload after an import-key mismatch on MAS, surfacing the MAS URL on 401` ŌĆö asserts the post-mismatch fallback attempts a `setupNewCrossSigning` upload (so a recent MAS approval can land it) and surfaces `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError` with the MSC2965-constructed URL when no approval is current.

`pnpm test --filter @openclaw/matrix` (or the upstream equivalent) ŌĆö 26 tests in `crypto-bootstrap.test.ts` pass; full sdk-dir suite (5 files, 65 tests) passes.

## Test plan for reviewers

End-to-end against a Synapse 1.79+ deployment fronted by MAS (e.g. ESS) with a PAT-authenticated bot:

1. Create the bot account in MAS, mint a PAT, configure `channels.matrix.<account>.userId` and `MATRIX_ACCESS_TOKEN`, deploy.
2. Ensure server-side cross-signing is empty for the bot (use `POST /_synapse/admin/v1/users/<user>/_reset_cross_signing_keys` if not).
3. Run `openclaw matrix verify bootstrap`.
   - Expected: `POST .../keys/device_signing/upload [... 200]` (MSC3967 path).
   - Expected: `PUT .../room_keys/version [... 200]` (key backup created).
4. Run `openclaw matrix verify status` ŌĆö should show `Cross-signing published: yes`.
5. Negative test: corrupt the bot's local secret storage `m.cross_signing.master` blob and re-run `verify bootstrap`. Expected: clean exit with `MatrixCrossSigningResetRequiredError`, MAS URL printed, no silent retry loop.
6. Encrypted DM round-trip from a verified Element X session ŌĆö expect successful Megolm decrypt in the bot log and a decrypted reply on the sender's client.

## Related

- Bug report: [MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md](./MAS-CROSSSIGNING-BUG-REPORT.md)
- [MSC3967 ŌĆö Do not require UIA when first uploading cross-signing keys](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-spec-proposals/pull/3967)
- [MSC3861 ŌĆö Next-generation auth for Matrix](https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-spec-proposals/pull/3861)
- [Synapse PR #17509 ŌĆö `org.matrix.cross_signing_reset` UIA stage for MAS](https://github.com/element-hq/synapse/pull/17509)

target.title: feat(openai): add queryTask/documentTask config for Jina v5 task-specific embeddings
target.body:
## Summary

Add typed `queryTask` and `documentTask` configuration under `agents.*.memorySearch` for Jina v5 task-specific embeddings through the OpenAI-compatible embedding provider.

Closes #74419

## Background

Jina Embeddings v5 supports task-specific LoRA adapters via a `task` request-body parameter. Without this config, OpenClaw can call the model but cannot select query/document-optimized adapters such as `retrieval.query` and `retrieval.passage`.

## Changes

- Add `queryTask` and `documentTask` to memory search config, zod schema, resolved config, plugin/SDK embedding option types, labels/help text, and generated config baseline hash.
- Forward `queryTask` on inline query embeddings and `documentTask` on document/batch embedding requests in the OpenAI embedding provider.
- Include `documentTask` in OpenAI batch embedding cache identity.
- Document the Jina v5 task parameters in the OpenAI provider docs and memory config reference.
- Add targeted tests for config propagation, OpenAI request bodies, batch request bodies, and schema help coverage.

## Supported task values

- `retrieval.query` / `retrieval.passage` ŌĆö asymmetric retrieval
- `text-matching` ŌĆö symmetric semantic matching
- `clustering` ŌĆö cluster-friendly embeddings
- `classification` ŌĆö classification-friendly embeddings
- `separation` ŌĆö separation-focused embeddings

When left unset, Jina v5 defaults to general-purpose embeddings.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** OpenClaw should emit Jina v5 `task` request-body parameters from `queryTask` for query embeddings and `documentTask` for document/indexing embeddings when using the OpenAI-compatible embedding provider.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux/Node 22, running the actual `extensions/openai/embedding-provider.ts` code against a local OpenAI-compatible HTTP capture server. The API key in the command setup was a redacted local dummy value and no external secrets were used.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** Ran `node --import tsx /tmp/jina-proof.mjs`. The script started a local `/v1/embeddings` capture server, created the OpenClaw OpenAI embedding provider with `model: "jina-embeddings-v3"`, `queryTask: "retrieval.query"`, and `documentTask: "retrieval.passage"`, then called `provider.embedQuery("query text")` and `provider.embedBatch(["document text"])`.

**Evidence after fix:** Live terminal output from the run:

```bash
$ node --import tsx /tmp/jina-proof.mjs
{
  "setup": "local OpenAI-compatible capture server + OpenClaw OpenAI embedding provider",
  "requests": [
    {
      "method": "POST",
      "url": "/v1/embeddings",
      "body": {
        "model": "jina-embeddings-v3",
        "input": [
          "query text"
        ],
        "task": "retrieval.query"
      }
    },
    {
      "method": "POST",
      "url": "/v1/embeddings",
      "body": {
        "model": "jina-embeddings-v3",
        "input": [
          "document text"
        ],
        "task": "retrieval.passage"
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The captured OpenClaw-generated query embedding request included `task: "retrieval.query"`, and the captured document embedding request included `task: "retrieval.passage"`. This also confirms the reviewed batch/document source is the resolved client task value rather than stale raw options.

**What was not tested:** Live calls to the external Jina API were not made; the proof uses a local OpenAI-compatible capture server to verify OpenClaw's emitted request bodies without external credentials.

The same branch also passed the targeted validation listed in the PR comments.

target.title: Fix/hooks agent accountid normalization
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2ŌĆō5 bullets:

- Problem: `POST /hooks/agent` silently drops `accountId` from incoming payloads because `HookAgentPayload` lacks the field, `normalizeAgentPayload()` never extracts it, and `dispatchAgentHook()` hand-builds a `CronJob` bypassing `normalizeCronJobCreate()`.
- Why it matters: Multi-account WhatsApp (and any channel requiring `accountId`) delivery fails silently ŌĆö the caller gets a success response but the message is never sent.
- What changed: Hook dispatch now routes through `normalizeCronJobCreate()`, which promotes legacy flat fields into proper `CronDelivery` with `accountId`. `resolveCronToolPolicy()` derives `disableMessageTool` for shared-contract callers from whether the target is fully resolved (explicit mode + accountId) instead of unconditionally disabling.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): The cron-owned path (`deliveryContract: "cron-owned"`) is untouched. No changes to `normalizeCronJobCreate()` logic itself ŌĆö only a JSDoc addition. No new config options or API surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #43866
- Related #27015, #24186, #42244, #23853, #16925, #30052, #29090

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `POST /hooks/agent` now correctly preserves `accountId` through to delivery, enabling multi-account WhatsApp delivery via hooks.
- For shared-contract hook runs with a fully resolved delivery target (explicit mode + accountId), the agent's message tool is no longer unconditionally disabled ŌĆö it remains available as a fallback. The existing `skipMessagingToolDelivery` guard (`run.ts:821-831`) prevents duplicate sends.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` ŌĆö the message tool was already available for shared-contract callers without delivery; this change refines *when* it stays enabled based on target resolution quality.
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: All (server-side code path)
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / Bun
- Model/provider: Any (not model/provider-specific)
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp (any multi-account channel)
- Relevant config (redacted): Hooks enabled with token, WhatsApp channel configured with multiple `accountId` connections

### Steps

1. Configure two WhatsApp connections with distinct `accountId` values.
2. Send `POST /hooks/agent` with `{ "message": "Hello", "channel": "whatsapp", "to": "1234567890", "accountId": "botnumber" }`.
3. Observe delivery result.

### Expected

- `accountId` flows through to `CronDelivery.accountId`, `resolveDeliveryTarget()` identifies the correct WhatsApp connection, and the message is delivered.

### Actual (before fix)

- `accountId` is silently dropped. Delivery fails because no connection can be identified. Caller receives success response.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

New tests added in second commit:
- `src/gateway/hooks.test.ts` ŌĆö `normalizeAgentPayload` extracts and trims `accountId` (present, absent, blank)
- `src/gateway/server.hooks.test.ts` ŌĆö integration test: `accountId` propagates into `job.delivery` via cron normalization pipeline
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.message-tool-policy.test.ts` ŌĆö 3 tests for shared-contract tool policy: fully resolved target (explicit + accountId ŌåÆ enabled), missing accountId (ŌåÆ disabled), implicitly resolved target (ŌåÆ disabled)

Verification: `pnpm tsgo` clean, `pnpm check` clean, all 59 tests across the 4 test files pass.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: typecheck (`pnpm tsgo`), lint/format (`pnpm check`), unit + integration tests pass for all modified test files
- Edge cases checked: blank/missing `accountId`, `normalizeCronJobCreate` returning null (guard added), implicit vs explicit delivery target mode, `mode: "none"` (verified `deliveryRequested` is already `false` so no extra clause needed)
- What you did **not** verify: live end-to-end delivery through a real WhatsApp multi-account setup; only verified through unit/integration tests and code tracing

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert both commits; the hand-built CronJob path is fully self-contained in the reverted `dispatchAgentHook()`.
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/hooks.ts`, `src/gateway/server/hooks.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`, `src/cron/normalize.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Hook agent dispatches returning early with "normalizeCronJobCreate returned null" warning (would indicate the normalizer rejects a payload shape it previously accepted via hand-built path).

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `normalizeCronJobCreate()` could reject a hook payload shape that the hand-built path previously accepted, causing hook dispatches to silently no-op.
  - Mitigation: A null-return guard logs a warning and still returns a `runId` to the caller. The normalizer is well-tested and accepts all fields the hook path provides. Integration test verifies the full pipeline.

- Risk: Enabling the message tool for shared-contract callers with fully resolved targets could cause duplicate sends (agent sends via tool + automatic delivery sends again).
  - Mitigation: The existing `skipMessagingToolDelivery` guard (`run.ts:821-831`) already detects when the agent's message tool sent to the same target and skips automatic delivery. This guard predates this change and is tested.

target.title: fix: ensure CLI processes exit after command completion on Windows
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes CLI processes (node.exe openclaw.mjs) remaining alive after command execution on Windows. The CLI entrypoint was missing process.exit() calls after commands completed, causing processes to hang.

## Changes

- Added explicit `process.exit(process.exitCode ?? 0)` call after CLI entrypoint execution in openclaw.mjs
- Ensures all async code paths properly terminate on Windows

## Testing

- Verified CLI commands now exit cleanly on Windows after completion
- Existing test suite passes

Fixes openclaw/openclaw#74378

target.title: feat(browser): add agent download actions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the agent-facing `browser` tool did not expose the managed browser download helpers even though the CLI/control API already supported `/download` and `/wait/download`.
- Why it matters: agents could inspect pages and snapshots but could not ask the managed browser to click a download ref and return the saved file path.
- What changed: add `download` and `waitfordownload` actions to the browser tool schema/routing, export the existing runtime helpers, document the actions, and add regression coverage for host and node proxy routing.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): existing-session download interception remains managed-only; this does not bypass the browser server's existing 501/unsupported behavior for profiles that cannot intercept downloads.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related #38519, #12378
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the browser extension already had managed download endpoints and client helpers, but the agent tool schema and routing never exposed `download` / `waitfordownload`. During implementation, the new action routing also needed to align outer transport timeouts with browser-side download waits instead of keeping the older fixed 20s HTTP/proxy timeout.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no agent-tool regression test asserting download action routing or transport timeout propagation.
- Contributing context (if known): prior PR #12378 attempted a similar action surface but did not land.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.downloads.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: agent `browser` actions route `download` and `waitfordownload` through the host client or node proxy, preserve request bodies, require explicit `ref`/`path` where needed, and give long download waits enough outer transport timeout.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the browser-side download implementation already exists; the risk here is the agent tool contract and proxy/client timeout handoff.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Agents can now call the built-in `browser` tool with `action="download"` to click a snapshot ref and save the resulting file, or `action="waitfordownload"` to wait for the next managed download. Results include the saved download path returned by the existing browser control API.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[agent browser tool] -> [snapshot/action helpers only] -> [no download action]

After:
[agent browser tool] -> [download/waitfordownload] -> [managed browser endpoint] -> [saved file path]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this exposes existing managed browser download endpoints through the agent tool. It keeps the existing browser server/profile capability checks, keeps existing-session download interception managed-only, and constrains saved paths to the browser download path handling already used by the control API.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.8.0-55-generic x86_64
- Runtime/container: Node v24.13.0, pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Browser plugin / built-in browser tool
- Relevant config (redacted): default managed browser action routing; node proxy tests use mocked browser-capable nodes

### Steps

1. Create the browser tool.
2. Execute `action="download"` with a snapshot `ref` and output `path`, or execute `action="waitfordownload"` with an optional output `path`.
3. For node targets, route the request through `browser.proxy` and preserve both body timeout and outer transport timeout.

### Expected

- The action reaches `/download` or `/wait/download` and returns the browser download payload, including the saved file path.
- Explicit and default download waits are not cut short by the outer host/proxy transport timeout.

### Actual

- Implemented behavior matches expected in unit/seam tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Verification run locally:

```text
pnpm test extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.downloads.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
# passed: 2 files, 56 tests

NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test:changed
# passed: 2 shards, 854 passed, 1 skipped

pnpm docs:check-mdx
# passed

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/tools/browser-control.md extensions/browser/plugin-registration.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-runtime.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.runtime.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.schema.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/constants.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.downloads.test.ts
# passed

git diff --check
# passed

NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=2048 node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig tsconfig.oxlint.extensions.json extensions/browser/plugin-registration.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-runtime.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.runtime.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.schema.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client-actions-core.downloads.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/constants.ts
# passed: 0 warnings, 0 errors
```

Note: `pnpm check:changed` passed conflict-marker checks, changelog attribution checks, extension production typecheck, and extension test typecheck, then local broad `lint:extensions` was terminated by `tsgolint` with SIGTERM on an 8 GB host. I used the targeted repo lint wrapper above to avoid exceeding the host memory limit.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: host-client download routing; host-client wait-for-download routing; node proxy download routing; node proxy wait-for-download routing; default 120s download wait transport timeout; explicit 30s download wait transport timeout; required `ref`/`path` validation; docs/format/lint/typecheck coverage listed above.
- Edge cases checked: omitted `timeoutMs` uses the browser-side default download wait plus transport slack; explicit `timeoutMs` scales both proxy and gateway waits; existing-session managed-only scope is documented and unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: a live Chromium download in a real browser session; this PR relies on the existing browser-side download implementation and adds the missing agent tool contract/routing coverage.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: exposing downloads through the agent browser tool could let agents save files unexpectedly.
  - Mitigation: the action uses existing managed browser endpoints and their path/profile constraints; unsupported existing-session profiles remain blocked by the browser server.
- Risk: long download waits could be cut short by outer transport timeouts.
  - Mitigation: host client and node proxy transport timeouts now scale from the effective download wait with slack, with tests for explicit and default waits.

target.title: fix: gate same-model retries to idempotent calls
target.body:
## Summary

This is a narrow ProjectClownfish replacement for the same-model transient retry behavior proposed in #44385. It preserves attribution to @nicolasgrasset and source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44385 while excluding unrelated outbound DeliverySerializer and Mattermost dependency changes.

## Scope

- Add a bounded finite retry budget for transient model fallback failures.
- Keep permanent errors moving directly to fallback.
- Preserve cooldown probe behavior and avoid same-provider probe retry delays.
- Avoid retrying stateful/non-idempotent callback paths such as memory flush when unsafe.
- Add focused model-fallback tests for timeout, rate_limit, overloaded/server errors, permanent errors, invalid retry counts, single-model configs, cooldown probes, and retry-unsafe callers.

## Validation

- `pnpm -s vitest run src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

Closes or supersedes the same-model retry family only after this replacement PR lands and post-merge closeout revalidates the covered issues.

ProjectClownfish replacement details:
- Cluster: ghcrawl-156725-autonomous-smoke
- Source PRs: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44385
- Credit: Credit @nicolasgrasset for the original implementation direction and source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44385.; Mention that the replacement intentionally carries forward only the same-model transient retry portion and leaves DeliverySerializer/Mattermost changes out of scope.
- Validation: pnpm -s vitest run src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts; pnpm check:changed

The PR adds default-off MCP Apps host support with `mcp.apps.enabled`, MCP UI resource-to-canvas rendering, app-facing Gateway `mcp.*` proxy methods, Control UI app bridge rendering, docs, tests, and a new UI dependency.

PR surface: Source +1131, Tests +1018, Docs +51, Config +1, Other +25. Total +2226 across 47 files.

Reproducibility: not applicable. as a current-main bug. The merge blockers are source-reproducible on the PR head: unbounded resource decode/persistence and missing spec-required CSP/sandbox-proxy handling are visible in the changed files.

target.title: add option for MCP apps support
target.body:
## Summary
 - Problem: OpenClaw calls MCP servers, but ignores MCP Apps UI metadata/resources
 - Why it matters: MCP Apps turns trusted MCP servers into richer tool providers with purpose-built UI. Without host support, OpenClaw misses that part of the MCP ecosystem even when the server is configured and callable.
- What changed: Added opt-in MCP Apps host support: parse UI metadata, hide app-only tools from the model, fetch MCP App resources into canvas documents, render them through the existing embed/canvas path with an app bridge, and proxy app-initiated MCP calls through the active session runtime.
- What changed: Added restart/reload hardening for restored app views, wider assistant canvas layout, docs, config schema support,tests, and a security-audit warning when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
-  What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal MCP tool execution still works without MCP Apps. MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled`; this does not add a marketplace, new MCP server examples, or plugin-only architecture.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- MCP app-only tools are not materialized for the model.
- MCP Apps UI payloads are only fetched/rendered when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
- App bridge calls are rejected when MCP Apps are disabled.
- App bridge calls enforce app-callable tool visibility.
- Persisted/restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
- Existing `[embed]` canvas rendering remains the fallback path when MCP Apps metadata is absent or disabled.

- Target test or file:
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations. 
- Scenario the test should lock in:
    - MCP Apps metadata is preserved from MCP tool catalogs through tool results and chat rendering.
    - App-only tools are hidden from the model.
    - Model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
    - MCP Apps UI rendering and bridge proxy calls require `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
    - Restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
    - Existing canvas/embed rendering remains the fallback when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
    - The feature crosses runtime materialization, gateway proxying, config/bootstrap, and UI rendering. Focused unit and seam tests cover those boundaries without requiring a live browser, real MCP server fleet, or container e2e in normal CI.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
    - Existing canvas/embed tests covered generic iframe rendering, but not MCP Apps metadata, app-only visibility, app bridge calls, or restored MCP runtime behavior.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations.
  
## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```mermaid
  flowchart LR
    A[MCP server tool] --> B[Tool result with UI metadata]
    B --> C[OpenClaw MCP runtime]
    C --> D[Fetch MCP App HTML resource]
    D --> E[Canvas document / existing embed path]
    E --> F[Control UI mcp-app-view iframe]
    F --> G[AppBridge]
    G --> H[Gateway mcp.* proxy]
    H --> C
  ```
## Security Impact (required)
  - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
  - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No for normal MCP transport; Yes in the sense that enabled MCP Apps can make app-initiated MCP calls back through the existing MCP session runtime.
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
    - Risks:
       - MCP servers with MCP Apps support can provide HTML/JS UI resources that OpenClaw renders in chat.
       -  rendered app views can call app-visible MCP tools and read MCP resources through the active session runtime.
       - app views receive the tool input/result payload they are rendering.
    - Mitigations:
        - MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
        - app views render in sandboxed iframes using the existing Control UI embed sandbox policy.
        - app-only tools are hidden from the model, and model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
        - app bridge calls are scoped to the active MCP session/server and exposed through narrow `mcp.*` gateway methods.
        - `openclaw security audit` warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
- Trust model: enabling MCP Apps means trusting the configured MCP servers not only as tool providers, but also as UI code providers.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment
  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local OpenClaw container via Podman
  - Model/provider: Claude Sonnet 4.6
  - Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat with configured MCP servers
  - Relevant config (redacted):

```json
  {
    "mcp": {
      "apps": {
        "enabled": true
      },
      "servers": {
        "system-monitor": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-system-monitor", "--stdio"]
        },
        "pdf": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-pdf", "--stdio"]
        },
        "qr": {
          "url": "http://127.0.0.1:3108/mcp",
          "transport": "streamable-http"
        }
      }
    }
  }
```

### Steps
  1. Build and run an OpenClaw image from this branch.
  2. Enable MCP Apps with openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json.
  3. Configure the system-monitor, pdf, and qr MCP servers.
  4. Start the QR MCP sidecar if using the QR example.
  5. Start a fresh chat/session in the Control UI.
  6. Prompt the agent to show live system info using the system-monitor MCP Apps UI.
  7. Prompt the agent to generate a QR code using the QR MCP Apps UI.
  8. Prompt the agent to display https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762 using the PDF MCP Apps UI.
  9. Restart the OpenClaw container and reload the Control UI.
  10. Confirm the persisted PDF app view reloads and reconnects.

### Expected

  - Normal MCP tools still work.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=true, MCP Apps UI resources render as interactive embedded app views.
  - System monitor renders a live monitor UI.
  - QR renders an embedded QR UI.
  - PDF renders an interactive PDF viewer.
  - Restored app views can reconnect after gateway/container restart.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=false, app bridge calls are rejected and MCP Apps render through the normal fallback path instead of the app bridge.

### Actual

  - Verified locally with system-monitor, QR, and PDF MCP Apps examples.
  - Verified upstream image comparison: upstream can call the MCP servers but does not render the MCP Apps UI resources.
  - Verified restart/reload behavior for the PDF app view.

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aaa93975-4406-493e-a060-4ac64763704b

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
    - No migration is required for existing installs. MCP Apps support is default-off.
    - To enable it, set:
      ```bash
      openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json
      ```
    - Configure MCP servers that expose MCP Apps / `mcp-ui` resources.
    - Restart OpenClaw so the updated config is loaded.

## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: Enabling MCP Apps allows configured MCP servers to provide HTML/JS UI that renders inside OpenClaw.
    - Mitigation: Feature is opt-in via `mcp.apps.enabled`; app UI renders in a sandboxed iframe; docs and security audit call out the trust boundary.
  - Risk: App views can call app-visible MCP tools/resources through the bridge, increasing the practical surface area of a trusted MCP server.
    - Mitigation: Bridge is scoped to MCP app methods only; model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls; app-only tools are hidden from the model.
  - Risk: Restored chat views may need to reconnect to MCP runtimes after a container restart.
    - Mitigation: Gateway proxy lazily recreates the session MCP runtime from config when an app bridge call arrives and no runtime is currently active.
  - Risk: Servers without MCP Apps support, or installs with the feature disabled, should not regress.
    - Mitigation: Existing MCP tool execution path remains unchanged; UI falls back to the existing embed/canvas rendering behavior when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.

target.title: Fix dashboard chat run recovery across reconnects
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes two closely related Gateway Dashboard chat reliability problems:

1. Dashboard chat could lose busy-state on reconnect/tab return and incorrectly fall back to `New session` / `Send` while the session still had an active run.
2. Gateway restarts could interrupt an in-flight chat run without leaving a visible assistant-side notice in the chat transcript.

## What changed

- include `activeChatRuns` in the gateway hello snapshot
- recover `chatRunId` / busy state from snapshot on reconnect instead of blindly clearing it
- keep the composer in busy mode while a run is still abortable
- stop forcing sidebar chat navigation back to `main`; prefer the last active chat session
- abort active chat runs during gateway shutdown and inject a durable assistant transcript notice
- process restart sentinel wake immediately on startup and inject a transcript notice for internal sessions without external delivery routes

## Validation

- `pnpm vitest run src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-restart-sentinel.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=6144 pnpm -s tsc --noEmit --pretty false`

## Related

- Closes #41064
- Related: #38836
- Related: #21641
- Related: #26986

target.title: Fix session transcript path canonicalization on save
target.body:
# OpenClaw session-store path fix, 2026-04-12

## Status
Applied live to installed runtime. Also ported carefully into a fresh source clone of the matching `v2026.4.11` upstream tag, but not yet merged upstream or installed from that source tree.

## Source clone
- Repo: `/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/workspace/external/openclaw-upstream`
- Branch: `fix/session-store-transcript-path-canonicalization`

## Protected file
`/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/store-Cw5RrVCw.js`

## Backup
`/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/backups/runtime-patches/store-Cw5RrVCw.js.pre-20260412-1233`

## What changed
Added a save-time canonicalization step so persisted `sessionFile` and `transcriptPath` values are rewritten into the real sessions directory before session-store writes hit disk.

## Why
Without this, restored/recovered session entries could keep workspace-style transcript paths, then later be treated as missing/orphaned and get swept or misread.

## Verification performed
- syntax check passed
- synthetic save test passed
- gateway restarted cleanly
- post-restart audit:
  - `entries 913`
  - `legacy_non_agent_keys 0`
  - `non_cron_duplicate_sessionids 0`
  - `missing_jsonl_paths 0`

## Release blocker rule
Treat this as a release blocker until the fix is confirmed upstream and in the installed version. Before any OpenClaw update or manual overwrite touching runtime/config/session storage, stop and verify this protection first.

## Source patch details
Changed files in the source clone:
- `src/config/sessions/store.ts`
- `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
- `src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts`

Validation in source clone:
- `node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts`
- Result: `2 passed`, `29 passed`

## Patch export
- Mailbox-style patch export written for handoff/cherry-pick use:
  - `/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/workspace/patches/openclaw-session-store-path-fix-2026-04-12.patch`

## Current git state
- Branch is clean and still anchored on tag base `v2026.4.11`
- Head commit:
  - `e1c0d1a` `Fix session transcript path canonicalization on save`

## Next upstream action
Open or stage an upstream PR from the source clone using commit `e1c0d1a` or the exported patch above. Keep the live runtime bandage in place until an official release is installed and re-verified on this machine.

target.title: fix(gateway): restore codex-cli chat history imports
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `chat.history` only backfilled external CLI transcript history for `claude-cli`, so Codex-backed sessions could look empty
after refresh when the local transcript was missing.
- Why it matters: users could lose visible history in Gateway/Control UI refresh flows even though the Codex CLI transcript still
existed on disk.
- What changed: added Codex CLI transcript parsing + import support, filtered commentary-only Codex history before merge, merged
multiple bound CLI transcripts when backfilling an empty local history, and blocked old CLI backfill after session reset until a new
CLI binding is established.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no changes to live chat send flow, provider execution, or non-CLI session history behavior.

AI-assisted. Testing: targeted tests + `pnpm check` passed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the CLI session history import path only supported `claude-cli`, and the import selection logic only chose one external
transcript source.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no Codex-specific history import coverage in `src/gateway/cli-session-history.test.ts`,
and no guard for reset semantics with preserved CLI bindings.
- Contributing context (if known): Codex transcripts include commentary/final-answer phases and can coexist with other CLI bindings
on the same OpenClaw session.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/gateway/cli-session-history.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.sessions.gateway-server-sessions-a.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/cli-session.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Codex-backed sessions backfill history from the Codex session store when the local transcript is empty.
  - Commentary-only Codex messages do not consume `chat.history` visibility budget before sanitization.
  - Empty local history can merge multiple bound CLI transcript sources.
  - Session reset preserves CLI bindings without re-importing old external CLI history into the fresh session.
  - Rebinding a CLI session clears the reset-time suppression flag.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
  - these tests cover the parser, merge policy, Gateway `chat.history` seam, and reset persistence without needing a full end-to-end
CLI run.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
  - `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts` already covered Claude CLI backfill.
- If no new test is added, why not:
  - N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Codex-backed Gateway chat history now restores prior turns from the Codex CLI session store on refresh when no local transcript is
available.
- Commentary-only Codex progress messages are not surfaced as visible history entries.
- Reset sessions no longer repopulate old external CLI history just because preserved CLI bindings still exist.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[refresh chat] -> [local transcript empty] -> [claude-cli only backfill] -> [codex history missing]

After:
[refresh chat] -> [local transcript empty] -> [merge visible bound CLI transcripts] -> [history restored]
[sessions.reset] -> [preserve CLI binding + suppress old import] -> [fresh empty session stays empty]
```


## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation:
    - N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: codex-cli
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway chat.history
- Relevant config (redacted): session entry with cliSessionBindings["codex-cli"].sessionId

### Steps

1. Create or reuse a session entry whose local transcript is empty, but whose cliSessionBindings points at an existing Codex CLI
   session.
2. Call chat.history for that session.
3. Reset the session and call chat.history again.

### Expected

- Before reset: Codex transcript turns are restored from the external Codex session store.
- After reset: the new session remains empty until new local or newly bound CLI history exists.

### Actual

- Before this fix: Codex-backed refreshes could show empty history because only Claude CLI backfill existed.
- Before this fix: if multiple CLI bindings existed, only one external transcript source was imported.
- During review hardening: reset needed an explicit suppression path so preserved old CLI bindings would not refill the new empty
  session.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
    - pnpm check
    - pnpm test src/agents/cli-session.test.ts
    - pnpm test src/gateway/cli-session-history.test.ts
    - pnpm test src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts -t "chat.history backfills claude-cli sessions from Claude
      project files"
    - pnpm test src/gateway/server.sessions.gateway-server-sessions-a.test.ts -t "sessions.reset preserves spawned session ownership
      metadata"
- Edge cases checked:
    - commentary and final answer sharing timestamps
    - Codex text elements and attachment import
    - Codex session-path cache isolation by home directory
    - missing cached Codex file
    - multiple CLI bindings on one session
    - reset suppression and suppression clearing on CLI rebind
- What you did not verify:
    - a fully green repo-wide pnpm test run; the full suite had unrelated existing failures outside this change set

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex-backed Gateway `chat.history` import now restores visible turns from current Codex rollout `response_item` message records when the OpenClaw local transcript is empty, while preserving commentary filtering, multi-CLI merge behavior, and reset-time import suppression.
- Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw repo checkout on commit `77de40575d1d76b3bb6ea1d5b860a4c977b7ecca`, Node `v25.8.2`, pnpm `11.1.0`, real source modules from `src/gateway/cli-session-history.ts`, `src/gateway/cli-session-history.codex.ts`, and `src/gateway/cli-session-history.claude.ts`, temporary on-disk `CODEX_HOME` plus Claude project transcript files.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: From the repo root, ran a `node --import tsx` terminal probe that created a temporary Codex JSONL rollout containing `response_item` records for user, assistant commentary, assistant final answer, reasoning, and developer messages; then called the production Codex resolver/parser and `augmentChatHistoryWithCliSessionImports` for Codex-only import, multi-binding merge, full reset suppression, and provider-scoped suppression.
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from the current-head probe:

```json
{
  "command": "node --import tsx response_item Codex/Claude CLI transcript import probe",
  "head": "77de40575d1d76b3bb6ea1d5b860a4c977b7ecca",
  "nodeVersion": "v25.8.2",
  "pnpmVersion": "11.1.0",

    {

    },

  ],

    }
  ],
  "mergedVisibleMessageCount": 4,
  "mergedVisibleMessages": [
    {
      "role": "user",
      "text": "show prior codex response_item history",
      "importedFrom": "codex-cli",
      "externalId": "response_item:2026-05-22T01:52:01.000Z:0"
    },
    {
      "role": "assistant",

      "importedFrom": "codex-cli",
      "externalId": "response_item:2026-05-22T01:52:04.000Z:2"
    },
    {
      "role": "user",
      "text": "also merge claude history",
      "importedFrom": "claude-cli",

    },
    {
      "role": "assistant",
      "provider": "claude-cli",
      "text": "claude history also restored",
      "importedFrom": "claude-cli",
      "externalId": "claude-assistant-1"
    }
  ],
  "resetSuppressedMessageCount": 0,
  "codexOnlySuppressedKeepsClaudeCount": 2,
  "codexOnlySuppressedKeepsClaudeMessages": [
    {
      "role": "user",
      "text": "also merge claude history",
      "importedFrom": "claude-cli",
      "externalId": "claude-user-1"
    },
    {

      "text": "claude history also restored",
      "importedFrom": "claude-cli",
      "externalId": "claude-assistant-1"

}
```

- Observed result after fix: The production resolver found the transcript under `CODEX_HOME`, the parser imported current Codex `response_item` user/assistant messages, ignored reasoning and developer records, filtered assistant commentary out of visible `chat.history`, merged Codex and Claude CLI transcripts for empty-history backfill, returned an empty history under full reset suppression, and allowed Claude history through when only Codex import was provider-suppressed.

- CI status at proof update: GitHub checks are rerunning for current head `77de40575d1d76b3bb6ea1d5b860a4c977b7ecca` after this push.
- What was not tested: Full browser/UI refresh and a live Codex CLI binary run were not exercised in this terminal probe; the proof targets the production filesystem transcript import, parser, merge, commentary filtering, multi-binding merge, provider-scoped suppression, and reset suppression path that backs Gateway `chat.history`.

target.title: perf(qmd): persistent export-state cache + stat fast path in exportSessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** For agents with thousands of session transcripts, `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` was the dominant gateway CPU consumer. Every boot and every QMD sync interval walked every session JSONL and called `buildSessionEntry()` per file, which performs full read + parse + redaction + hash/render work even when the transcript export was unchanged.
- **Why it matters:** The previous in-memory skip state only avoided some markdown rewrites after a manager instance had already rebuilt each entry. It did not survive gateway restart, so cold starts and periodic syncs could still create large CPU and GC spikes on production-sized transcript sets.
- **What changed:**
  1. Added a per-agent SQLite cache table, `qmd_session_export_cache`, in `openclaw-agent.sqlite`, keyed by `(session_file, export_dir, render_version)`.
  2. Added a stat + full-content-fingerprint fast path before `buildSessionEntry()`. When cached `size`, `mtimeMs`, `ino`, full content fingerprint, and cached markdown target all match, QMD skips the expensive full-session rebuild.
  3. Preserved the slow path for changed transcripts, missing markdown targets, mismatched cached targets, retention pruning, export-dir changes, and render-version changes.
  4. Kept raw per-agent DB/Kysely access inside the core state owner and exposed only QMD-specific cache helpers through a private local-only SDK subpath, `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal`, scoped to the bundled `memory-core` plugin.
  5. Batch-loaded the current export scope cache rows once per `exportSessions()` pass and reused an in-memory lookup map, so large transcript sets do not reopen/refresh the per-agent DB accessor once per session file.
  6. Excluded the generated private QMD SDK helper artifacts from npm package output: `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.js` and `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.d.ts`.

**Scope boundary:** `renderSessionMarkdown()`, session redaction, line wrapping, and the markdown format QMD consumes are unchanged. `buildSessionEntry()` remains the authoritative slow path. The cache is scoped by export directory and `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION`, so config moves and render-byte changes invalidate cleanly.

**Effect on Eddie's main agent (4,052 sessions / 158 MB):**
- Boot QMD sync: **312s -> single-digit seconds**
- Interval QMD sync: **38s -> ~50-500 ms**
- The every-5-min CPU spike + GC tail collapses

## Linked Issue

- Closes openclaw-ys0 (Beads tracking issue)

## Test Coverage

New and updated tests cover the cache contract:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
  - persists session-export state across manager restarts
  - rebuilds after same-size transcript rewrites, proving content changes are not hidden by matching stat fields
  - ignores cache rows scoped to a previous `exportDir`
  - rebuilds markdown when the cached target has been deleted
  - rebuilds the expected markdown when a current-scope cache row points at another target
  - preserves existing session-export behavior for checkpoint/session filtering
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.test.ts`
  - schema shape includes the new derived-state cache table
  - per-agent DB schema metadata and `user_version` remain at version 1
  - newer-schema refusal still protects downgrade safety
- `test/release-check.test.ts`
  - package output rejects private local-only plugin SDK artifacts
  - package `files` exclusions include every private local-only SDK artifact, including the new QMD cache helper

## Tradeoffs / Known Caveats

- The fast path still uses stat identity to avoid hashing obviously changed files, but it verifies a full SHA-1 content fingerprint before skipping the rebuild. Same-size rewrites with preserved or seeded stat identity do not suppress updated markdown.
- `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` must be bumped when changing `renderSessionMarkdown()`, redaction, line wrapping, or any other logic that changes the bytes written to `qmd/sessions/*.md`.
- The cache table is derived state; the per-agent DB `user_version` remains at 1 for rollback safety.
- No hand-written `CHANGELOG.md` entry is included; current repo policy keeps release notes out of normal PRs and derives them during release generation.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed:** QMD session export persists its unchanged-transcript fast-path state in the per-agent SQLite DB without exposing raw per-agent DB/Kysely access through the public plugin SDK. It must still rebuild markdown when a cached target is missing, when the cached target points elsewhere, and when stat identity matches but the transcript content fingerprint changed. The private QMD SDK artifact must also stay out of package output.
- **Real environment tested:** Current PR head `eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30` in the `perf/qmd-export-stat-fast-path` worktree, after merging current `openclaw/main` and cleaning unrelated formatter-only merge churn, using the real `QmdMemoryManager`, real `resolveMemoryBackendConfig`, the private local-only QMD cache subpath, real per-agent `openclaw-agent.sqlite` storage, and local `qmd 2.1.0` on PATH. The proof ran against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`; no Vitest harness or mocked QMD export cache was used.
- **Exact steps run after this patch:** `node --import tsx --input-type=module` from the topic worktree with an inline driver. The driver created one session JSONL, ran `QmdMemoryManager.sync({ reason: "manual" })` across fresh manager instances, inspected and seeded `qmd_session_export_cache` through the private QMD helper subpath, rewrote the transcript with a deliberately stale fingerprint row whose size/mtime/inode matched the source, deleted the generated markdown target, and verified the next sync rebuilt it.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the live run:

```text
current head: eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30
qmd version: qmd 2.1.0
=== Phase 1: first sync writes markdown and SQLite cache ===
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=899
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=377
sync 1: sync done in 380 ms
cache target is export file: true
fingerprint length: 40
=== Phase 2: unchanged sync uses fast path ===
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=828
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=299
sync 2: sync done in 301 ms
export mtime unchanged: true
export size unchanged: true
=== Phase 3: matching stat row with stale content fingerprint rebuilds ===
seeded cache size matches source: true
seeded cache mtime matches source: true
seeded cache ino matches source: true
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=813
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=306
sync 3: sync done in 308 ms
export mtime newer: true
cache fingerprint changed: true
cache fingerprint length after rebuild: 40
=== Phase 4: delete markdown while SQLite cache survives ===
export exists after delete: false
sqlite cache row still present: true
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=818
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=307
sync 4: sync done in 309 ms
rebuilt export exists: true
cache target restored to export file: true

All runtime assertions passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The first sync wrote markdown and a SQLite cache row with the expected derived target and 40-character fingerprint. The second sync preserved markdown mtime/size, proving unchanged input took the persisted fast path. The third sync rebuilt after a same-size rewrite even though the seeded cache row matched the source stat fields, proving the full-content fingerprint prevents stale skips. After deleting markdown while leaving SQLite cache intact, the fourth sync regenerated the target and restored the cache target to the expected export file.
- **What was not tested:** I did not rerun a benchmark against Eddie's full 4,052-session production corpus after the SDK-boundary rewrite, base-branch merge, package exclusion fix, cached-target validation fix, and batch-read follow-up. The proof above validates the current SQLite cache behavior and regression paths on real runtime code; the original production-sized timing numbers remain observational context.

## Verification

Current head `eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30` includes:

- `3468c322a6` (`fix(qmd): exclude private cache SDK artifact from package`)
- signed base-refresh merges from current `openclaw/main` to clear the PR's dirty/conflicting merge state
- cleanup restoring unrelated Slack/Telegram/installer/test formatter churn back to current `openclaw/main`, leaving exactly the QMD/cache/SDK/package diff

Commands run after the package exclusion fix and/or after the base merge:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/release-check.test.ts
# 56 passed

node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs
# OK: all referenced openclaw/plugin-sdk/<subpath> imports are exported

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=src-outside-plugin-sdk
# no violations; baseline matches

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=plugin-sdk-internal
# no violations; baseline matches

pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
# OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check
# all SDK entrypoints: 339; public package SDK entrypoints: 321; local-only entrypoints: 18; package-exported forbidden subpaths: 0

pnpm exec oxfmt --check package.json docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.ts src/plugins/sdk-alias.ts scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-private-local-only-subpaths.json
# clean

pnpm tsgo:core
# exited 0

pnpm tsgo:core:test
# exited 0

git diff --check
# clean

node --import tsx --input-type=module
# real-runtime QMD/SQLite proof above; All runtime assertions passed.
```

target.title: fix(lmstudio): bound model load success response body to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`ensureLmstudioModelLoaded` in `extensions/lmstudio/src/models.fetch.ts` POSTs to LM Studio's `/api/v1/models/load` and reads the **success** response with an unbounded `await response.json()`. LM Studio is a self-hosted/local server endpoint that the agent reaches over HTTP; a misbehaving, misconfigured, or compromised server (or anything sitting on the configured `baseUrl`, including an SSRF-redirected target) can return a success body with no `Content-Length` and stream an arbitrarily large JSON payload.

Because `response.json()` buffers the entire body before parsing, that unbounded body is fully read into memory on the provider/discovery path before any size check runs. That is a memory-pressure / hang vector on a code path that runs during model discovery and before first inference ŌĆö exactly the kind of unbounded external-response read the response-limit campaign has been closing across the codebase.

The sibling **error** body on the same endpoint was already bounded (`readResponseTextLimited`, 8 KiB) in a prior change. This PR closes the remaining unbounded read: the **success** JSON body.

## Changes

- `extensions/lmstudio/src/models.fetch.ts`: replace the unbounded `await response.json()` on the `/api/v1/models/load` success path with the shared, already-bounded `readProviderJsonResponse<LmstudioLoadResponse>(...)` helper (re-exported from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`, same helper the `/api/v1/models` discovery path already uses). It reads the body through `readResponseWithLimit` under the 16 MiB provider-JSON cap, cancels the stream on overflow, and wraps malformed JSON with the caller's label. No new abstraction is introduced and the redundant local `try/catch` is removed.
- `extensions/lmstudio/src/models.test.ts`: migrate the model fetch/load mocks from bare `{ ok, json }` placeholders to real `Response` objects (the bounded readers require a real body stream), and add a regression test that streams an oversized (>16 MiB) success body and asserts the load path throws a bounded error and cancels the stream instead of buffering it all. Updates the malformed-load assertion to the helper's owned message.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: unbounded buffering of the LM Studio `/api/v1/models/load` success JSON body; after the fix the read is capped at 16 MiB and the stream is cancelled on overflow.
- **Real environment tested**: a local `node:http` server (real sockets, chunked transfer-encoding, no `Content-Length`) driving the real exported `ensureLmstudioModelLoaded` / `readProviderJsonResponse` via the global `fetch` (real `Response` body streams). Run with `node --import tsx proof.mts`.
- **Exact steps**: (1) normal small `{ "status": "loaded" }` response, end-to-end `ensureLmstudioModelLoaded`; (2) oversized 20 MiB chunked success body, end-to-end; (3) a body that hangs forever after a prefix, driving `readProviderJsonResponse` with an explicit small cap; (4) negative control reading the same 20 MiB body with the pre-fix unbounded `await response.text()`.
- **Evidence after fix**: (1) returns `"proof-model"` (still parses normally); (2) throws `LM Studio model load: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and the server observed only ~17.5 MiB sent before the socket closed (vs 20 MiB total) ŌĆö the stream was cancelled mid-flight; (3) throws `... exceeds 2048 bytes` and the server socket was closed (cancelled) instead of hanging; (4) the unbounded read consumed the full 20 MiB, confirming the cap is load-bearing.
- **Observed result**: oversized/hanging bodies are stopped at the cap with a bounded error and the upstream stream is cancelled; well-formed small bodies still parse.
- **What was not tested**: a real third-party LM Studio binary returning a pathological body (used a faithful local HTTP server instead); behavior under genuine OS memory exhaustion (the goal is to prevent reaching it).

## Evidence

```
[proof] µ£¼Õ£░ server ÕÉ»ÕŖ© http://127.0.0.1:36821
PASS  normal-small ń½»Õł░ń½»Ķ¦Żµ×ÉõĖŹńĀ┤ ŌĆö ensureLmstudioModelLoaded Ķ┐öÕø× proof-model
PASS  oversized µŖø bounded error ŌĆö error="LM Studio model load: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS  oversized server ÕÅæķĆüķćÅ < Õģ©ķćÅ 20MiB(stream Ķó½µÅÉÕēŹ cancel) ŌĆö server Õ«×ÕÅæ 17.50MiB (cap 16MiB,Ķ┐£Õ░Åõ║Ä 20MiB Õģ©ķćÅ)
PASS  hang body Õł░ cap ÕŹ│µŖø bounded error(õĖŹµŚĀķÖÉµīéĶĄĘ) ŌĆö error="LM Studio model load: JSON response exceeds 2048 bytes"
PASS  hang body cap ÕæĮõĖŁÕÉÄ server ń½» socket Ķó½ cancel/close ŌĆö server Õ«×ÕÅæ 720922 bytes ÕÉÄ socket close=true
PASS  negative control:µŚĀńĢī await .text() Õģ©ķćÅĶ»╗Õģź 20MiB(Ķ»üµśÄ cap load-bearing) ŌĆö µŚĀńĢīĶ»╗Õł░ 20.00MiB µ¢ćµ£¼ / server Õ«×ÕÅæ 20.00MiB(Õ»╣ńģ¦:bounded õ╗ģĶ»╗Õł░ cap)
[proof] ALL PASS
```

Unit tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/lmstudio/src/models.test.ts --run` ŌåÆ 24 passed (24), including the new `bounds oversized model load success bodies`. `oxlint` and `tsgo` (extensions source + test) clean on the changed files.

**Label**: security

---

This is the symmetric companion to the response-limit campaign (e.g. #95103 / #95108 / #95218): the LM Studio discovery `/api/v1/models` path and the `/api/v1/models/load` error body were already bounded; this PR finishes the set by bounding the `/api/v1/models/load` success body with the same shared helper and cap.

target.title: fix(security): close active April 4 transport and auth gaps
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: config-time explicit provider proxies accepted blocked internal hosts, `config.set` auth rotation left shared-auth sessions alive, and Discord outbound text could resolve `@everyone` / mention tokens from model output.
- Why it matters: these gaps leave room for SSRF via operator config, stale access after token rotation, and prompt-injected Discord-wide pings.
- What changed: added config-time proxy URL validation for explicit provider proxies, disconnected shared-auth clients on `config.set` auth changes, neutralized outbound Discord mentions across webhook/bot/payload sends, and added a device-pairing regression test for empty-scope node refresh filtering.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no repo-wide auth model changes, no new proxy opt-ins, no Android/mobile pairing work, no docs changes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #60387
- Related #59848
- Related #60208
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: security-sensitive paths trusted operator/config surfaces inconsistently, so config-time proxy overrides skipped the existing SSRF classifier, `config.set` skipped the shared-auth disconnect path used by patch/apply, and Discord mention neutralization only covered approval payloads.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no focused regression coverage for config-time proxy validation, `config.set` auth eviction, or non-approval Discord outbound mention sanitization.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): report items came from #59848, #60387, and the April 4 security triage sweep; device-scope regression guard locks in the already-fixed empty-scope pairing path from #60208.
- Why this regressed now: transport/auth seams were hardened incrementally at runtime but not uniformly at adjacent config/send entry points.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
