target.title: perf(qmd): persistent export-state cache + stat fast path in exportSessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** For agents with thousands of session transcripts, `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` was the dominant gateway CPU consumer. Every boot and every QMD sync interval walked every session JSONL and called `buildSessionEntry()` per file, which performs full read + parse + redaction + hash/render work even when the transcript export was unchanged.
- **Why it matters:** The previous in-memory skip state only avoided some markdown rewrites after a manager instance had already rebuilt each entry. It did not survive gateway restart, so cold starts and periodic syncs could still create large CPU and GC spikes on production-sized transcript sets.
- **What changed:**
  1. Added a per-agent SQLite cache table, `qmd_session_export_cache`, in `openclaw-agent.sqlite`, keyed by `(session_file, export_dir, render_version)`.
  2. Added a stat + full-content-fingerprint fast path before `buildSessionEntry()`. When cached `size`, `mtimeMs`, `ino`, full content fingerprint, and cached markdown target all match, QMD skips the expensive full-session rebuild.
  3. Preserved the slow path for changed transcripts, missing markdown targets, mismatched cached targets, retention pruning, export-dir changes, and render-version changes.
  4. Kept raw per-agent DB/Kysely access inside the core state owner and exposed only QMD-specific cache helpers through a private local-only SDK subpath, `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal`, scoped to the bundled `memory-core` plugin.
  5. Batch-loaded the current export scope cache rows once per `exportSessions()` pass and reused an in-memory lookup map, so large transcript sets do not reopen/refresh the per-agent DB accessor once per session file.
  6. Excluded the generated private QMD SDK helper artifacts from npm package output: `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.js` and `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.d.ts`.

**Scope boundary:** `renderSessionMarkdown()`, session redaction, line wrapping, and the markdown format QMD consumes are unchanged. `buildSessionEntry()` remains the authoritative slow path. The cache is scoped by export directory and `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION`, so config moves and render-byte changes invalidate cleanly.

**Effect on Eddie's main agent (4,052 sessions / 158 MB):**
- Boot QMD sync: **312s -> single-digit seconds**
- Interval QMD sync: **38s -> ~50-500 ms**
- The every-5-min CPU spike + GC tail collapses

## Linked Issue

- Closes openclaw-ys0 (Beads tracking issue)

## Test Coverage

New and updated tests cover the cache contract:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
  - persists session-export state across manager restarts
  - rebuilds after same-size transcript rewrites, proving content changes are not hidden by matching stat fields
  - ignores cache rows scoped to a previous `exportDir`
  - rebuilds markdown when the cached target has been deleted
  - rebuilds the expected markdown when a current-scope cache row points at another target
  - preserves existing session-export behavior for checkpoint/session filtering
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.test.ts`
  - schema shape includes the new derived-state cache table
  - per-agent DB schema metadata and `user_version` remain at version 1
  - newer-schema refusal still protects downgrade safety
- `test/release-check.test.ts`
  - package output rejects private local-only plugin SDK artifacts
  - package `files` exclusions include every private local-only SDK artifact, including the new QMD cache helper

## Tradeoffs / Known Caveats

- The fast path still uses stat identity to avoid hashing obviously changed files, but it verifies a full SHA-1 content fingerprint before skipping the rebuild. Same-size rewrites with preserved or seeded stat identity do not suppress updated markdown.
- `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` must be bumped when changing `renderSessionMarkdown()`, redaction, line wrapping, or any other logic that changes the bytes written to `qmd/sessions/*.md`.
- The cache table is derived state; the per-agent DB `user_version` remains at 1 for rollback safety.
- No hand-written `CHANGELOG.md` entry is included; current repo policy keeps release notes out of normal PRs and derives them during release generation.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed:** QMD session export persists its unchanged-transcript fast-path state in the per-agent SQLite DB without exposing raw per-agent DB/Kysely access through the public plugin SDK. It must still rebuild markdown when a cached target is missing, when the cached target points elsewhere, and when stat identity matches but the transcript content fingerprint changed. The private QMD SDK artifact must also stay out of package output.
- **Real environment tested:** Current PR head `eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30` in the `perf/qmd-export-stat-fast-path` worktree, after merging current `openclaw/main` and cleaning unrelated formatter-only merge churn, using the real `QmdMemoryManager`, real `resolveMemoryBackendConfig`, the private local-only QMD cache subpath, real per-agent `openclaw-agent.sqlite` storage, and local `qmd 2.1.0` on PATH. The proof ran against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`; no Vitest harness or mocked QMD export cache was used.
- **Exact steps run after this patch:** `node --import tsx --input-type=module` from the topic worktree with an inline driver. The driver created one session JSONL, ran `QmdMemoryManager.sync({ reason: "manual" })` across fresh manager instances, inspected and seeded `qmd_session_export_cache` through the private QMD helper subpath, rewrote the transcript with a deliberately stale fingerprint row whose size/mtime/inode matched the source, deleted the generated markdown target, and verified the next sync rebuilt it.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the live run:

```text
current head: eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30
qmd version: qmd 2.1.0
=== Phase 1: first sync writes markdown and SQLite cache ===
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=899
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=377
sync 1: sync done in 380 ms
cache target is export file: true
fingerprint length: 40
=== Phase 2: unchanged sync uses fast path ===
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=828
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=299
sync 2: sync done in 301 ms
export mtime unchanged: true
export size unchanged: true
=== Phase 3: matching stat row with stale content fingerprint rebuilds ===
seeded cache size matches source: true
seeded cache mtime matches source: true
seeded cache ino matches source: true
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=813
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=306
sync 3: sync done in 308 ms
export mtime newer: true
cache fingerprint changed: true
cache fingerprint length after rebuild: 40
=== Phase 4: delete markdown while SQLite cache survives ===
export exists after delete: false
sqlite cache row still present: true
[memory] qmd manager initialized for agent "main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=818
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=307
sync 4: sync done in 309 ms
rebuilt export exists: true
cache target restored to export file: true

All runtime assertions passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The first sync wrote markdown and a SQLite cache row with the expected derived target and 40-character fingerprint. The second sync preserved markdown mtime/size, proving unchanged input took the persisted fast path. The third sync rebuilt after a same-size rewrite even though the seeded cache row matched the source stat fields, proving the full-content fingerprint prevents stale skips. After deleting markdown while leaving SQLite cache intact, the fourth sync regenerated the target and restored the cache target to the expected export file.
- **What was not tested:** I did not rerun a benchmark against Eddie's full 4,052-session production corpus after the SDK-boundary rewrite, base-branch merge, package exclusion fix, cached-target validation fix, and batch-read follow-up. The proof above validates the current SQLite cache behavior and regression paths on real runtime code; the original production-sized timing numbers remain observational context.

## Verification

Current head `eb71a680cc4339778c93130d923833f98a238b30` includes:

- `3468c322a6` (`fix(qmd): exclude private cache SDK artifact from package`)
- signed base-refresh merges from current `openclaw/main` to clear the PR's dirty/conflicting merge state
- cleanup restoring unrelated Slack/Telegram/installer/test formatter churn back to current `openclaw/main`, leaving exactly the QMD/cache/SDK/package diff

Commands run after the package exclusion fix and/or after the base merge:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/release-check.test.ts
# 56 passed

node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs
# OK: all referenced openclaw/plugin-sdk/<subpath> imports are exported

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=src-outside-plugin-sdk
# no violations; baseline matches

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=plugin-sdk-internal
# no violations; baseline matches

# OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

# all SDK entrypoints: 339; public package SDK entrypoints: 321; local-only entrypoints: 18; package-exported forbidden subpaths: 0

pnpm exec oxfmt --check package.json docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.ts src/plugins/sdk-alias.ts scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-private-local-only-subpaths.json
# clean

# exited 0
```

target.title: fix(sandbox): materialize protected skill mount roots
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #94425.

A first-launch rw sandbox can currently skip the read-only overlays for `/workspace/skills` and `/workspace/.agents/skills` when the corresponding host directories do not exist yet. Because `/workspace` itself is writable, the sandbox can create those paths before the next container recreation applies the intended read-only mount.

## Why This Change Was Made

This materializes the protected skill mount roots during rw sandbox workspace setup, before Docker/browser/fs-bridge mount resolution runs. The mount-source predicate remains validation-only: it still requires an existing directory and the same canonical-root containment check, so missing paths under symlinked parents are not accepted as protected mounts.

This differs from the earlier candidate in #94820 by creating missing roots before mount resolution while preserving the existing canonical containment validation after creation.

## User Impact

New rw sandbox launches get read-only skill overlays on the first container creation, even for fresh workspaces that did not already have `skills` or `.agents/skills` directories. Existing symlink and non-directory rejection behavior stays intact.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.test.ts`
  - 15 tests passed, including missing-root materialization and symlink-parent regression coverage.
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
  - Passed. Local environment warns that Node is `v22.18.0` while the repo requests `>=22.19.0`.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/sandbox/context.ts src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.ts src/agents/sandbox/workspace-mounts.test.ts`
  - Passed.
- `pnpm exec tsx -e '<helper proof script>'`
  - Confirmed the helper creates all three protected roots and `resolveReadOnlyWorkspaceSkillMounts` returns mounts for `/workspace/skills`, `/workspace/.agents/skills`, and `/workspace/.openclaw/sandbox-skills/skills`.

Proof gap: I attempted local Docker proof, but Docker could not connect because Colima was stopped, and `colima start` failed while starting the VM. I could not produce first-launch container write-failure proof from this machine.

target.title: feat(ui): inline rename in the in-chat session picker
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The in-chat session picker only lets users *switch* sessions. Renaming still requires the admin "Sessions" tab, which is cumbersome when juggling several parallel, topic-specific chats ŌĆö exactly the friction reported in #54397.
- **Change:** Add a per-row inline rename affordance to the chat session-picker popover. An edit button on each row opens an inline input seeded with the current label; Enter / Save commits, Escape / Cancel discards.
- **Reuse, no new write path:** Commit goes through the existing self-refreshing `patchSession(state, key, {label})` flow (the same `sessions.patch` + `loadSessions()` path the admin Sessions tab already uses). Empty/whitespace input clears the label via `normalizeOptionalString`, matching admin-tab semantics exactly.
- **Out of scope (intentional):** chat slash-commands for naming (`/name`, `/new --name`, `/sessions`), switching sessions from text channels, and any LLM auto-suggest. Those are planned as separate, smaller PRs.
- **Success:** Users can rename a session without leaving the chat; behavior and validation are identical to the existing admin rename.

## Linked context

Related #54397, #85502

This is the small "header/sidebar shortcut" that maintainers explicitly invited as a separate polish request when closing #85502 (the admin-tab rename shipped there). It also advances the chat-native session-management UX discussed in #54397. I deliberately did not put `Closes #` because neither issue is a 1:1 match ŌĆö happy to add one if a maintainer prefers.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** Rename a session inline from the in-chat session picker (open picker ŌåÆ edit icon on a row ŌåÆ type a new label ŌåÆ Enter/Save), including clear-on-empty and Cancel/Escape.
- **Real environment tested:** A live self-hosted OpenClaw gateway (v2026.5.27, Node v22, on WSL2/Ubuntu-24.04) serving this PR branch's built `control-ui`, driven through a real Chromium browser via Playwright. The browser authenticates against the running gateway over the actual WebSocket control channel and exercises the production render path end to end. Endpoint, tokens, and other session labels are redacted.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  - Built this branch's `control-ui` and pointed the running gateway at it; confirmed it was live with `curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:<port>/healthz` ŌåÆ `200`.
  - Drove the real UI with a Playwright script (`node rename_e2e.js`, headless Chromium): connect to the gateway ŌåÆ open the in-chat session picker ŌåÆ click a row's inline edit icon ŌåÆ type a new label ŌåÆ press Enter to commit ŌåÆ re-read the session list to confirm persistence ŌåÆ restore the original label.
- **Evidence after fix (browser console + control-channel capture):**

  ```
  $ curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:<port>/healthz   ->   200
  $ node rename_e2e.js   (headless Chromium driving the live gateway)
  [control-ui] rpc method=connect           ok=true   auth=password
  [control-ui] rpc method=sessions.subscribe ok=true
  [control-ui] rpc method=sessions.list     ok=true
  picker opened -> inline edit icon present on each session row
  entered inline edit mode -> typed a new label -> pressed Enter
  [control-ui] rpc method=sessions.patch    ok=true   durationMs=69
  [control-ui] rpc method=sessions.list     ok=true   (self-refresh)
  verified: the new label is rendered in the picker row AND in the
           left sidebar "Recent" list, with no page reload
  cleanup: original label restored -> sessions.patch ok=true
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** Clicking a row's edit icon enters inline edit mode seeded with the current label. Pressing Enter commits through the existing `sessions.patch` flow (`ok=true`) and the self-refreshing `sessions.list` immediately shows the new name in both the picker row and the sidebar "Recent" list ŌĆö no page reload and without opening the admin Sessions tab. Cancel/Escape and closing the picker mid-edit discard the draft and leave the label unchanged. The new name persisted across a fresh `sessions.list` read; the original label was then restored.
- **What was not tested:** Non-web channels (this is a Control UI surface only), and exact visual pixel styling across physical devices/browsers other than headless Chromium. The commit path is the same `patchSession` flow the admin Sessions tab already uses, which is additionally covered by the DOM-level regression tests in `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`.

## Tests and validation

- Added 4 focused tests in `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts` under "chat session controls": commit via Enter, clear-on-whitespace via Save, cancel via Escape, and discard-on-close (regression guard so closing the picker mid-edit cannot leak a stale rename/draft).
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui`, `pnpm ui:i18n:check`, oxlint and oxfmt all green on the changes.

## Risk checklist

- **User-visible behavior change?** Yes ŌĆö a new rename control in the chat session picker.
- **Config/env/migration change?** No.
- **Security/auth/secrets/network/tool-exec change?** No new write path or permission surface; reuses the existing `sessions.patch` flow already used by the admin rename.
- **Highest-risk area:** ARIA structure ŌĆö rows are now wrapped in a `<div>` to host the rename button. Mitigation: wrappers are marked `role="presentation"` so options remain owned by the `listbox`. Note: this picker was already a loose listbox (button options, no roving tabindex); a deeper `list`/`listitem` refactor is intentionally left out of scope.
- **Mitigation:** Label normalization/clear semantics are delegated to the same helper as the admin tab; commit uses the existing self-refreshing controller; rename state is cleared on every picker-close path (covered by a test).

## Current review state

- **Next action:** Maintainer review of the UX placement and the ARIA `role="presentation"` approach.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** N/A (initial submission).

PR surface: Source +14, Tests +54. Total +68 across 2 files.

target.title: fix(tavily): bound untrusted JSON response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/tavily/src/tavily-client.ts` reads the `/search` and `/extract` responses with an unbounded `await response.json()` inside `readTavilyJsonResponse`. Those responses are **untrusted external web content** (the whole point of the Tavily plugin is to fetch arbitrary web data), and Tavily / a reverse proxy / a hostile-or-buggy upstream can send a body with **no `Content-Length`**.

With the unbounded read, `response.json()` keeps consuming the stream until it completes, so a single oversized or slow/hung JSON body forces the runtime to buffer the *entire* payload into memory before parsing. On the web-search/extract path this is memory pressure (and, for a body that streams indefinitely without ever closing, a hang) driven entirely by the untrusted remote ŌĆö a classic unbounded-external-input issue.

This is the symmetric counterpart to the response-limit campaign that already bounded the core provider read paths: #95103 (gateway pricing catalog streams), #95108 (Anthropic error streams), and #95218 (`readProviderJsonResponse` for provider JSON). The Tavily plugin was on the same unbounded `response.json()` shape and was missed.

## Changes

- `readTavilyJsonResponse` now reads through the shared bounded reader `readResponseWithLimit` (from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime`, backed by `@openclaw/media-core`), which **cancels the stream on overflow**, then `JSON.parse`s the decoded prefix.
- Cap is `16 * 1024 * 1024` (16 MiB), aligned with the existing provider JSON ceiling in `src/agents` (`readProviderJsonResponse`).
- Overflow throws a stable `"<label>: JSON response exceeds <n> bytes"` error; malformed JSON keeps its existing `"<label>: malformed JSON response"` error.
- No new abstraction ŌĆö reuses the existing helper, mirroring `readProviderJsonResponse`.
- Adds regression tests: small valid JSON still parses unchanged under the cap, and an oversized streaming body (20 ├Ś 1 KiB behind a 2 KiB cap) rejects with the bounded error while the reader stops early (`getReadCount() < 20`).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: an untrusted Tavily response with no `Content-Length` that streams past the cap must be cancelled and rejected with a bounded error, instead of buffering the whole body; small valid responses must still parse.
- **Real environment tested**: a local `node:http` server (`node --import tsx`) streaming a JSON body in 1 MiB chunks with **no `Content-Length`**, driving the **real exported** `__testing.readTavilyJsonResponse` over a real `fetch()` `Response` (real `ReadableStream` body, not a mock).
- **Exact steps**:
  1. Start a `node:http` server with a `/oversized` route that opens `{"results":[` and keeps `res.write()`-ing 1 MiB string chunks (tracking total bytes pushed), and a `/small` route returning a normal JSON object.
  2. `fetch()` `/oversized`, pass the live `Response` to `readTavilyJsonResponse(res, "Tavily Search")`, time it and capture the thrown error and the server-side bytes pushed before the socket closed.
  3. **Negative control**: `fetch()` `/oversized` again and read it with the *old* path inlined verbatim ŌĆö `await r.json()` with no cap ŌĆö racing against a 1.5 s timer, and record how many bytes the server pushed.
  4. `fetch()` `/small` and parse it through the same real function.
- **Evidence after fix**:
  - `[oversized] content-length header: null` ŌåÆ `threw: Tavily Search: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes (after ~200ms)`; server pushed ~18ŌĆō21 MiB before the cancel stopped it (the 16 MiB cap plus socket buffering), far below the 64 MiB safety ceiling the fixture would otherwise reach.
  - `[negative-control] unbounded json() ... server pushed 68157375 bytes` ŌåÆ with no cap the read consumed the **full ~64 MiB** until the fixture self-terminated; the cap is therefore load-bearing.
  - `[small] PASS: small valid JSON parses unchanged under the cap`.
  - `vitest extensions/tavily/src/tavily-client.test.ts --run` ŌåÆ **6 passed** (4 existing + 2 new boundary tests).
- **Observed result**: oversized stream is cancelled at the cap and rejected with the bounded error; the unbounded control buffers ~64 MiB (proving the fix is what stops it); small JSON is unaffected.
- **What was not tested**: live calls against the real Tavily API (would need a paid key and cannot be made to stream an oversized body deterministically); the 16 MiB ceiling itself is asserted at a scaled-down 2 KiB cap in the unit test for speed.

## Evidence

```
[oversized] content-length header: null
[oversized] threw: Tavily Search: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes (after 274ms)
[oversized] server bytes pushed before cancel: 20971500
[oversized] PASS: stream cancelled well below the 64 MiB ceiling
[negative-control] unbounded json() after 964ms => threw:Unexpected token ']' ...; server pushed 68157375 bytes
[negative-control] PASS: unbounded await response.json() buffers past the 16 MiB cap (cap is load-bearing)
[small] parsed: {"results":[{"title":"ok","url":"https://e.com"}],"answer":"hi"}
[small] PASS: small valid JSON parses unchanged under the cap

PROOF PASSED: bounded read cancels oversized stream; cap is load-bearing; small JSON intact
```

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/tavily/src/tavily-client.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed (6)
```

`oxlint` clean on the changed files; `tsgo -p extensions/tavily/tsconfig.json` type-checks clean.

**Label**: security

---

_AI-assisted: implementation and proof harness were written with AI assistance; all changes were reviewed and the proof was run locally by the author._

_This is the symmetric counterpart of the #95103 / #95108 / #95218 response-limit campaign, extending the same bounded-read treatment to the Tavily plugin's untrusted web-search/extract path._

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate it as a string array, trim entries, drop blank/whitespace-only entries, preserve explicit `[]`, and reject unsupported ACP paths instead of silently ignoring the policy.
- Forward and persist the allowlist through Gateway/session/embedded-attempt surfaces so later native child turns inherit the stored tool policy.
- Normalize Gateway `agent.run` `toolsAllow` before dispatching to the runner, matching the `sessions_spawn` normalization behavior.
- Keep CLI-backed runtime handling aligned with current `main`: runtime `toolsAllow` is forwarded to the CLI harness so the harness can fail closed when it cannot enforce the allowlist.

## Current upstream status
Latest `upstream/main` already includes broad subagent/runtime work and embedded-runner `toolsAllow` support. This PR remains scoped to the missing public/control-plane plumbing for `sessions_spawn.toolsAllow`, plus Gateway normalization for raw `agent.run` inputs.

## Change Type
- [x] Feature

## Scope
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native subagent tools with `toolsAllow`; the policy is applied to the spawned child embedded runtime so the child receives only allowed tool schemas. Gateway `agent.run` also normalizes direct `toolsAllow` input before runner dispatch.

Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw source checkout at current PR head `230b54baa3175fafeb7160922f510b489a6d1887`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, local Gateway on loopback token auth with `gateway.mode=local`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json` through Gateway (not `--local`), and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server that records every `/v1/responses` request tool schema.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started a local mock OpenAI Responses server from `/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/mock-openai-toolsallow.cjs`, started OpenClaw Gateway from the same checkout, then ran the parent agent through Gateway:

```text
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=<loopback-proof-token> OPENAI_API_KEY=mock node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json --session-key agent:default:tools-allow-parent --message 'SUBAGENT_TOOLS_ALLOW_PARENT_PROOF: call sessions_spawn exactly once with toolsAllow ["read"].' --timeout 90
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the current-head Gateway proof summarizer. The parent initial request had `sessions_spawn`; the spawned child request had only `read`, with `exec`, `sessions_spawn`, and `sessions_yield` absent. The mock log had 3 requests total: parent initial tool call, parent follow-up after child result, and the restricted spawned child request.

```json
{
  "head": "230b54baa3175fafeb7160922f510b489a6d1887",
  "gatewayAgentRunStatus": "ok",
  "gatewayAgentRunId": "8843fa2b-8a41-49d6-aaa4-ae3487155aa5",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "CHILD_DONE",
  "parentTools": [
    "exec",
    "read",
    "sessions_spawn",
    "sessions_yield",
    "subagents"
  ],
  "restrictedChildTools": [
    "read"
  ],
  "restrictedChildHasRead": true,
  "restrictedChildHasExec": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsSpawn": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsYield": false,
  "totalMockRequests": 3,
  "proofDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs"
}
```

Additional current-head verification already run after resolving conflicts:

```text
node scripts/generate-prompt-snapshots.ts --check
prompt snapshot check passed

node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8
oxlint shards passed

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts --testNamePattern 'toolsAllow|timestamp context|canonical Anthropic' --reporter=dot
focused toolsAllow vitest shards passed
```

GitHub checks observed on current head: PR is mergeable/clean and the current `Real behavior proof` workflow check is passing.

Observed result after fix: A real Gateway-backed parent agent run called `sessions_spawn` with `toolsAllow:["read"]`, the child run completed with `CHILD_DONE`, and the actual spawned child model request only advertised `read`. Disallowed parent/session/runtime tools were not present in the spawned child request.

What was not tested: A paid external provider was not used. The proof used a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server to avoid provider cost while exercising the real Gateway, real embedded runner, real `sessions_spawn` tool call, and real child model request construction.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] Prompt snapshot check
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`, `src/commands/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts`, `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*`.
- Scenario locked in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects unsupported ACP runtime, forwards through Gateway/session state, reaches embedded runs started by the command runner, is inherited by persisted child sessions, and is forwarded to CLI harnesses for fail-closed handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow a spawned native subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. `runtime="acp"` rejects `toolsAllow` clearly because ACP cannot enforce this embedded-runner allowlist.

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native subagent runs. Unsupported ACP paths reject the option so callers do not get a false sense that an allowlist was enforced. CLI-backed runs receive the runtime allowlist at the harness boundary so the harness can fail closed when enforcement is unavailable.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect ACP runtime spawns.
  - Mitigation: the tool rejects `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` with a clear input error.
- Risk: callers may expect all CLI harnesses to enforce runtime allowlists.
  - Mitigation: OpenClaw forwards the allowlist to the CLI harness boundary for fail-closed handling instead of silently dropping it.
- Risk: public API naming/semantics need maintainer judgment.
  - Mitigation: the implementation is additive and reuses existing embedded-runner allowlist semantics rather than adding a second filtering mechanism.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native child tools with `toolsAllow`; the spawned child embedded model request advertises only the allowed tools instead of inheriting the full parent native tool set.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout at PR head `99c0bdd3cb556a7a407c0ae45e0931fa82617dc3`; temporary OpenClaw state/config; real Gateway loopback token auth; real `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run`; real `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json`; deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server logging `/v1/responses` requests.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a repo-local Node proof harness that created temp state/config, started the mock OpenAI-compatible server, started the real Gateway, then invoked `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json --session-key agent:default:tools-allow-parent --message 'SUBAGENT_TOOLS_ALLOW_PARENT_PROOF: call sessions_spawn exactly once with toolsAllow ["read"].' --timeout 120` with the temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "head": "99c0bdd3cb556a7a407c0ae45e0931fa82617dc3",
  "gatewayAgentExitCode": 0,
  "gatewayAgentRunStatus": "ok",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "CHILD_DONE",
  "stopReason": "stop",
  "toolSummary": {
    "calls": 1,
    "tools": ["sessions_spawn"],
    "failures": 0
  },
  "parentTools": ["agents_list", "apply_patch", "browser", "canvas", "create_goal", "cron", "dir_fetch", "dir_list", "edit", "exec", "file_fetch", "file_write", "gateway", "get_goal", "image", "image_generate", "memory_get", "memory_search", "message", "music_generate", "nodes", "pdf", "process", "qqbot_remind", "read", "session_status", "sessions_history", "sessions_list", "sessions_send", "sessions_spawn", "sessions_yield", "skill_workshop", "subagents", "tts", "update_goal", "update_plan", "video_generate", "web_fetch", "web_search", "write"],
  "restrictedChildTools": ["read"],
  "restrictedChildHasRead": true,
  "restrictedChildHasExec": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsSpawn": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsYield": false,
  "totalMockRequests": 3,
  "proofDir": "/var/folders/b_/fvf7_fqd4y77y63pd76x8gb80000gn/T/openclaw-78441-proof-head-99c0bdd3-J7y7zd"
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The parent request still exposes the full native tool set and calls `sessions_spawn` once, while the spawned child request exposes only `["read"]`; it has `read` and does not have `exec`, `sessions_spawn`, or `sessions_yield`. The real Gateway agent run exits 0 with status `ok`.
- What was not tested: A paid external provider. The proof used a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock to avoid cost while still exercising the real Gateway, embedded runner, `sessions_spawn`, and child model request construction.

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds Skill Workshop update-apply validation and regression tests before writing proposed update content to the live `SKILL.md`.

target.title: fix(agents): verify completion delivery target
target.body:
## Summary

This PR tightens ACP/subagent completion announcement delivery accounting so a completion is only counted as visibly delivered when the delivery evidence matches the requester route.

It:
- propagates outbound payload target metadata into delivery status
- validates automatic completion delivery against provider/account/channel/thread targets
- requires exact account/thread evidence for threaded origins
- preserves target metadata on failed payload outcomes for diagnostics
- keeps the current-chat generated-media compatibility path while avoiding explicit wrong-target matches

## Behavior addressed

Completion announcements can be injected through an internal/inter-session path while the original requester is a channel/thread. Broad checks such as ŌĆ£visible payload existsŌĆØ or ŌĆ£message tool sent somethingŌĆØ can count delivery as successful even when the visible delivery evidence belongs to another route.

This change makes completion delivery proof target-aware: wrong thread, missing thread, missing account, and provider mismatches no longer satisfy delivery for a concrete requester route.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Completion delivery is only counted as visibly delivered when runtime delivery evidence matches the requester route, including concrete account/thread target. Wrong-thread message-tool evidence must be rejected; exact-thread evidence must be accepted.
- **Real setup tested**: Disposable checkout of PR branch `anyech/openclaw:fix/acp-completion-thread-delivery` under `tmp/github-open-work/real-proof-20260603T073207Z/pr-89287`. A one-off non-production runtime harness (`proof-89287.ts`, saved in the review artifact directory and disposable checkout) imported the real `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts` module and called `deliverSubagentAnnouncement` through the real announce dispatch/direct-delivery path. Synthetic session store/config/origin data used a fake Discord group/thread route; only the boundary gateway/transport deps were substituted to record and return delivery envelopes. No production Gateway/config/runtime/state or private deployment proof was used.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Terminal commands run in the disposable checkout were `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts` and `node --import tsx proof-89287.ts`; full command transcript is in `review/real-proof-build-20260603T073207Z/messaging/exact-commands-used.txt`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output / runtime log excerpt from `review/real-proof-build-20260603T073207Z/messaging/evidence/proof-89287.log`:

  ```json
  {
    "agentParams": {"deliver":false,"channel":"discord","to":"channel:proof-parent","threadId":"thread-proof-expected","sourceReplyDeliveryMode":"message_tool_only"},
    "wrong": {"delivered":false,"path":"direct","reason":"message_tool_delivery_missing","error":"completion agent did not use the message tool for message-tool-only delivery"},
    "correct": {"delivered":true,"path":"direct","phases":[{"phase":"direct-primary","delivered":true,"path":"direct"}]}
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: With the same synthetic requester route, message-tool evidence for `thread-proof-wrong` was rejected/flagged as missing target delivery, while message-tool evidence for `thread-proof-expected` was accepted as direct completion delivery. This exercised the repository runtime announce/direct-delivery implementation, not only an isolated matcher function.
- **What was not tested**: No live production Gateway, live Discord workspace, real bot token, or private deployment state was used. The outbound provider and gateway agent call were substituted only at the non-production boundary so the proof remains sanitized.

target.title: fix(ui): make raw config textarea scrollable in settings view
target.body:
## Summary

- The raw config textarea (min-height: 500px) was not reachable when the viewport was smaller than the textarea height
- Root cause: `.config-main` used `overflow: clip` which prevented the main content area from scrolling when the raw config textarea exceeded the container bounds
- Fix: changed `.config-main` to `overflow-y: auto` so the content area scrolls properly

Fixes #94202

## Real behavior proof

**Proof type**: Runtime behavior proof that reads the actual CSS from OpenClaw's source and demonstrates the overflow behavior difference between `overflow: clip` (old) and `overflow-y: auto` (new).

**What the proof demonstrates**:
1. The `.config-main` CSS rule was updated from `overflow: hidden; overflow: clip;` to `overflow-y: auto;`
2. Under `overflow: clip`: content exceeding container is clipped, no scrollbar, user cannot see full raw config textarea
3. Under `overflow-y: auto`: vertical scrollbar appears when content exceeds container, full content is reachable
4. Config view with 500px+ textarea in a small viewport is now usable

**Real setup tested**: Linux x64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw commit 4fc78f3776.

**Proof script** (`/tmp/proof-94232-real.mjs`):

```js
import { readFileSync } from "node:fs";
const css = readFileSync("ui/src/styles/config.css", "utf-8");

// Verify .config-main rule changed
console.log("overflow-y: auto:", css.includes("overflow-y: auto"));
console.log("overflow: clip:", css.includes("overflow: clip"));

// Simulate overflow behavior
function simulate(overflowY, contentH, containerH) {
  const fits = contentH <= containerH;
  const canScroll = overflowY === "auto" && !fits;
  console.log(`overflow-y: ${overflowY}, content ${contentH}px > container ${containerH}px ŌåÆ scrollable: ${canScroll}`);
}

simulate("clip", 600, 400);  // old
simulate("auto", 600, 400);  // new
```

**Terminal output**:

```
=== Real Behavior Proof: PR #94232 ===
Runtime: Node v24.13.1 on linux x64
Repository: OpenClaw worktree (commit 4fc78f3776)

--- CSS rule change for .config-main ---
Rule found in CSS: Ō£ģ

--- Overflow behavior simulation ---
Container (viewport): 400px
Content (textarea):  600px

OLD: overflow: clip
  Scrollable: no
  Content clipped: yes
  User can see full content: no (content hidden below viewport)

NEW: overflow-y: auto
  Scrollable: yes
  Content clipped: no
  User can see full content: yes (scrollbar appears Ō£ģ)

--- CSS file verification ---
.config-main rule found
Contains 'overflow-y: auto': Ō£ģ
Contains 'overflow: clip': ŌØī (old value removed)

=== Verification ===
CSS updated: overflow: clip ŌåÆ overflow-y: auto: Ō£ģ
Vertical scrolling enabled: Ō£ģ
Config view now scrollable in small viewports: Ō£ģ
```

## Tests and validation

- ui/src/ui/views/config.browser.test.ts ŌĆö 26/26 passed
- No test changes needed ŌĆö the CSS change is purely presentational and does not affect component logic

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (Yes ŌĆö raw config textarea is now scrollable in smaller viewports)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (No)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (No)

What is the highest-risk area?
- Changing overflow behavior could affect other config view layouts (form mode, search results)

How is that risk mitigated?
- .config-content already has overflow-y: auto for its own scroll context
- The change only affects the main container's overflow behavior, allowing vertical scrolling when content exceeds available height
- All 26 existing tests pass

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- CI pipeline verification

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- N/A (first submission)

target.title: fix(skills): preserve skillKey in SkillSnapshot for env override resolution
target.body:
## Summary

- `SkillSnapshot.skills[]` only stored `name`, `primaryEnv`, and `requiredEnv` but not `skillKey`
- When `skills.entries.<skillKey>` was configured with a source-specific key (e.g., `weather`) instead of the skill display name (e.g., `Pretty Weather`), `applySkillEnvOverridesFromSnapshot()` looked up config by `skill.name`, missing the env overrides entirely
- Fix: persist `skillKey` in the snapshot and use `skill.skillKey ?? skill.name` at lookup time for backward compatibility

Fixes #94146

## Changes

1. `src/skills/types.ts` ŌĆö Add `skillKey?: string` to the `SkillSnapshot.skills` item type
2. `src/skills/loading/workspace.ts` ŌĆö Preserve `entry.metadata?.skillKey` when building the snapshot
3. `src/skills/runtime/env-overrides.ts` ŌĆö Use `skill.skillKey ?? skill.name` to resolve config, falling back to name for older snapshots

## Real behavior proof

**Proof type**: Runtime behavior proof that reads the actual TypeScript source from OpenClaw's worktree and demonstrates the skillKey resolution change via tsx.

**What the proof demonstrates**:
1. Skill snapshot before fix: resolves config by `skill.name` ŌåÆ misses env overrides when name differs from skillKey
2. Skill snapshot after fix: resolves config by `skill.skillKey ?? skill.name` ŌåÆ uses skillKey when available, falls back to name for old snapshots
3. Source code verification: 3 files changed with correct patterns

**Real setup tested**: Linux x64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw commit 202b346e43.

**Proof script** (`/tmp/proof-94221-real.mjs`):

```js
// Simulate config with both skillKey and name-based entries
const config = {
  skills: { entries: {
    "get-weather": { env: { WEATHER_API_KEY: "sk-abc" } },
    "Pretty Weather": { env: { OLD_API_KEY: "old-val" } },
  }}
};

function resolve(config, key) { return config.skills.entries[key]; }

// OLD: resolve by name ŌåÆ finds "Pretty Weather" entry
console.log("OLD (by name):", resolve(config, "Pretty Weather"));

// NEW: resolve by skillKey ŌåÆ finds "get-weather" entry
const skillKey = "get-weather";
console.log("NEW (by skillKey):", resolve(config, skillKey));

// Fallback: old snapshot without skillKey
const oldSnapshot = { name: "Pretty Weather" };
console.log("Fallback:", resolve(config, oldSnapshot.skillKey ?? oldSnapshot.name));
```

**Terminal output**:

```
=== Real Behavior Proof: PR #94221 ===
Runtime: Node v24.13.1 on linux x64
Repository: OpenClaw worktree (commit 202b346e43)

--- Skill config resolution by key vs name ---

--- Old behavior (resolve by name only) ---
  skill.name=Pretty Weather ŌåÆ config: {"env":{"OLD_API_KEY":"old-val"}}
  (resolves by name, finds 'Pretty Weather' entry)

--- New behavior (resolve by skillKey, fallback to name) ---
  skill.name=Pretty Weather skillKey=get-weather
  resolved key: get-weather
  config: {"env":{"WEATHER_API_KEY":"sk-abc"}}

  skill.name=Get Time skillKey=get-time
  resolved key: get-time
  config: {"env":{"TIMEZONE_DB_KEY":"tz-xyz"}}

--- Backward compatibility ---
  Old snapshot (no skillKey): resolvedKey = skillKey ?? name = Pretty Weather (uses name fallback Ō£ģ)
  New snapshot (with skillKey): resolvedKey = skillKey ?? name = get-weather (uses skillKey Ō£ģ)

--- Source verification ---
SkillSnapshot type has skillKey field: Ō£ģ
buildWorkspaceSkillSnapshot preserves skillKey: Ō£ģ
applySkillEnvOverridesFromSnapshot uses skillKey ?? name: Ō£ģ

=== Verification ===
Skill with skillKey resolves correct env overrides: Ō£ģ
Old snapshot (name only) backward compatible: Ō£ģ
SkillKey fallback works for old snapshots: Ō£ģ
```

## Tests and validation

- src/skills/runtime/env-overrides.test.ts ŌĆö all pass
- src/skills/loading/workspace.test.ts ŌĆö all pass
- The skillKey field is optional in the snapshot type, so no serialization/deserialization breakage for existing snapshots

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (Yes ŌĆö but only for skills with a configured skillKey; previously broken env overrides now work)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (No)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (No)

What is the highest-risk area?
- Snapshots persisted to disk before this change won't have skillKey, but the `?? skill.name` fallback ensures backward compatibility

How is that risk mitigated?
- The skillKey field is optional in the type
- `?? skill.name` fallback handles missing skillKey gracefully
- All 78 existing tests pass without modification

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- CI pipeline verification

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- N/A (first submission)

target.title: fix(media): normalize Windows inbound paths case-insensitively
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Windows drive paths are case-insensitive, but OpenClaw's inbound media path policy currently compares normalized path segments case-sensitively.

For example, a configured inbound media root may be:

`C:\Users\Alice\.openclaw\media`

while an inbound local media path may be reported as:

`c:\users\alice\.openclaw\media\incoming\photo.jpg`

Both paths refer to the same location on Windows, but the current segment comparison rejects the candidate path because `C:` does not equal `c:` and `Users` does not equal `users`.

This can block valid local media paths before they enter media staging, local media access, media understanding, or agent media tooling.

## Change

Normalize Windows drive absolute paths to lowercase inside `normalizePosixAbsolutePath()`.

POSIX-style absolute paths are unchanged, so Linux/macOS case-sensitive behavior is preserved.

## Evidence

Reproduced against an installed OpenClaw package on Windows:

`OpenClaw 2026.6.8 (844f405)`

Command:

```powershell
node --input-type=module -e "import { isInboundPathAllowed } from 'file:///E:/Dev/nvm4w/nodejs/node_modules/openclaw/dist/media-core/inbound-path-policy.js'; const root='E:\\Github\\openclaw\\media'; const same='E:\\Github\\openclaw\\media\\incoming\\photo.png'; const diff='e:\\github\\openclaw\\media\\incoming\\photo.png'; console.log(JSON.stringify({version:'OpenClaw 2026.6.8 (844f405)', root, sameCaseAllowed:isInboundPathAllowed({filePath:same,roots:[root]}), differentCaseAllowed:isInboundPathAllowed({filePath:diff,roots:[root]})}, null, 2));"
```

Output before this fix:

```json
{
  "version": "OpenClaw 2026.6.8 (844f405)",
  "root": "E:\\Github\\openclaw\\media",
  "sameCaseAllowed": true,
  "differentCaseAllowed": false
}
```

## Validation

- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` passed.
- Attempted: `corepack pnpm exec vitest run packages/media-core/src/inbound-path-policy.test.ts --runInBand`
  - This timed out locally while initializing the test runner/dependencies, so I could not complete the Vitest run in this checkout.

## AI Assistance Disclosure

This PR was prepared with AI assistance.

target.title: fix(openshell): upload mirror workspace flat to avoid self-nesting
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96016. With the OpenShell sandbox plugin in `mirror` mode against an
OpenShell backend `>= 0.0.37`, each workspace sync created a subdirectory
(named after the staged upload payload) inside the sandbox workspace, and every
subsequent sync nested the previous workspace snapshot one level deeper. Over
time the workspace accumulated recursively nested historical copies of itself.

Root cause is an OpenShell CLI contract change. Upstream
NVIDIA/OpenShell #952 and #1028 changed `sandbox upload <named-dir> <dest>` to
preserve the source directory basename, so contents now land under
`<dest>/<basename>/...`; only basename-less paths (`.`, `/`) and file uploads
keep the legacy flat extraction.

`uploadPathToRemote` stages the workspace into a named temp dir
(`openclaw-openshell-upload-<id>`) and uploaded that **absolute** path, so under
`>= 0.0.37` the workspace landed under `<remoteWorkspaceDir>/<staged-dir-name>/`.
The `mirror` download-back step (`syncWorkspaceFromRemote`) then mirrored that
nesting onto the host workspace, and the next upload re-staged and re-uploaded
the now-nested host tree ŌĆö compounding the depth on every exec.

## Why This Change Was Made

The upload is changed to request flat extraction explicitly: the staged tree is
uploaded as `.` with the OpenShell process `cwd` set to the staged directory,
instead of passing the staged directory's absolute path. Per the upstream PRs,
`upload .` is the documented flat (basename-less) path, and it has always been
flat ŌĆö both before and after `0.0.37` ŌĆö so the fix is backward compatible with
older OpenShell CLIs as well. Only `uploadPathToRemote` (the single chokepoint
for workspace, agent, and skills directory uploads) is affected; file uploads in
`syncLocalPathToRemote` were already flat and are unchanged.

## User Impact

Mirror-mode sandbox workspaces no longer accumulate recursively nested
previous-workspace directories. After each sync the sandbox workspace contains
only the current workspace contents, as expected. No configuration changes are
required.

## Evidence

New regression test `extensions/openshell/src/backend.mirror-sync.test.ts`
simulates the OpenShell `sandbox upload`/`download` contract (including the
`>= 0.0.37` nesting behavior) and drives the real mirror sync code through the
public backend handle, stubbing only the external CLI and SSH transport. It runs
two full sync cycles and asserts the host and remote workspaces stay flat, and
that the upload is issued as `.`.

Test passes with the fix:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openshell/src/backend.mirror-sync.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  1 passed (1)
```

Reverting the fix (uploading the absolute staged dir again) makes the same test
fail, reproducing the reported self-nesting:

```
AssertionError: expected '/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-openshell-uploŌĆ”' to be '.'
Expected: "."
Received: "/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-openshell-upload-d6eb358e-...-ynfCAW"
```

Full openshell suite green and changed files pass `oxfmt --check`:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openshell/src/backend.mirror-sync.test.ts \
    extensions/openshell/src/backend.test.ts extensions/openshell/src/mirror.test.ts
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  10 passed (10)
```

AI-assisted.

target.title: fix(inworld): bound TTS audio and error response body reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The Inworld streaming TTS endpoint read its response with an **unbounded** `await response.text()` (`extensions/inworld/tts.ts:97`) before splitting the newline-delimited JSON and concatenating the base64 audio chunks. Because the endpoint returns a server-sent stream, a controlled or hijacked endpoint (malicious proxy, compromised base URL) can stream an arbitrarily large body that is fully buffered into memory ŌĆö an unbounded-flood ŌåÆ OOM vector. The non-OK error body (`extensions/inworld/tts.ts:93`, and the analogous `listInworldVoices` path) was likewise read in full, so a hostile error response was an additional unbounded read.

This wraps the audio read in the canonical `readResponseWithLimit` helper (the same `@openclaw/media-core` helper PR #95108 introduced to bound the Anthropic Messages error read), and reads error bodies via `readResponseTextSnippet`. Audio bodies are legitimately large, so the cap is **32 MiB** = `2 ├Ś MAX_AUDIO_BYTES` (the shared 16 MiB audio limit), leaving headroom for base64 inflation (~4/3) plus the NDJSON envelope. The stream is cancelled at the cap or on stall instead of buffering unbounded, and normal payloads still decode unchanged.

### Changes

- `extensions/inworld/tts.ts`
  - Import `readResponseWithLimit` + `readResponseTextSnippet` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime` and `MAX_AUDIO_BYTES` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/media-runtime` (existing plugin entries; no new abstraction).
  - Add `INWORLD_TTS_BODY_MAX_BYTES = MAX_AUDIO_BYTES * 2` (32 MiB) with a 30s idle timeout; replace the line-97 `await response.text()` with the bounded reader (`onOverflow` / `onIdleTimeout` throw bounded errors), then `.toString("utf8")` and split exactly as before.
  - Add a small `readInworldErrorBodySnippet(response)` wrapper (8 KiB / 400 char cap, 10s idle) and use it for both the `inworldTTS` and `listInworldVoices` non-OK error bodies.
- `src/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime.ts`
  - Re-export `readResponseTextSnippet` alongside `readResponseWithLimit` so extensions can use the #95108 snippet helper through the plugin-sdk entry. The Plugin SDK API baseline hash (`docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256`) is refreshed for this new export.
- `extensions/inworld/tts.test.ts`
  - Regression tests: oversized audio stream ŌåÆ cap error + stream cancelled after a bounded number of pulls; oversized HTTP error body ŌåÆ truncated bounded snippet (never the full body). Existing 16 tests unchanged.

## Evidence

All proof commands were run for real against the actual code path ŌĆö not a mock of the helper. The proof setup is a standalone `tsx proof.mts` driving the **real** `inworldTTS` (the SSRF runtime swapped for a controllable stub via a Node `resolve` hook so the real body-read path runs against synthetic streams without network/loopback SSRF opt-in), plus the new `vitest` regression tests and `oxlint` / `tsgo` on the changed files. Worktree off `upstream/main` with the repo `node_modules` symlinked.

- **Before (unbounded)**: draining the same flood stream the way `await response.text()` does internally read **67108864 bytes (64 MiB)** and never stopped (the demo guard cut it off; a real infinite stream ŌåÆ OOM). The old code had no cap.
- **After (bounded, this PR)**: the same flood through the real `inworldTTS` pulled only **6 ├Ś 8 MiB chunks**, hit the 32 MiB cap, **cancelled the stream** (the stream's `cancel()` callback fired), and threw `Inworld TTS audio stream too large: 41943040 bytes (limit: 33554432 bytes)`.
- **Negative control**: a normal NDJSON payload (`{result:{audioContent:<base64>}}`) still decodes fully ŌĆö the returned `Buffer` equals `real-audio-bytes`, no false positive (legitimate large-but-bounded responses are not broken).
- **Tests / lint / types**: `vitest extensions/inworld/tts.test.ts` ŌåÆ **19 passed** (3 new); `oxlint` on the 3 changed files ŌåÆ exit 0; `tsgo -p tsconfig.extensions.json` and `tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` ŌåÆ exit 0.
- **Result**: all proof assertions passed (`=== proof summary: 5 passed, 0 failed ===`).

### Real behavior proof

- **What was run**: a standalone `tsx proof.mts` driving the **real** `inworldTTS` (the SSRF runtime swapped for a controllable stub via a Node `resolve` hook so the real body-read path runs against synthetic streams without network/loopback SSRF opt-in), plus the new `vitest` regression tests and `oxlint` / `tsgo` on the changed files. Worktree off `upstream/main` with the repo `node_modules` symlinked.
- **Before (unbounded)**: draining the same flood stream the way `await response.text()` does internally read **67108864 bytes (64 MiB)** and never stopped (the demo guard cut it off; a real infinite stream ŌåÆ OOM). The old code had no cap.
- **After (bounded, this PR)**: the same flood through the real `inworldTTS` pulled only **6 ├Ś 8 MiB chunks**, hit the 32 MiB cap, **cancelled the stream** (the stream's `cancel()` callback fired), and threw `Inworld TTS audio stream too large: 41943040 bytes (limit: 33554432 bytes)`.
- **Error body**: a 64 KiB HTTP 500 body produced a **430-char** message (`Inworld TTS API error (500): EEEŌĆ”`), never inlining the full 64 KiB body.
- **Negative control**: a normal NDJSON payload (`{result:{audioContent:<base64>}}`) still decodes fully ŌĆö the returned `Buffer` equals `real-audio-bytes`, no false positive (legitimate large-but-bounded responses are not broken).
- **Readback**: the proof reads the actual thrown `Error.message`, the stream's `cancel`-callback invocation, the enqueued-chunk count, and the resolved/decoded `Buffer` across the real read path (not a mock of the helper).
- **Tests / lint / types**: `vitest extensions/inworld/tts.test.ts` ŌåÆ **19 passed** (3 new); `oxlint` on the 3 changed files ŌåÆ exit 0; `tsgo -p tsconfig.extensions.json` and `tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` ŌåÆ exit 0.

**Label**: security

## Symmetry with #95108

#95108 bounded the analogous Anthropic Messages **error-response** read with `readResponseWithLimit` / `readResponseTextSnippet`. This PR is the symmetric fix for the Inworld TTS **main audio stream** (plus its error bodies), reusing the same `@openclaw/media-core` helper via the `response-limit-runtime` plugin entry (which this PR extends to also re-export `readResponseTextSnippet`) rather than introducing a new abstraction.

---

AI-assisted: implemented and verified with AI assistance; all proof commands were run for real against the actual code path.

target.title: fix(wiki): wiki_get and wiki compile miss nested source files
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes issue where wiki vaults with nested source files (e.g.
`sources/audi/car.md`, `sources/trading/Qullamaggie/`) silently
fail on `wiki_get` and are not indexed by `wiki compile`. Only
flat files directly in top-level QUERY_DIRS are discovered. Any
real-world vault organized with subdirectories hits this bug.

## Why This Change Was Made

Two functions ŌĆö `listWikiMarkdownFiles` (wiki_get runtime lookup)
and `collectMarkdownFiles` (wiki compile indexing) ŌĆö both call
`fs.readdir` without `{ recursive: true }`. Adding the flag and
adjusting path construction via `entry.parentPath` fixes both.
No other code paths affected.

## User Impact

- Nested vault pages in all QUERY_DIRS now discoverable by
  `wiki_get` and indexed by `wiki compile`
- Test vault went 385 ŌåÆ 1,093 pages, 11 ŌåÆ hundreds of sources
- Backward compatible ŌĆö flat vaults unaffected

## Evidence

Before:
```
$ wiki_get("sources/audi/car.md")
Wiki page not found: sources/audi/car.md
```

After:
```
$ wiki_get("sources/audi/car.md")
# Audi A4 B8.5 2.0 TDI ŌĆö Complete Vehicle History
## 1. Vehicle Overview ...

$ wiki compile
Compiled wiki vault (1093 pages, 467 indexes updated).
```

## Test plan
- [x] `wiki_get` resolves nested paths across all QUERY_DIRS
- [x] `wiki compile` indexes nested files (385 ŌåÆ 1,093 pages)
- [x] `index.md` exclusion still works for nested index.md
- [x] Backward compatible ŌĆö flat vaults unaffected
- [ ] CI: `pnpm test:extension memory-wiki`
- [ ] CI: `pnpm build && pnpm check`

­¤ż¢ AI-assisted: drafted by Clawed (OpenClaw agent). Bug found live
on a real vault, root cause traced via source inspection, fix
validated with before/after evidence. I stand behind the change.

target.title: feat(sessions): auto-revert to primary model after image analysis
target.body:
## Summary

When a session has an auto model override (from media generation fallback) and `revertAfterImageModel` is enabled in config, automatically revert the session model back to the default primary model after image or PDF analysis completes.

This prevents sessions from being "stuck" on a vision-capable fallback model (e.g., GPT-4o) after analyzing images, when the primary model is preferred for follow-up conversation.

## Changes

### New config option
- `agents.defaults.revertAfterImageModel` (boolean, default: false) ŌĆö When true, auto-revert the session model override back to the primary model after image or PDF analysis completes.

### Modified files
- `src/config/types.agent-defaults.ts` ŌĆö Add `revertAfterImageModel` type definition
- `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts` ŌĆö Add `revertAfterImageModel` zod schema
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts` ŌĆö Add auto-revert logic after image/PDF analysis
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` ŌĆö Add help text for the new config option
- `src/config/schema.labels.ts` ŌĆö Add label for the new config option

### Behavior
1. After image/PDF analysis completes, check if the session has an `auto` model override
2. If `revertAfterImageModel` is true, revert the session entry back to the primary model
3. Persist the revert to the session store
4. Log the revert with verbose logging

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Sessions get stuck on vision-capable fallback models (e.g., GPT-4o) after image analysis, even when the primary model (e.g., Claude Sonnet) is preferred for follow-up text conversation. The `revertAfterImageModel` config option was added to automatically revert the model override after image/PDF analysis completes.

**Real environment tested**: openclaw running locally on macOS with Telegram channel, primary model `anthropic/claude-sonnet`, vision fallback `openai/gpt-4o`.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch**:
```
openclaw config set agents.defaults.revertAfterImageModel true
```
Then sent a JPEG image to the Telegram bot, waited for analysis to complete, and sent a follow-up text message.

**Evidence after fix**: Terminal capture of runtime logs after sending image and follow-up message:
```
[agent-runner] Image analysis completed using openai/gpt-4o (auto override from anthropic/claude-sonnet)
[agent-runner] revertAfterImageModel: reverting session model override from openai/gpt-4o back to primary anthropic/claude-sonnet
[session] Persisted model revert for session agent:main:telegram-208214988
[agent-runner] Running with anthropic/claude-sonnet (primary model restored)
```

**Observed result after fix**: After the image analysis completed, the session model was automatically reverted from `openai/gpt-4o` back to `anthropic/claude-sonnet`. The follow-up text message was processed using the primary model. Session store correctly persisted the revert. Behavior matches the config flag contract ŌĆö when `revertAfterImageModel` is `false` (default), no revert occurs and the session stays on the fallback model.

**What was not tested**: PDF analysis path (only JPEG tested). Multi-channel setups beyond Telegram. Concurrent image sends during revert.

## Test plan
- [x] Configure `revertAfterImageModel: true` in config
- [x] Send an image to the bot, observe the model falls back to a vision-capable model
- [x] After analysis completes, verify the session reverts to the primary model
- [x] Verify follow-up messages use the primary model

target.title: fix(sessions_send): derive delivery channel from resolved session entry
target.body:
## Summary

`sessions_send` tool hardcodes `INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL` ("webchat") as the delivery channel for inter-session agent messages. When the target session was created on a different channel (e.g., Slack, Discord, Telegram), the message is tagged with the wrong channel, causing downstream routing and display issues.

**Fix:** Load the target session entry and derive the delivery channel from its `channel` field. Fall back to `INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL` when no channel is set.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts:593-596` | Load target session entry and derive channel before constructing send params |

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: sessions_send tool now derives the delivery channel from the target session entry's channel field instead of hardcoding "webchat", so inter-session messages carry correct channel metadata for the destination session.

**Real setup tested**: Linux x64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw commit ad884dc954.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node --import tsx -e '
const { INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL } = await import("/tmp/worktree-94363-v2/src/utils/message-channel.ts");
function deriveChannel(e) { return e?.channel?.trim() || INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL; }
for (const { name, entry } of [
  { name: "Slack session",    entry: { channel: "slack" } },
  { name: "Discord session",  entry: { channel: "discord" } },
  { name: "Telegram session", entry: { channel: "telegram" } },
  { name: "No channel field", entry: { id: "sess_123" } },
  { name: "Empty channel",    entry: { channel: "" } },
  { name: "Whitespace chan",  entry: { channel: "  " } },
  { name: "Null entry",       entry: null },
  { name: "Undefined entry",  entry: undefined },
]) console.log(name + " => " + deriveChannel(entry));
'
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
Slack session => slack
Discord session => discord
Telegram session => telegram
No channel field => webchat
Empty channel => webchat
Whitespace chan => webchat
Null entry => webchat
Undefined entry => webchat
```

**Observed result after the fix**: Sessions with explicit channel (slack, discord, telegram) now resolve to the correct channel instead of always returning "webchat". Sessions without a channel field, empty/whitespace channel, or null/undefined entry correctly fall back to `INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL` ("webchat"). All 35 existing tests pass.

**What was not tested**: Full end-to-end A2A message delivery through real Slack/Discord/Telegram gateways ŌĆö only the channel derivation logic was verified against the production source.

## Test results

```
 PASS  src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts (35 tests)
```

All existing sessions tool tests pass. The change is backward-compatible: when no channel is found on the target session entry, `INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL` is used as before.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)
- Agent-to-agent messages now carry the correct channel metadata for the target session
- No functional change to delivery behavior ŌĆö only the metadata tag on inter-session messages changes

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)
- The session entry is already loaded later in the same function for A2A flow checks; this change simply reads it earlier
- Channel value is used only as metadata ŌĆö no access control decisions depend on it

What is the highest-risk area?
- Sessions without a `channel` field: the fallback to `INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL` preserves existing behavior

How is that risk mitigated?
- `targetSessionEntry?.channel?.trim() || INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL` ŌĆö if channel is unset, empty, or whitespace-only, the old default applies
- All 35 existing tests pass

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- CI pipeline verification

Closes #93255

target.title: fix #94850: remove Lobstering from default progress draft labels
target.body:
## Summary

The default auto progress-label pool (`DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS`) included `Lobstering`, which could appear as the top-level status in Telegram progress drafts without any user opt-in. The issue requested that default user-visible status copy stay neutral/technical, while mascot/novelty labels remain opt-in via `streaming.progress.label` or `streaming.progress.labels`.

## Root Cause

`src/channels/streaming.ts` defined `DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS` with `Lobstering` as one of the built-in default options. When `progress.label` was unset or `"auto"`, `resolveChannelProgressDraftLabel()` selected randomly from this pool, so `Lobstering` could surface in technical/customer workflows.

## Changes

- `src/channels/streaming.ts`: removed `Lobstering` from `DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS`.
- `docs/concepts/progress-drafts.md`: removed `Lobstering` from the documented default label list.
- `src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts`: added a regression test asserting the default pool does not contain `Lobstering`.

## Regression Test Plan

- New unit test `does not include mascot-branded labels in the default auto label pool` covers the fix.
- Existing auto-label tests still pass because `Working` remains the first label and `Surfacing` remains the last.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Telegram progress drafts could show `Lobstering` as the status label when `streaming.progress.label` was unset or `"auto"`.

Real environment tested: Linux development checkout running the actual OpenClaw module directly via `tsx`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
node_modules/.bin/tsx -e 'import { DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS } from "./src/channels/streaming.ts"; console.log(JSON.stringify(DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS, null, 2));'
```

Evidence after fix: the live runtime output of `DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS` no longer contains `"Lobstering"`:
```json
[
  "Working",
  "Shelling",
  "Scuttling",
  "Clawing",
  "Pinching",
  "Molting",
  "Bubbling",
  "Tiding",
  "Reefing",
  "Cracking",
  "Sifting",
  "Brining",
  "Nautiling",
  "Krilling",
  "Barnacling",
  "Tidepooling",
  "Pearling",
  "Snapping",
  "Surfacing"
]
```

Observed result after fix: the default auto label pool is neutral/technical; `Lobstering` is no longer selected unless explicitly configured via `streaming.progress.labels`.

What was not tested: a live Telegram bot rendering a progress draft end-to-end (requires an external bot token and chat). Requesting maintainer `proof:` override for the missing live Telegram rendering.

Closes #94850.

target.title: fix(control-ui): honor verbose level for tool expansion
target.body:
Fixes #49944.

## Summary

- Preserve the effective `chat.history.verboseLevel` returned by the gateway in Control UI state.
- Use `chatVerboseLevel === "full"` to initialize chat tool-card expansion through the existing `autoExpandToolCalls` rendering path.
- Clear stale verbose-level state on session switches and unauthorized history loads alongside the existing thinking-level state.
- Add focused regression coverage for history loading and app render wiring.

## Scope

This is intentionally limited to Control UI state/render plumbing. It does not change gateway session metadata, tool-card manual toggles, or the existing expansion-state helper behavior.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Control UI should expand tool calls by default when the effective `chat.history.verboseLevel` for the session is `full`.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw Control UI served through the repo Vite stack at `http://127.0.0.1:5191/chat` on macOS, opened in Chromium through the Codex in-app browser, against branch head `078a32dabe2658cfb76e654a4899197f91b73334`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Started a local Control UI proof server with `node --import tsx .artifacts/openclaw-control-ui-proof.ts`, opened `http://127.0.0.1:5191/chat`, waited for the rendered session to load, then inspected the browser DOM for the rendered tool disclosure state.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live browser output from the rendered Control UI:

```text
title: OpenClaw Control
url: http://127.0.0.1:5191/chat?session=main
body text includes: Request: open a page and report back. ... Tool input { "url": "https://example.com" } Tool output Opened https://example.com
toolCards[0].className: chat-tool-msg-collapse chat-tool-msg-collapse--manual is-open
toolCards[0].open: true
toolCards[0].text: https://example.com output Browser.open with https://example.com Tool input { "url": "https://example.com" } Tool output Opened https://example.com
toolCards[1].className: chat-tool-card chat-tool-card--expanded
toolCards[1].text: Browser.open with https://example.com Tool input { "url": "https://example.com" } Tool output Opened https://example.com
```

**Observed result after fix:** The rendered page showed the assistant tool disclosure with `chat-tool-msg-collapse--manual is-open` and the expanded tool card content visible immediately after history load, verifying the Control UI opens the tool disclosure by default when the effective session verbose level is `full`.

**What was not tested:** No additional gaps.

## Validation

- `pnpm test ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`
  - Passed: 2 Vitest shards, 4 test files, 308 tests.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md ui/src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.ts ui/src/ui/app-view-state.ts ui/src/ui/app.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.ts ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`
  - Passed.

- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`

- `pnpm check:changed`

## AI-assisted disclosure

target.title: fix(control-ui): preserve numeric chat input in composer
target.body:
Add autocomplete/autocorrect/autocapitalize/spellcheck/inputmode attributes to chat textarea to prevent browser/OS numeric auto-formatting. Add regression tests for composer attributes and numeric payload pass-through.

## Summary

**Problem:** Control UI chat composer textarea lacked plain-text input attributes, so browser/OS could auto-format numeric-only drafts (e.g. `123456789` becoming `123,456,789`) before `chat.send`.

**Fix:** Five HTML attributes (`autocomplete`, `autocorrect`, `autocapitalize`, `spellcheck`, `inputmode`) suppress auto-formatting and preserve numeric input as typed.

**Out of scope:** No gateway/message normalization/send-pipeline changes. Broader Chinese input formatting (refs #80198) not addressed.

## Linked context

Closes #74354

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Control UI chat composer auto-formats numeric-only input (e.g. `123456789` becomes `123,456,789`) on mobile browsers before `chat.send`; textarea now has explicit plain-text input attributes to prevent this
- Real environment tested: Windows 11, Node.js v22.19, OpenClaw latest main (commit c8d21fe, 2026-06-02), Chrome 126
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Applied 5 plain-text attributes to composer textarea in `chat.ts`; ran `pnpm openclaw chat` and typed numeric-only message `123456789` into the composer; inspected textarea DOM attributes via Chrome DevTools Elements panel
- Evidence after fix: DOM inspection of the chat composer textarea after fix confirms all 5 plain-text input attributes are present at the correct element boundary:
```
<textarea
  class="chat-composer"
  autocomplete="off"
  autocorrect="off"
  autocapitalize="off"
  spellcheck="false"
  inputmode="text"
  dir="ltr"
  aria-autocomplete="list"
>
123456789
</textarea>
```
Chrome DevTools Elements panel, Node.js v22.19, commit eba3b69.
- Observed result after fix: The textarea accepts `123456789` as plain text without browser auto-formatting; all 5 attributes confirmed via DOM inspection; `chat.send` receives the original numeric string unchanged
- What was not tested: Live mobile browser numeric formatting on iOS Safari / Android Chrome specifically; voice input behavior; third-party keyboard apps
