target.title: fix(cron): add .catch() re-arm and watchdog to prevent runtime timer chain death
target.body:
## Summary

When the cron `setTimeout` chain breaks at runtime, the scheduler silently stops firing and never recovers until a gateway restart. This was observed after ~2.5 days of continuous gateway uptime on macOS (Apple Silicon) with no errors logged.

**Root cause**: The `.catch()` handler in `armTimer()` and `armRunningRecheckTimer()` logs the error but does NOT call `armTimer(state)`, so if `onTimer()` ever rejects without reaching its `finally` block, the timer chain is permanently broken.

**Contributing factor**: On macOS, background processes (like the gateway running as a launchd agent) are subject to App Nap and timer coalescing. While `MAX_TIMER_DELAY_MS = 60s` should be short enough for the clamped `setTimeout` to survive, the OS can still defer callbacks in edge cases, and if the chain breaks once, there is no recovery mechanism.

## Changes

### 1. Re-arm in `.catch()` handler (`timer.ts`)

Both `armTimer()` and `armRunningRecheckTimer()` now call `armTimer(state)` inside `.catch()`, ensuring the timer chain is never permanently broken:

```ts
void onTimer(state).catch((err) => {
  state.deps.log.error({ err: String(err) }, "cron: timer tick failed");
  armTimer(state); // ensure chain is never broken
});
```

### 2. Independent watchdog `setInterval` (`timer.ts`)

Added `startCronWatchdog()` ŌĆö a `setInterval`-based watchdog (every 5 minutes) that detects when `nextWakeAtMs` is past-due by more than `MAX_TIMER_DELAY_MS` and force-triggers `onTimer` + re-arms the chain. `setInterval` is more resilient to OS-level timer deferral than chained `setTimeout` because libuv treats it as a persistent/repeating timer.

### 3. Lifecycle integration (`ops.ts`, `state.ts`)

- Watchdog is started in `start()` and stopped in `stop()`
- Added `_stopWatchdog` field to `CronServiceState`

### 4. Tests (`timer.watchdog.test.ts`)

- Watchdog detects stalled timer and logs warning
- Watchdog does not false-positive on healthy timers
- Watchdog respects `cronEnabled: false`
- Cleanup function stops the watchdog

## Relationship to other PRs

- **#68112** fixes a different bug: `start()` -> `runMissedJobs()` throwing kills the timer. That is a startup-time issue.
- **This PR** fixes a **runtime** issue: the timer chain dying during normal operation after hours/days of uptime.

The two fixes are complementary and non-overlapping.

Fixes #73166

target.title: fix(feishu): scope sequential queue by group session
target.body:
## Summary

Fix Feishu inbound queue scoping so grouped sessions are not still serialized at the whole-chat level.

When `groupSessionScope` is configured as `group_topic` or `group_topic_sender`, Feishu conversations are already routed into separate sessions, but `getFeishuSequentialKey` still returned `feishu:<accountId>:<chatId>` for normal messages. That meant a long-running request in one Feishu group topic could block unrelated topics/senders in the same group.

This PR makes the sequential queue key reuse the same group-session peer ID resolution used by conversation routing:

- default/group scope remains per chat
- `group_sender` queues by sender
- `group_topic` queues by topic/root/thread/message id
- `group_topic_sender` queues by topic + sender
- legacy `topicSessionMode: "enabled"` and per-group `groupSessionScope` overrides are honored
- `/stop` and `/btw` keep their existing stable per-chat lanes

It also includes a tiny Matrix test fixture update required after rebasing onto latest `main`, where the native approval runtime test helper type now requires `plannedTarget` and `view` fields.

## Tests

- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/sequential-key.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm test:extension feishu` ŌĆö 60 files / 678 tests passed
- `pnpm check:test-types`

target.title: feat: PluginStatusProvider plugin API for TUI footer status (resolves #64086)
target.body:
Resolves #64086. Coordinates with #53774 (broader footer-customization tracker).

## What this PR does

Adds a typed `PluginStatusProvider` interface and `registerStatusProvider` method to `OpenClawPluginApi`, following the same registration shape as `registerTool` / `registerHook`. Providers are stored in the plugin registry under a new `statusProviders` field.

```ts
export type PluginStatusProvider = {
  /** A unique identifier for this provider. */
  id: string;
  /** Function returning the status string, or null/undefined to hide it. */
  getStatus: () => string | null | undefined;
};
```

Plugin author usage:

```ts
api.registerStatusProvider({
  id: "contextclaw.savings",
  getStatus: () => `cc: -${savedPercent}%`,
});
```

## Scope

This PR lands the API/registry seam only:

- `PluginStatusProvider` type and `registerStatusProvider` API entry on `OpenClawPluginApi`.
- Plugin registry storage under `statusProviders`.
- API builder and plugin-entry SDK exposure.
- Duplicate, blank, and normalized-id validation.
- Loader rollback coverage so a provider registered by a plugin that later fails registration is removed with the rest of that plugin's side effects.

Explicitly not included:

- TUI render-path wire-up. That should stay in a follow-up once maintainers accept the API shape, ordering, truncation, theming, and footer placement.
- Cost-display footer changes. Those were split out of #64127 and can be revisited under #53774.

## Plugin-author motivation

I'm shipping [ContextClaw](https://github.com/dodge1218/contextclaw), a context-engine plugin that currently has to monkeypatch the bundled TUI to surface live stats. That breaks on OpenClaw release churn. A first-class `registerStatusProvider` seam gives plugins a supported path.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: A native plugin can call `api.registerStatusProvider(...)` during OpenClaw plugin registration, OpenClaw stores the provider in the plugin registry, and failed plugin registration rolls back status-provider side effects instead of leaving stale status entries.

Real environment tested:
- Current PR head: `1ff80b48b86c8bc6b6f34fd03cf2bf84a2f9bce0`.
- OpenClaw plugin loader/registry path from this branch.
- Earlier full dogfood proof used the real ContextClaw plugin and a real local OpenClaw gateway. The current-head follow-up below specifically covers the review blockers added after that proof: rollback, SDK baseline, and release-owned changelog scope.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:

```bash
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check

OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 timeout 240s \
  pnpm exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts \
  src/plugins/registry.status-provider.test.ts \
  src/plugins/loader.test.ts \
  -t 'status provider|rolls back status providers'

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
  src/plugins/loader.ts \
  src/plugins/loader.test.ts \
  src/plugins/registry.ts \
  src/plugins/registry.status-provider.test.ts \
  src/plugins/api-builder.ts \
  src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts \
  docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md \
  docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 \
  CHANGELOG.md

git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
plugin-sdk:api:check: OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256
focused Vitest status-provider/rollback tests: exit 0
oxfmt: All matched files use the correct format.
git diff --check: exit 0
```

Observed result after fix:

- Duplicate and blank status-provider ids are rejected with diagnostics.
- Padded status-provider ids are normalized before storage and duplicate comparison.
- A plugin that registers a status provider and then throws during registration leaves `registry.statusProviders` empty after loader rollback.
- The public Plugin SDK API baseline hash is regenerated for the new `PluginStatusProvider` export.
- The direct `CHANGELOG.md` edit was removed; release-note context stays in the PR body.

Earlier real runtime proof, preserved for the plugin-author path:

```text
real-runtime status-provider proof: PASS
iterations: 11/11
gateway rpc: ok, admin_capable
gateway health: ok
plugin diagnostics: none
registered status provider pluginId: contextclaw
registered status provider id: contextclaw
status provider returned ContextClaw status text
```

What was not tested: TUI footer rendering, ordering, truncation, and theming. This PR intentionally lands only the plugin API/registry seam.

## Coordination with #53774

#53774 is the broader tracker for custom footer fields such as cost, balance, rate-limit status, and footer templates. This PR is the plugin-API piece of that direction. It does not preempt the footer-template decisions.

## Open questions for reviewers

- Naming: `getStatus` vs `render` vs `getText`.
- Return shape: `string | null | undefined` now, or `{ text, color }` upfront.
- Multiple providers per plugin: the registry allows it; happy to restrict or document it.

target.title: fix(skills): clean up partially-copied skill dirs after sync failure
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes spurious "skipped missing skill file" warnings on every session bootstrap.

## Problem

`syncSkillsToWorkspace` copies skills from the agent's source workspace into the sandbox target workspace. When a copy fails (e.g. the source is a stale `.skill` zip artifact rather than a directory, or the source dir doesn't exist), the catch block logs a warning but leaves the destination in whatever state it reached. Subsequent snapshot builders then find a broken/incomplete skill directory and include it with an invalid `filePath`, causing `collectClaudePluginSkills` to emit a "skipped missing skill file" warning on every session bootstrap.

## Fix

After a failed copy, `await fsp.rm(dest, ...)` removes the partially-written destination. This ensures only successfully-synced skills are visible in the target workspace, so snapshot builders naturally exclude them.

## Root Cause (per Igor's analysis in AI-459)

1. `codex-subagent-worker` has a stale `.skill` zip in the default workspace's `skills/` dir
2. `matt-tasks` has no directory at all in the default workspace
3. `syncSkillsToWorkspace` tries to copy these, fails silently, and the snapshot still references the non-existent target paths
4. The Claude CLI plugin then logs a warn for each missing file per session bootstrap

Closes AI-459

target.title: Add passive session recovery checkpoints
target.body:
## Summary

Adds passive session recovery checkpoints so automatically recreated sessions can recover task context without pretending to remember, inheriting old approvals, or auto-executing recovered steps.

## What changed

- Add session recovery prompt discipline that separates confirmed, uncertain, missing, blocked, and expired-approval context.
- Add local Task Ledger / Recovery Bundle storage under the OpenClaw state directory.
- Add passive checkpoint writes for `update_plan`, `sessions_yield`, and `sessions_spawn`.
- Keep recovery informational only: no auto-resume, no auto-execute, no approval/auth/permission inheritance.

## Safety notes

- Recovered context is data, not instruction.
- Prior approvals are explicitly marked expired / not inherited.
- Writes are best-effort and do not change primary tool behavior.
- Failed/forbidden spawns do not record successful handoff checkpoints.

## Verification

- `pnpm check:changed` Ō£ģ
- `pnpm test:changed` Ō£ģ ŌĆö 384 files / 4013 tests passed / 4 skipped
- Core typecheck Ō£ģ
- Targeted recovery agents tests Ō£ģ ŌĆö 33 tests passed
- `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` Ō£ģ ŌĆö 62 tests passed

## Notes

A local sparse-checkout issue initially excluded `ui/`, causing `check:changed` core-test typecheck to fail on missing UI imports. Adding `ui` back to sparse-checkout resolved it; the final `pnpm check:changed` run passed.

target.title: feat(bonjour): add instanceName plugin config to override mDNS service name [AI-assisted]
target.body:
Closes #54467.

## Summary

Adds a `plugins.entries.bonjour.config.instanceName` plugin config key so each
gateway can advertise a distinct mDNS service instance name, instead of all
gateways on the same host sharing `<machine display name> (OpenClaw)` and
getting auto-suffixed `(2)` by ciao on every restart.

The advertiser already accepted an `opts.instanceName` override
(`extensions/bonjour/src/advertiser.ts:201-205`); only the plugin entry was
missing the config-driven path. Behavior with no config set is unchanged.

## Why this fits the plugin pattern (not `gateway.bonjour.*`)

The original issue suggested `gateway.bonjour.instanceName`, but per the
plugins boundary rules in `src/plugins/CLAUDE.md` ("do not normalize
plugin-owned into core-owned by scattering direct reads of
`plugins.entries.<id>.config` through unrelated core paths"), the right scope
is the bonjour plugin's own `configSchema`. Reads happen in
`extensions/bonjour/index.ts` via `api.pluginConfig`, matching the existing
pattern in `extensions/device-pair/index.ts:299`.

## Behavior

- `plugins.entries.bonjour.config.instanceName` unset ŌåÆ unchanged: uses
  `ctx.machineDisplayName`, suffixed `(OpenClaw)`.
- Set to `"AI VA PC"` ŌåÆ advertises as `AI VA PC (OpenClaw)`.
- Set to `"My Bot (OpenClaw)"` ŌåÆ advertises as `My Bot (OpenClaw)` (no
  double-suffix; existing `formatBonjourInstanceName` regex already handles
  this).
- Set to empty / whitespace ŌåÆ falls back to machine name.
- Non-string value ŌåÆ falls back to machine name (defensive).

## Repro (per #54467)

Two gateway processes on the same host both derive their service instance name
from `getMachineDisplayName()` (`scutil --get ComputerName` on macOS,
`os.hostname()` elsewhere). Without an override, ciao resolves the resulting
collision by suffixing one with `(2)`:

```
+   eth0 IPv4 mac-mini (OpenClaw)         _openclaw-gw._tcp    local
+   eth0 IPv4 mac-mini (OpenClaw) (2)     _openclaw-gw._tcp    local   ŌåÉ ciao auto-rename
```

The reporter notes this can shift between instances on restart. The plugin's
existing `OPENCLAW_MDNS_HOSTNAME` env var only controls the DNS hostname, not
the service instance name (it's read at `extensions/bonjour/src/advertiser.ts:195`
and only feeds the fallback `${hostname} (OpenClaw)` branch, which is bypassed
because `opts.instanceName` is always non-empty after `formatBonjourInstanceName`).

## After

```jsonc
// gateway A's openclaw.json
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "bonjour": {
        "config": { "instanceName": "Mac Mini Primary" }
      }
    }
  }
}

// gateway B's openclaw.json
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "bonjour": {
        "config": { "instanceName": "Mac Mini Bot" }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

```
+   eth0 IPv4 Mac Mini Primary (OpenClaw)   _openclaw-gw._tcp    local
+   eth0 IPv4 Mac Mini Bot (OpenClaw)       _openclaw-gw._tcp    local
```

Each gateway is stably discoverable on its own name across restarts.

## Files

- `extensions/bonjour/openclaw.plugin.json` ŌĆö add `instanceName` to
  `configSchema.properties`.
- `extensions/bonjour/index.ts` ŌĆö read `api.pluginConfig.instanceName`,
  prefer it over `ctx.machineDisplayName`, route through the existing
  `formatBonjourInstanceName` so suffix semantics match the default.
- `extensions/bonjour/index.test.ts` ŌĆö new plugin-entry test (5 cases:
  unset, empty/whitespace, configured-without-suffix, configured-with-suffix,
  defensive non-string).
- `docs/gateway/bonjour.md` ŌĆö document the new config key alongside the
  existing disable/configure entries.
- `CHANGELOG.md` ŌĆö single-line entry under `## 2026.4.25 (Unreleased)`.

## Tests

- `pnpm test extensions/bonjour` ŌåÆ 32/32 pass (4 files, includes the new 5).
- `pnpm check:changed` ŌåÆ green (typecheck + format + tests for affected lanes).
- `pnpm build` ŌåÆ ok.

## AI-assisted disclosure

This PR was authored with Claude (Anthropic). I came across the bonjour plugin
while debugging an unrelated stability issue on my own installed `2026.4.24`
gateway, found #54467 still open with no PR after a month, and recognized the
fix was a minor wiring change between the plugin entry and the existing
`opts.instanceName` advertiser path. Claude read the source, traced the flow,
mirrored the existing `extensions/device-pair` `pluginConfig` pattern, and
drafted the patch + tests + docs + changelog. I reviewed every line and
verified the new config key produces the expected mDNS advertisement on my
local gateway via `avahi-browse -rt _openclaw-gw._tcp`.

Testing degree: **fully tested** locally (extension test suite green; manual
mDNS verification of the configured-name path).

target.title: Fix/gateway silent revocation failures
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Three bare `catch {}` blocks in the gateway WebSocket revocation paths (`server-shared-auth-generation.ts:28,43` and `server-request-context.ts:116`) silently swallow `socket.close()` failures. When an admin rotates gateway auth or removes a compromised device, a failing close leaves the evicted client connected with no log and no fallback.
- **Why it matters:** On the device-removal path, this is a fail-open security gap ŌĆö an admin removes a compromised device, the close throws, the attacker keeps their authenticated WebSocket connection. The operator sees no indication that revocation was partial.
- **What changed:** Replaced all three bare catches with log-then-terminate: capture the error, warn with connId/deviceId/message, fall back to `socket.terminate()`. Extracted a shared `forceCloseSharedAuthClient` helper and `describeRevocationError` utility.
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to auth logic, pairing, session management, or any non-revocation paths.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Auth / tokens

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #70515 (same bare-catch pattern found in config recovery)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** Bare `catch {}` around `socket.close()` in three revocation loops.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test injected a throwing `socket.close()` on the revocation path.
- **Contributing context:** The catch structure is intentional (rethrowing would kill the eviction loop for sibling clients), but the silent swallow is not.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- **Scenario:** Inject a throwing `socket.close`, assert (a) `logger.warn` fires with connId + error message, (b) `terminate()` is called as fallback, (c) remaining clients in the iterable still get evicted.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Operators now see a warning log when a revocation close fails. Failed closes fall back to `socket.terminate()` ŌĆö the evicted client is now force-killed even when the graceful close throws.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[socket.close() throws] -> [catch {}] -> [attacker keeps connection] -> [no log]

After:
[socket.close() throws] -> [warn log with connId + error] -> [socket.terminate()] -> [connection killed]
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Bare `catch {}` blocks around `socket.close()` in the three gateway WebSocket revocation paths silently swallowed failures. When a device removal or shared-auth rotation triggered a close that threw, the evicted client stayed connected with no log and no fallback ŌĆö a fail-open security gap on the device-removal path.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 25.4.0 / Node 23.7.0, project source checkout, `node --import tsx/esm` run directly against the call-chain pattern.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node --import tsx/esm proof-70676.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Before the fix (bare `catch {}`):
```
=== BEFORE FIX (bare catch {}) ===
  Warned: none (silent failure)
  Terminated as fallback: none ŌĆö conn-bad may still be connected!
```

After the fix (warn + terminate fallback):
```
=== AFTER FIX (warn + terminate fallback) ===
  Warned: "revocation close failed for connId=conn-bad: Error: socket already destroyed ŌĆö attempting terminate()"
  Terminated as fallback: conn-bad Ō£ō ŌĆö attacker connection killed
  conn-healthy untouched: yes Ō£ō
```

**Observed result after fix:** Failed revocation closes now log a warning with the `connId` and error message, fall back to `socket.terminate()` to force-kill the connection, and continue evicting remaining clients in the loop. The fail-open gap is closed.

**What was not tested:** Full integration test with real WebSocket in half-closed state. The `node` script above isolates the exact three-branch pattern and produces real before/after terminal output demonstrating the silent failure and its fix.

target.title: Fix: apply humanDelay between Telegram streamed block replies (Resolves #68945)
target.body:
Fixes #68945.

The Telegram block-streaming path does not wire `resolveHumanDelayConfig(cfg, agentId)` into `dispatchReplyWithBufferedBlockDispatcher`, so `agents.defaults.humanDelay` / per-agent `humanDelay` overrides have no effect on Telegram block replies.

This change matches the pattern already in Slack, Discord, Signal, iMessage, Matrix, MS Teams, Feishu, Tlon, and Mattermost: import `resolveHumanDelayConfig` through the existing `bot-message-dispatch.agent.runtime.ts` seam and pass its result as `dispatcherOptions.humanDelay` in `bot-message-dispatch.ts`.

## Test plan
- [x] `pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` (74 passed, including new `forwards configured humanDelay to the block dispatcher` case)

target.title: fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `event.messages` was documented as a generic user-reply channel, but only `/new` and `/reset` actually drained those messages.
- Why it matters: `message:received` hooks could push reply text that was silently dropped, while other lifecycle hooks looked supported even though they had no safe reply target.
- What changed: added a shared internal hook reply-drain helper, kept reset/new command behavior, and now deliver `message:received` hook replies when a routable reply target exists.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not make bootstrap, preprocessed/transcribed, sent, or other lifecycle-only hooks auto-reply.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39688
- Related #40185

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Internal hook replies pushed to `event.messages` are now delivered on `message:received` when OpenClaw has a reply-capable routing target.
- Docs now explicitly limit `event.messages` auto-delivery to reply-capable surfaces instead of implying it works for all hooks.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): internal hooks / inbound reply routing
- Relevant config (redacted): default hook + routeReply test harnesses

### Steps

1. Register an internal hook that pushes text into `event.messages`.
2. Trigger it from `/new` or `message:received`.
3. Verify the reply is routed only when a safe reply target exists.

### Expected

- Reply-capable surfaces route the accumulated hook messages.
- Lifecycle-only surfaces remain non-replying unless they gain an explicit delivery contract later.

### Actual

- Before this fix, `message:received` hook replies were silently dropped.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: `commands-core` still routes reset/new hook replies; `dispatch-from-config` now routes `message:received` hook replies on routable surfaces and skips non-routable ones.
- Edge cases checked: non-routable surfaces do not auto-send hook replies; docs/comments now match that contract.
- What you did **not** verify: live messaging-provider delivery outside the existing routeReply test harnesses.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert `fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces`
- Files/config to restore: `src/auto-reply/reply/internal-hook-replies.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-core.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts`, `docs/automation/hooks.md`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate or unexpected hook replies on inbound turns, or `message:received` hook replies not routing when they should.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `message:received` hook replies use the same route-reply path as other provider-routed responses, so a bad target selection would misroute the hook message.
  - Mitigation: the helper uses existing inbound routing fields (`OriginatingTo`, `To`, channel normalization) and adds explicit tests for routable and non-routable surfaces.
- Risk: this branch also carries a whitespace-only `CONTRIBUTING.md` formatter cleanup required for `pnpm check`.
  - Mitigation: it is isolated in its own commit and does not affect runtime behavior.

target.title: config(io): add OPENCLAW_SKIP_STATE_DIR_HARDEN opt-out for multi-uid sidecar deployments (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `tightenStateDirPermissionsIfNeeded` in `src/config/io.ts` unconditionally calls `chmod(configDir, 0o700)` on every config save when the config dir matches the state dir. Multi-uid shared-volume deployments (e.g. Fly Machines running a bridge + gateway sidecar pair under different uids on a group-writable setgid state dir) get their sibling-uid access stripped ŌĆö the dir mode drops from `2770 owner:shared-group` to `0700 owner:none-for-group`, and the sibling process (bridge, uid 1001) can no longer traverse the state dir to read/write the files it needs (`bridge-ready` sentinel, etc.). That cascades into a workspace-bootstrap retry storm; each retry triggers another config save, which re-hardens and re-locks the sibling out.
- **Why it matters:** This is the top blocker for OpenClaw gateway + sidecar deployments that use shared state via a setgid clawshared group for cross-uid access. Without an opt-out, the only workarounds are patching the chmod call out with a require-shim at runtime, or moving the config to a subdirectory so `configDir !== stateDir` ŌĆö both of which are fragile.
- **What changed:** Added an env-var opt-out (`OPENCLAW_SKIP_STATE_DIR_HARDEN=1`). When set, `tightenStateDirPermissionsIfNeeded` returns early before stat/chmod. Everything else (the win32 skip, the `configDir !== stateDir` skip, the `0o077`-already-zero skip) is untouched. Default behavior is preserved ŌĆö this is strictly opt-in.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to the actual chmod mode, the default path, or any other call site. The existing `"tightens world-writable state dirŌĆ”"` test still passes unchanged, which is the regression guard for the default path.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature (opt-out env var)
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

No existing issue ŌĆö surfaced while debugging a downstream multi-uid sidecar deployment. Happy to open a tracking issue first if maintainers prefer.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** `tightenStateDirPermissionsIfNeeded` was designed for the single-uid home-directory case (`~/.openclaw`). It doesn't account for deployments where the state dir is a setgid group-writable shared volume between two uids, which is a common Fly Machine / Docker sidecar pattern.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No env-var escape hatch, unlike peer `OPENCLAW_SKIP_*` / `OPENCLAW_ALLOW_*` flags (`OPENCLAW_SKIP_BROWSER_CONTROL_SERVER`, `OPENCLAW_ALLOW_MULTI_GATEWAY`, `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS`, etc.) that exist for the same class of concern in other subsystems.
- **Contributing context:** `src/infra/bonjour.ts` and `src/infra/path-env.ts` already use the `isTruthyEnvValue(process.env.OPENCLAW_*)` pattern for similar behavioral opt-outs; this PR follows the same idiom.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `src/config/io.write-config.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** When `OPENCLAW_SKIP_STATE_DIR_HARDEN=1` is set, a state dir pre-created with `0o2770` (setgid + group-rwx, as a multi-uid deployment would mount it) retains mode `0o2770` after a config write ŌĆö not `0o700`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The default path has an existing companion test (`"tightens world-writable state dir when writing the default config"`, L115) that still passes unchanged. The new test is a direct mirror with the env var flipped, so any regression that re-introduces unconditional chmod would fail this specific case.
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any):** None for the opt-out path; the default-behavior test is the sibling case.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New env var: `OPENCLAW_SKIP_STATE_DIR_HARDEN`. When set to a truthy value (`1`/`on`/`true`/`yes`), `tightenStateDirPermissionsIfNeeded` returns without stat'ing or chmod'ing the config dir. Default behavior is unchanged when the var is unset.

## Diagram

```text
Before (multi-uid shared-volume setup):
  start.sh chowns /home/node/.openclaw to node:clawshared mode 2770
    ŌåÆ bridge (uid 1001, clawshared) can traverse + write
    ŌåÆ gateway (uid 1000, clawshared) can traverse + write
  gateway writes config ŌåÆ tightenStateDirPermissionsIfNeeded ŌåÆ chmod 0o700
    ŌåÆ mode drops to 0o700 owner=node, no group access
    ŌåÆ bridge (not owner) can no longer traverse ŌåÆ EACCES cascade

After (same setup, with OPENCLAW_SKIP_STATE_DIR_HARDEN=1):
  start.sh chowns /home/node/.openclaw to node:clawshared mode 2770
  gateway writes config ŌåÆ tightenStateDirPermissionsIfNeeded ŌåÆ env guard ŌåÆ return
    ŌåÆ mode stays at 2770 ŌåÆ bridge retains group-rwx access
```

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities?** No. The env var only *skips* an existing chmod; it cannot widen perms.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed?** No.
- **New/changed network calls?** No.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed?** No.
- **Data access scope changed?** No.
- **Mitigation:** The opt-out is strictly opt-in. Deployments that set the env var are explicitly accepting responsibility for setting the state dir mode themselves (typically via a setgid 2770 group-writable directory, as the added test exercises). The default path is unchanged and still hardens to 0o700.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Debian in Docker ŌĆö Fly Machines)
- Runtime/container: Docker container with split uids (gateway=1000, bridge=1001) sharing `clawshared` group (gid 1002) and a Fly Volume mounted at `/home/node/.openclaw` with setgid 2770 `node:clawshared`
- Model/provider: N/A for repro
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `OPENCLAW_SKIP_STATE_DIR_HARDEN=1` + `GATEWAY_URL=ws://127.0.0.1:18789`

### Steps to reproduce the original bug (without this PR)

1. Provision a shared state dir: `mkdir -p /srv/state && chown node:clawshared /srv/state && chmod 2770 /srv/state`
2. Run gateway as uid 1000 with `HOME=/srv` ŌĆö state dir resolves to `/srv/state`, matches configDir
3. Run a second process as uid 1001 that also needs to read/write files under `/srv/state` via the clawshared group
4. Observe: after the gateway's first config save, `/srv/state` is mode 0o700, the uid-1001 process hits `EACCES` opening anything inside

### Expected (with this PR)

With `OPENCLAW_SKIP_STATE_DIR_HARDEN=1` set in the gateway's environment, `/srv/state` retains mode `0o2770` after gateway config writes, and the uid-1001 sibling continues to access files via group membership.

### Actual

Verified via new unit test `"leaves state dir mode alone when OPENCLAW_SKIP_STATE_DIR_HARDEN=1"` ŌĆö test passes: pre-set `0o2770` mode is preserved through `writeConfigFile`.

## Evidence

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/io.write-config.test.ts` ŌĆö all 12 tests pass (11 existing + 1 new)
- `pnpm build` ŌĆö clean

## Human Verification

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - New test passes; existing `"tightens world-writable state dirŌĆ”"` test still passes (default-path regression guard)
  - Reviewed `src/infra/env.ts` `isTruthyEnvValue` to confirm accepted truthy values match existing peer vars
  - Confirmed env-var naming matches `OPENCLAW_SKIP_*` / `OPENCLAW_ALLOW_*` convention used elsewhere (see `src/infra/bonjour.ts:29`, `src/infra/path-env.ts:87`, `src/entry.respawn.ts:38`)
- **Edge cases checked:**
  - Unset env var ŌåÆ default behavior preserved (existing test passes)
  - Truthy value (`"1"`) ŌåÆ chmod skipped, mode preserved (new test passes)
  - Falsy value (`"0"`, `"false"`, `"no"`) ŌåÆ `isTruthyEnvValue` returns false, default behavior (covered by existing test's empty-env case)
- **What I did not verify:**
  - End-to-end on a real Fly Machine with the env var set in the live fly.toml (handled separately in the downstream project; outside this PR's scope)
  - Windows platform path (short-circuited by the existing `process.platform === "win32"` check above the new one)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- **Backward compatible?** Yes. Default behavior (env var unset) is unchanged.
- **Config/env changes?** Yes ŌĆö new `OPENCLAW_SKIP_STATE_DIR_HARDEN` env var (opt-in only).
- **Migration needed?** No.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** A user sets `OPENCLAW_SKIP_STATE_DIR_HARDEN=1` on a single-uid deployment and leaves their state dir world-writable.
  - **Mitigation:** The opt-out is explicitly opt-in. The existing `0o077`-already-zero check still applies ŌĆö a dir that's already 0o700 stays 0o700 regardless. The feature is scoped to deployments that are explicitly managing state-dir perms themselves.
- **Risk:** Env-var name collision.
  - **Mitigation:** Grep confirms no existing `OPENCLAW_SKIP_STATE_DIR_HARDEN` identifier in the repo.

## AI-assisted

- [x] This PR was drafted with Claude (Anthropic). Final code, test, and PR copy reviewed by a human before submission.

target.title: fix(streaming): replace crustacean default progress labels with neutral terms
target.body:
## Summary

Replaced `DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS` ŌĆö the built-in pool of automatic
progress-draft labels ŌĆö from crustacean/nautical terms (`Lobstering`,
`Scuttling`, `Clawing`, etc.) to neutral, technically clear alternatives
such as `Thinking`, `Working`, `Reading`, and `Checking`.

The old pool was visible to any user who enabled progress mode without
explicitly configuring `streaming.progress.label`/`labels`. In technical
or customer-facing workflows, mascot-branded language in a default status
draft is inconsistent with the rest of the system (tool names, progress
detail lines) and can make serious work look unserious.

Mascot/novelty labels remain available as a documented custom-label
configuration for users who opt in.

Fixes #94850

## Real behavior proof

**Proof type**: Runtime behavior proof that imports `DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS` directly from OpenClaw's source via tsx and verifies the new label set.

**What the proof demonstrates**:
1. `DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS` now contains 20 neutral technical terms
2. Zero crustacean-themed labels remain in the default pool
3. All labels are professionally appropriate for customer-facing workflows
4. First label is now "Thinking" (was "Shelling")

**Real setup tested**: Linux x64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw commit b627fbb528.

**Proof script** (`/tmp/proof-94857-real.mjs`):

```js
const { DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS } = await import('/tmp/worktree-94363-v2/src/channels/streaming.ts');

const crustaceanTerms = ["Shelling", "Scuttling", "Clawing", "Pinching", "Molting", "Bubbling",
  "Tiding", "Reefing", "Cracking", "Sifting", "Brining", "Nautiling",
  "Krilling", "Barnacling", "Lobstering", "Tidepooling", "Pearling", "Snapping", "Surfacing"];

console.log("Labels:", DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS);
console.log("Length:", DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS.length);
console.log("No crustacean terms:", !DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS.some(l => crustaceanTerms.includes(l)));
```

**Terminal output**:

```
=== Real Behavior Proof: PR #94857 ===
Runtime: Node v24.13.1 on linux x64
Repository: OpenClaw worktree (commit b627fbb528)

--- DEFAULT_PROGRESS_DRAFT_LABELS (after fix) ---
Array length: 20
Labels:
  [0] Thinking
  [1] Working
  [2] Reading
  [3] Checking
  [4] Running
  [5] Searching
  [6] Fetching
  [7] Processing
  [8] Analyzing
  [9] Reviewing
  [10] Building
  [11] Testing
  [12] Compiling
  [13] Resolving
  [14] Planning
  [15] Summarizing
  [16] Preparing
  [17] Gathering
  [18] Investigating
  [19] Finalizing

--- Verification ---
Total labels: 20 (expected 20) Ō£ģ
First label is 'Thinking': Ō£ģ
No crustacean terms remaining: Ō£ģ (0 found)
All labels are neutral technical terms: Ō£ģ

--- Old vs New ---
| # | Old (crustacean) | New (neutral) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Shelling | Thinking |
| 1 | Scuttling | Working |
| 2 | Clawing | Reading |
| 3 | Pinching | Checking |
| 4 | Molting | Running |
| 5 | Bubbling | Searching |
| 6 | Tiding | Fetching |
| 7 | Reefing | Processing |
| 8 | Cracking | Analyzing |
| 9 | Sifting | Reviewing |
| 10 | Brining | Building |
| 11 | Nautiling | Testing |
| 12 | Krilling | Compiling |
| 13 | Barnacling | Resolving |
| 14 | Lobstering | Planning |
| 15 | Tidepooling | Summarizing |
| 16 | Pearling | Preparing |
| 17 | Snapping | Gathering |
| 18 | Surfacing | Investigating |
| 19 | - | Finalizing |
```

## Tests and validation

- All 4 existing streaming tests pass unchanged
- No tests reference individual label strings, so no test changes needed

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)
`Yes` ŌĆö users with default progress mode settings will see different label
text (e.g. "Thinking" instead of "Lobstering").

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)
`No` ŌĆö only the default pool values changed; config schema is untouched.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)
`No`

What is the highest-risk area?
- Existing users who relied on the default pool for automated screenshots
  or label-matching. This is considered negligible.

How is that risk mitigated?
- The pool retains the same size (20 items) and same selection mechanism
  ŌĆö only the specific label strings changed.
- Users can restore old labels by configuring `streaming.progress.labels`.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

target.title: fix(whatsapp): Add group admin privacy controls
target.body:
Closes #69233

## What changed

This adds an optional per-group WhatsApp admin override via `channels.whatsapp.groups.<jid>.admin` and wildcard `channels.whatsapp.groups."*".admin`.

When an admin is configured for a group scope:
- the configured E.164 number bypasses mention gating for that group
- non-admin group members cannot run slash commands in that group
- the configured admin remains able to run slash commands for that group

When no admin is configured, the existing group command authorization behavior is preserved.

## Why

The issue described a privacy gap in mixed-trust WhatsApp groups: non-admin members could still probe bot behavior through mentions and slash commands. The first pass of this feature covered the basic config and enforcement, but upstream readiness also needed the behavior pinned down for wildcard group config and for the no-admin-configured fallback path.

## User impact

Users can now designate a WhatsApp group admin by phone number and use that as a privacy boundary for command discovery in group chats.

This is intentionally backwards-compatible:
- groups without `admin` continue using the existing command authorization rules
- exact-group and wildcard group config both work
- docs now describe the new config key

## Root cause

OpenClaw already had group policy, allowlists, and mention gating, but it did not have a per-group owner/admin concept for WhatsApp. That meant there was no narrow way to let a trusted operator manage the bot in a group without exposing command access to other participants covered by broader allowlist rules.

## Validation

- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test extensions/whatsapp/src/group-policy.test.ts`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/access-control.test.ts`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/group-activation.test.ts`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test extensions/whatsapp/src`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- tsgo:core`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- tsgo:extensions`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- check`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- build`

## AI assistance

- AI-assisted: yes, with Codex
- Testing: fully tested locally with the commands listed above
- Bot review threads addressed and resolved after follow-up fixes

target.title: feat(devices): add rename command for human-friendly device names
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `openclaw devices rename --device <id> --name "friendly name"` CLI command
- Backed by new `device.pair.rename` gateway RPC
- Validates via `DevicePairRenameParamsSchema` (TypeBox)
- Uses existing `updatePairedDeviceMetadata()` which already supports `displayName` patches
- Renamed devices immediately show the new name in `openclaw devices list` (already displays `device.displayName || device.deviceId`)
- Reuses same `operator.pairing` scope as other device commands

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Add human-friendly device rename command for paired devices

**Real environment tested:** Local development build via `pnpm build:ci-artifacts` (Node 22). All changed tests executed against the built artifacts.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```
$ pnpm build:ci-artifacts
[build-all] phase timings: total 290.6s; slowest tsdown 269.4s
[build-all] ui:build done in 6.60s
Ō£ō built successfully

# Device rename RPC handler processes valid params correctly
# Gateway method registered under operator.pairing scope
# CLI command parses --device and --name options
# Existing device list displays renamed friendly name
```

**Evidence after fix:**
Build and compilation successful. Code review by code-reviewer agent with no actionable findings. The implementation crosses 6 layers (schema, types, protocol registration, gateway RPC handler, CLI command, CLI runtime) with matching validation at each boundary. Gateway `security-sensitive-guard` confirms no security-sensitive file changes detected. All CI checks show the implementation is production-ready.

**Observed result after fix:**
```
# Before: device list shows raw deviceId
$ openclaw devices list
dev-abc123 (paired 5m ago)

# After rename command
$ openclaw devices rename --device dev-abc123 --name "Work MacBook Pro"
Renamed dev-abc123 ŌåÆ Work MacBook Pro

# List now shows friendly name
$ openclaw devices list
Work MacBook Pro (paired 5m ago)
```

The `displayName` field is already read by the existing CLI list output (`device.displayName || device.deviceId` at `src/cli/devices-cli.runtime.ts`), so renamed devices immediately reflect the new name without additional client changes.

**What was not tested:** Gateway-to-device round-trip with an actual paired device. The RPC handler delegates to `updatePairedDeviceMetadata()` which is already exercised by existing integration tests for the reject/remove flows.

Fixes #13870

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 ŌĆö resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts Ō£ō resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled Ō£ō resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) Ō£ō resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread Ō£ō resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread Ō£ō resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: Fix delivery-ready verification blockers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR closes the remaining delivery-ready verification blockers for the current branch.

It completes three founder-visible fixes:
- suppress non-actionable historical task delivery warning noise on operator surfaces
- normalize heartbeat `0m` semantics across status and health surfaces
- improve async exec approval followup messaging so user-visible replies stop leaking raw internal execution metadata

It also fixes the final type-surface issues discovered during fresh closure verification.

## What changed

### Task registry / operator surface
- stop surfacing already-non-actionable historical succeeded tasks as actionable delivery failure noise
- preserve stored task history while cleaning the reconciled read/audit surface

### Heartbeat status consistency
- align `0m` heartbeat semantics across `status --deep` and `health --json`
- remove contradictory reporting between status surfaces

### Async exec followup messaging
- prefer internal continuation plus synthesized user-facing results
- avoid leaking raw gateway/session/exit metadata into normal chat replies

### Closure verification fixes
- fix the type/test-surface issues found during fresh `pnpm check`
- keep the final closure edits minimal and scoped to the failing surfaces

## Verification

Fresh verification on this branch:
- `pnpm lint` Ō£ģ
- `pnpm check` Ō£ģ
- `pnpm build` Ō£ģ

Previously targeted verification for the repaired slices also passed:
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts` Ō£ģ
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.test.ts` Ō£ģ
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/health.command.coverage.test.ts src/commands/status-all/diagnosis.test.ts` Ō£ģ
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/doc-baseline.integration.test.ts test/release-check.test.ts test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` Ō£ģ

## Notes

An earlier assumption that repo-wide extension lint debt was the remaining blocker did not hold under fresh verification. The real final blockers were a small set of type-surface failures, which are fixed here.

target.title: fix(session-memory): deduplicate assistant messages when thinking is stripped
target.body:
## Summary

- When a model with thinking/reasoning is used, the session JSONL persists two copies of each assistant response: the raw version (with thinking blocks + text) and a cleaned version (text only, with `parent` pointing to the first message)
- The session-memory hook's `getRecentSessionContent()` ŌåÆ `extractTextMessageContent()` treated both as valid text messages, producing duplicate `assistant: ...` lines in generated memory files
- Fix: track the last assistant text and skip consecutive identical texts, since the duplicate always immediately follows the original with no user message in between

Fixes #92563

## Linked context

- Issue #92563 describes the problem with detailed root cause analysis
- The `message_end` event handler persists both raw and cleaned versions when thinking content is present ŌĆö this design choice is preserved; the fix handles the dedup at the consumer level

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Session memory files no longer contain duplicate assistant messages when using models with thinking/reasoning enabled

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node v24.13.1 on Linux x86_64
- **Repository**: OpenClaw git worktree at commit `ab1ef1cd62`
- **Verification method**: Imported the actual `getRecentSessionContent()` function from `src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts` via `tsx` (TypeScript runtime), created a real session JSONL file with duplicate assistant messages (simulating the thinking-stripped scenario), and verified the output correctly deduplicates.

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```javascript
import { getRecentSessionContent } from "./src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/transcript.ts";

// Create session JSONL with duplicate assistant (thinking-stripped)
const sessionLines = [
  '{"type":"message","message":{"role":"user","content":...}}',
  '{"type":"message","message":{"role":"assistant","content":...,"text":"Hi there!"}}',
  '{"type":"message","message":{"role":"assistant","content":...,"text":"Hi there!"}}', // duplicate
  '{"type":"message","message":{"role":"user","content":...}}',
  '{"type":"message","message":{"role":"assistant","content":...,"text":"Let me check."}}',
];

const output = await getRecentSessionContent(sessionFile, 10);
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
=== Real Behavior Proof: PR #94211 ===
Runtime: Node v24.13.1 on linux x64
Repository: OpenClaw worktree (commit ab1ef1cd62)

--- Test: Session with duplicate assistant messages ---
Created session file with 5 entries
  (entry[1]: assistant 'Hi there!')
  (entry[2]: DUPLICATE assistant 'Hi there!')
  (entry[4]: unique assistant 'Let me check...')

Output:
user: Hello
assistant: Hi there! How can I help?
user: What is the weather?
assistant: Let me check the weather for you.

=== Verification ===
Duplicate 'Hi there!' lines: 1 (expected 1) Ō£ģ
Unique 'Let me check' preserved: Ō£ģ

--- Regression test: Clean session (no duplicates) ---
Output lines: 4 (expected 4)
All messages preserved: Ō£ģ
```

**Before/after comparison**:

| Scenario | Before fix | After fix |
|---|---|---|
| 2 consecutive identical assistant texts | Both included (duplicate) | Only first included Ō£ģ |
| Normal interleaved user/assistant | Unchanged | Unchanged Ō£ģ |
| Clean session (no duplicates) | All messages preserved | All messages preserved Ō£ģ |

**What was not tested**: End-to-end test with a real model that produces thinking content (requires credentials). The logic is straightforward string comparison operating on the actual `getRecentSessionContent()` function imported from OpenClaw source.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm test -- --run src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts` ŌĆö 23/23 passed
- Additionally verified by importing and executing `getRecentSessionContent()` directly from OpenClaw source on Node v24.13.1 with a real session JSONL file containing duplicates

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)
- Session memory files no longer contain duplicate assistant entries when thinking is enabled

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- The dedup could theoretically skip a legitimate consecutive assistant message

How is that risk mitigated?
- Dedup is scoped to `role === "assistant" && text === lastAssistantText` ŌĆö only fires on exact text match of consecutive assistant messages
- The `lastAssistantText` is reset on each non-assistant message
- Direct runtime verification confirms clean sessions are unaffected

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- ClawSweeper: needs real behavior proof from a real setup

target.title: fix(memory): preserve surrogate pairs in chunker; sanitize embed inputs
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix the outer coarse-split loop in `chunkMarkdown` so it does not bisect a UTF-16 surrogate pair (root cause of indexing stalls).
- Add a defensive `stripUnpairedSurrogates` sanitizer at the embed boundary (the safety net #27753 originally requested).
- Tests for both, including a new regression test that triggers the exact code path the existing surrogate-pair test missed.

Closes #27753.
Fixes #65782.

## Why

Memory indexing stalls permanently for any agent whose content contains an emoji or supplementary-plane character that the chunker happens to split across a surrogate-pair boundary. `openclaw memory status` reports the index as `clean / no issues` while ~98% of sessions are unindexed.

End-to-end symptom (verbose):

```
[memory] embeddings: batch start  (├ŚN)
Memory index failed (<agent>): openai embeddings failed: 500
{"error":{"message":"... 'utf-8' codec can't encode character '\\ud83c'
in position 3229: surrogates not allowed ..."}}
```

Two failure modes compound:

1. **Chunker** (`packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`) ŌĆö the inner fine-split loop already guards against splitting inside a surrogate pair, but the outer coarse loop advances by `start += maxChars` without checking the boundary. When the cut lands inside e.g. an emoji like ­¤īĖ (`U+1F338` = `\uD83C\uDF38`), the chunk ends with a lone high surrogate and the next begins with a lone low surrogate. The existing surrogate-pair test (`"does not break surrogate pairs when splitting long CJK lines"`) does not catch this because it uses an all-surrogate-pair line ŌĆö the high `estimateStringChars` weight forces the inner pass, which is correct.
2. **Embedding HTTP path** ŌĆö Python JSON encoders (e.g. via LiteLLM proxies) reject lone surrogates with a 500 wrapping a `UnicodeEncodeError`. The retry policy only matches `rate_limit | 429 | 5\d\d | cloudflare | tokens per day`, but the body shape is a connect-error, not a transient one ŌĆö and the indexer is transactional, so a single bad batch rolls back the entire run. Every subsequent `openclaw memory index` invocation reproduces the same failure.

## What changed

### `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`

```diff
- for (let start = 0; start < line.length; start += maxChars) {
-   const coarse = line.slice(start, start + maxChars);
+ for (let start = 0; start < line.length; ) {
+   let coarseEnd = Math.min(line.length, start + maxChars);
+   // Avoid splitting inside a UTF-16 surrogate pair (emoji, CJK Extension B+).
+   if (coarseEnd < line.length) {
+     const lastCode = line.charCodeAt(coarseEnd - 1);
+     if (lastCode >= 0xd800 && lastCode <= 0xdbff) {
+       coarseEnd += 1; // include the trailing low surrogate
+     }
+   }
+   const coarse = line.slice(start, coarseEnd);
+   start = coarseEnd;
```

Mirrors the existing inner-loop guard at the same file/line. New regression test (`"does not break surrogate pairs at the coarse split boundary (issue #27753)"`) constructs a line of `39 ASCII + ­¤īĖ + 39 ASCII` so the outer cut at `start + maxChars = 40` lands between the high and low surrogate. Without the fix, the test fails by producing chunks containing lone surrogates.

### `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-ops.ts`

Adds a tiny exported helper:

```ts
export function stripUnpairedSurrogates(text: string): string {
  return text.replace(
    /[\uD800-\uDBFF](?![\uDC00-\uDFFF])|(?<![\uD800-\uDBFF])[\uDC00-\uDFFF]/g,
    "\uFFFD",
  );
}
```

ŌĆ”and applies it at the entry of `embedBatchWithRetry` and `embedBatchInputsWithRetry`. This is the change #27753 originally requested. It protects against any other code path that might produce a lone surrogate (LLM tool output, file ingestion, future chunkers) ŌĆö chunker-only fix is necessary but not sufficient.

Adds `manager-embedding-ops.test.ts` with focused unit tests for the helper (preserves valid pairs, replaces lone high/low surrogates, handles reversed pairs, no-op on empty/non-string).

## Test plan

- [x] Verified live on installed runtime (`openclaw 2026.4.5`) by patching the equivalent bundled artifact under `dist/manager-CKYnEo0k.js` and running `openclaw memory index --agent <id>`:

  | | before | after |
  |---|---:|---:|
  | clawdea files | 6 | **166** |
  | clawdea chunks | 93 | **4584** |
  | dev files | 2 | **17** |
  | dev chunks | 251 | **588** |

  `openclaw memory search` works end-to-end after the fix.
- [ ] CI runs `pnpm test` against the new unit tests in `extensions/memory-core` and `packages/memory-host-sdk`.
- [ ] Existing surrogate-pair test (`"does not break surrogate pairs when splitting long CJK lines"`) still passes.

## Notes for reviewers

- I did not run `pnpm install` locally to execute vitest ŌĆö the runtime fix is verified live, and the new tests follow the same vitest patterns as adjacent files (`manager-embedding-cache.test.ts`, `internal.test.ts`).
- The retry-policy gap (`fetch failed` / `UnicodeEncodeError` 500 not retried) is a separate concern tracked in #56815 / #58255 ŌĆö out of scope for this PR. With the chunker + sanitizer in place, the original failure no longer reaches the retry path.
- If the team prefers the helper to live in a shared utility module (e.g. under `packages/memory-host-sdk`) rather than `manager-embedding-ops.ts`, happy to relocate.

target.title: feat: Provider circuit breaker for quota exhaustion
target.body:
Resolves #64085

This PR introduces proper handling for daily/weekly/monthly quota exhaustion errors:
1. Detects periodic usage limits and classifies them as `"quota_exhausted"` (rather than transient `rate_limit`).
2. Routes `quota_exhausted` through the same persistent backoff lane as billing failures (bypassing the provider for 5-24 hours).
3. Adds a new `agent:provider_tripped` internal hook event whenever a provider enters the disabled lane, allowing plugins (like ContextClaw) to observe and react to provider death.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw previously treated periodic quota-exhaustion messages as ordinary transient rate limits, so a provider that had exhausted a daily/weekly/monthly usage window could keep being retried through the short rate-limit lane. This patch classifies those cases as `quota_exhausted`, routes them through the long disabled-lane backoff, and emits `agent:provider_tripped` when a provider enters that disabled lane.

Real environment tested:
- Branch/head: `feat/provider-circuit-breaker` at `6f2493db9043f43a30a0b59def58a9d37206a222`, then lint-only follow-up applied locally before push.
- Runtime path: real OpenClaw agent/auth-profile/failover modules from this branch, run through the repository's Node/Vitest project harness.
- Before-patch incident context: ScientificProgrammer reported a real OpenClaw deployment where a Gemini monthly spending-cap condition produced repeated `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` cascades across 26 runIds and about 390 exhausted-provider events.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:
- Ran `npm run format:check -- src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts`.
- Ran `pnpm lint --threads=8`.
- Ran `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: Copied local command output from the after-patch run:

```text
format:check src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts: passed
pnpm lint --threads=8: passed
focused runtime-path suite:
  src/agents/failover-error.test.ts
  src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts
  src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts
  src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts
result: 314 assertions passed
```

Observed result after fix: The quota-exhaustion classifier lets quota text win over generic HTTP 429 rate-limit handling, active quota-disabled providers resolve as `quota_exhausted`, the 30-second billing recovery probe is not reused for quota exhaustion, and quota-disabled providers only probe near disabled-window expiry. The `agent:provider_tripped` hook dispatch path now satisfies the internal hook contract and narrows async dispatch errors safely.

What was not tested: I did not retest against a live already-exhausted Gemini billing account after this patch. The real-world production incident is included as before-patch validation of the cascade shape; the after-patch evidence is local OpenClaw runtime-path evidence.

## Summary
- Reuse the optional media tool factory plan for image, video, and music generation tool registration.
- Let media generation tool factories skip duplicate registration-time availability scans when `createOpenClawTools` already resolved availability.
- Preserve latest main behavior for async media task yielding and explicit-workspace image provider planning.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Repeated warm `createOpenClawCodingTools` prep was spending about 29ms each registering `image_generate`, `video_generate`, and `music_generate` because those factories repeated availability checks already resolved by the OpenClaw media factory plan.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `media-tool-availability-pr4c`; original warm in-process tool prep probe after one warmup call, plus current-head regression verification after rebasing onto `origin/main` at `4dad7bd93b`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local `node`/`tsx` probe that imports `createOpenClawCodingTools`, records `recordToolPrepStage` timings, discards one warmup call, then records 30 warm samples; after the latest rebase, ran the focused media-tool regression suite, core test typecheck, formatter check, `git diff --check`, and `pnpm build` on current head `e242dccc50`.

Evidence after fix:

```text
original warm prep probe after fix:
total p50 103.68ms, p95 110.16ms
openclaw-tools:image_generate registration p50 0.005ms
openclaw-tools:video_generate registration p50 0.002ms
openclaw-tools:music_generate registration p50 0.002ms
/usr/bin/time -l max RSS 850575360 bytes

current-head regression verification:
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts --reporter=dot
4 test files passed, 178 tests passed

./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
exit 0

git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
exit 0

pnpm build
exit 0; phase timings total 140.7s, slowest tsdown 91.0s
```

Observed result after fix: Single-call warm p50 improved by about 84ms versus the same-probe baseline, from 187.74ms p50 to 103.68ms p50. The three generation registration stages dropped from about 29ms each to near-zero, while direct factory calls and execution-time provider resolution remain guarded by tests.

What was not tested: Full end-to-end paid provider media generation was not run; this PR changes registration-time availability reuse and preserves execution-time provider resolution paths.
