target.title: fix: accept valid MCP tool schemas without strict inputSchema.type check
target.body:
## Summary
Fixes MCP server loading failure when tools have `inputSchema` without explicit `type: "object"`.

Closes #63602

## Changes
- `listAllTools` validates each `tools/list` page **once** with `RelaxedListToolsResultSchema` and catalogs **that same validated payload** ŌĆö there is no strict-then-relist flow, so a stateful or hostile server cannot pass validation with one response and have a different response consumed (no validation-to-use gap).
- `RelaxedToolSchema` is **derived from the pinned SDK's own `ToolSchema`** (`ToolSchema.extend` / `ToolSchema.shape.inputSchema.extend`) and differs from it at exactly one point: the root `inputSchema.type` is an optional string instead of `z.literal("object")`. Every other field ŌĆö `outputSchema`, task `execution` metadata, `annotations`, pagination ŌĆö keeps the exact pinned SDK contract, so malformed metadata still rejects the page.
- After every page parse, the SDK's `cacheToolMetadata()` is invoked with the validated tools ŌĆö the same side effect `client.listTools()` runs ŌĆö so `callTool()` enforces output-schema validation and task-required guards identically to the SDK flow. The call is optional (no crash if a future SDK removes the method) and a dedicated test pins the method's existence so a dependency upgrade that drops it fails loudly rather than silently shedding the guards.
- `normalizeInputSchema()` backfills `type: "object"` only when the field is missing; `isValidRootInputSchema()` + the catalog gate skip tools with an explicit non-object root type (e.g. `"string"`, `"array"`) with a `logWarn`. Server `toolCount` reports accepted tools only.

## Root Cause
The MCP SDK v1.29.0 `ToolSchema` validates `inputSchema.type` as `z.literal("object")`. Some MCP servers (especially older SDKs or custom implementations) omit `type` from `inputSchema`, returning `{ properties: { ... } }`, which trips a Zod validation error: `invalid_value expected "object" in inputSchema.type` and crashes the whole catalog.

## v6 notes (current revision)
Addresses the P1 from the latest review (validation-to-use gap, confidence 0.96) by adopting its recommended repair: single-response validation that differs from the pinned SDK `ToolSchema` only at the root `inputSchema.type`, with SDK-equivalent metadata caching, explicit non-object filtering, and pagination preserved.
- The strict-then-relist flow is gone entirely; the request count per page is 1 in all cases.
- New stateful-server regression: a server that returns a benign typeless payload on request 1 and a malicious payload on request 2 gets exactly one `tools/list` request, and only the validated payload is cataloged. **Mutation-checked**: against the previous strict-then-relist implementation this test fails with the catalog containing the drifted (never-strictly-validated) tool ŌĆö reproducing the reported attack ŌĆö and the server log showing two list requests.
- New SDK-seam pin test: `Client.prototype.cacheToolMetadata` must exist, making the documented private-method dependency an explicit, loudly-failing audit point on SDK upgrades (P1 risk #2 from the same review).
- Relaxation-scope unit tests: malformed `outputSchema`, malformed `execution.taskSupport`, and missing `inputSchema` all still reject the page; pagination (`nextCursor`) keeps the SDK contract.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Bundle MCP tool cataloging accepts tools whose root `inputSchema` omits `type` by validating each `tools/list` response once against a schema derived from the pinned SDK contract (relaxed only at the root `inputSchema.type`), backfills `type: "object"`, skips explicit non-object roots, keeps servers with unrelated strict failures rejected, never issues a second list request, and preserves the SDK's `cacheToolMetadata()` side effects for every page.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS from the rebased PR head, Node 25.2.1, pnpm 11.2.2, driving the real bundle MCP runtime (`getOrCreateSessionMcpRuntime` ŌåÆ `getCatalog()` ŌåÆ `callTool()`) against four real out-of-process stdio MCP servers, one of them stateful (different payload per request).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `pnpm test src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test`, `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, and `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1` on both files, plus a live four-server e2e script via `pnpm exec tsx`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  42 passed (42)
pnpm tsgo:core / tsgo:core:test: clean
oxlint / oxfmt --check: clean

e2e (four live stdio servers):
tools: ["legacySdkServer/search_docs","driftServer/benign_typeless_tool"]
servers: {"legacySdkServer":{"toolCount":1},"driftServer":{"toolCount":1}}
rejected: ["badOutputServer","mixedServer"]
driftServer tools/list requests received: 1        // exactly one ŌĆö no second-request window
callTool(search_docs) ŌåÆ McpError -32600: Tool search_docs has an output schema but did not
  return structured content                        // metadata cache populated from the same response
```

Observed result after fix: A `tools/list` payload whose `inputSchema` omits `type` catalogs cleanly with `type: "object"` backfilled; an explicit `type: "array"` sibling is skipped with a logWarn; servers with malformed `outputSchema` (alone or mixed with a repairable tool) stay rejected; a stateful server attempting response drift receives exactly one list request and only its validated payload is cataloged; `callTool()` enforces `outputSchema` for typeless tools exactly as the SDK strict flow would.

What was not tested: A live third-party MCP server connected to an actual model-provider tool call was not tested in this environment.

target.title: feat(plugins): add session followup turn API and gateway-restart extension
target.body:
## Summary

Add a new `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()` method to the plugin runtime, enabling plugins to schedule proactive agent turns for any session ŌĆö including cold sessions with no active user interaction.

> **Supersedes #60951** ŌĆö rebased onto latest main, all CI issues resolved, bug fixes included.

## Problem

Plugins have no way to inject a user-role message into an existing session and trigger an agent turn. The available primitives (`enqueueSystemEvent`, `subagent.run`) either don't initiate turns or create new sessions instead of continuing the original conversation. This blocks use cases like:

- **Post-restart session resume** ŌĆö an agent restarts its own gateway and needs to automatically continue working after the restart completes
- **Model backend switching** ŌĆö an agent swaps inference backends (e.g., switching between local LLMs on a single-GPU workstation) and needs to resume the session on the new model with full context
- **Proactive plugin notifications** ŌĆö a service detects an external event and needs to wake a sleeping session
- **Deferred task completion** ŌĆö a plugin schedules follow-up work after a long-running background operation

## Solution

### Core API: `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()`

A new `followup` namespace on `PluginRuntimeCore` that:

1. **Reconstructs a full `FollowupRun`** from the session store via `buildFollowupRunForSession()` ŌĆö resolving agent identity, workspace, provider/model, session file, and delivery context from the persisted `SessionEntry`.

2. **Enqueues the run** into the existing followup queue infrastructure with preserved queue mode (respects `mode` of any existing queue for the session key, defaults to `"followup"`).

3. **Handles cold-start drain** by detecting whether the session's followup queue is already draining. If not (cold session), creates a fresh followup runner with preloaded session metadata and a no-op typing controller, then kicks the drain loop.

```typescript
// Plugin usage
const enqueued = await api.runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn({
  sessionKey: "agent:my-agent:telegram:direct:12345",
  prompt: "Background task complete. Here are the results...",
  source: "my-plugin",
});
```

### Bundled Extension: `gateway-restart`

A reference implementation that demonstrates the followup turn API:

- **Tool** (`gateway_restart`): Agent calls this to run optional allowlisted pre-commands, write a restart marker (only after commands succeed), then exit the process. Systemd (or equivalent) auto-restarts the gateway.

- **Service** (`gateway-restart-watcher`): On startup, checks for the restart marker. If found, calls `enqueueFollowupTurn()` to resume the original session with a completion message. Marker is deleted only after successful enqueue ŌĆö preserved for retry on failure.

Disabled by default (`enabledByDefault: false`). Opt-in via `plugins.entries.gateway-restart.enabled: true`.

## Files Changed

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.ts` | New: cold-session FollowupRun builder with session store resolution |
| `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.ts` | New: plugin runtime followup namespace with hot/cold session detection |
| `src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts` | Add `followup` type to `PluginRuntimeCore` |
| `src/plugins/runtime/index.ts` | Wire `createRuntimeFollowup()` |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/index.ts` | New: gateway-restart plugin (tool + watcher service) |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/openclaw.plugin.json` | New: plugin manifest |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/package.json` | New: package metadata |
| `test/helpers/plugins/plugin-runtime-mock.ts` | Add `followup` to mock |
| `**/**.test.ts` | 24 unit tests across 3 test files |

## Bug Fixes (vs #60951)

1. **Marker written after pre-commands** ŌĆö prevents stale markers when a pre-command fails
2. **Marker deleted after enqueue confirmed** ŌĆö preserves marker for retry if `enqueueFollowupTurn()` returns false
3. **Hot session guard** ŌĆö `rememberFollowupDrainCallback` only registers for cold sessions; existing hot-session drain runners are not overwritten
4. **Queue mode preservation** ŌĆö `enqueueFollowupRun` respects existing queue mode instead of forcing `"followup"`
5. **Session metadata preload** ŌĆö cold followup runners receive preloaded session store and entry for accounting/refresh paths

## Testing

- 24 unit tests across 3 test files (all passing)
- `pnpm check` passes (tsgo + oxlint + all architecture checks)
- Rebased onto latest main
- End-to-end verified on a local workstation running OpenClaw 2026.4.8 with Gemma 4 26B via llama.cpp

## Use Cases This Enables

This API is the foundation for several plugins currently in development:

- **`model-switch`** ŌĆö seamless switching between local LLMs on single-GPU workstations, with session continuation via `enqueueFollowupTurn()` after the new model is healthy
- **`inference-guard`** ŌĆö priority-aware request scheduling for single-slot inference (heartbeat/cron defer behind user messages)
- **Self-modifying agents** ŌĆö agents that update their own config, restart the gateway, and validate changes automatically

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm check` passes
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run extensions/gateway-restart/index.test.ts` ŌĆö 6 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.test.ts` ŌĆö 9 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.test.ts` ŌĆö 9 tests pass
- [ ] Gateway-restart tool writes marker only after pre-commands succeed
- [ ] Watcher service enqueues followup turn and deletes marker on success
- [ ] Watcher preserves marker on enqueue failure

## AI Disclosure

This PR was developed with AI assistance (Claude Code). Testing level: **fully tested** ŌĆö 24 unit tests, `pnpm check` green, end-to-end verified on local hardware. The author understands all code in this PR and can explain design decisions.

­¤ż¢ Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: CI: report circular dependencies in PRs
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2ŌĆō5 bullets:

- Problem: We did not have a repo-standard way to detect and inspect runtime circular imports across `src`, `extensions`, and `scripts`.
- Why it matters: These cycles can drag large strongly connected components into the runtime graph and make lazy/runtime boundaries harder to preserve.
- What changed: I added `dependency-cruiser` repo tooling, a focused circular-dependency config, a local report script, and a non-blocking CI job that publishes PR summaries plus raw artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): I did not start failing CI on existing cycles and I did not refactor any of the current cycle clusters in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: This is tooling/CI wiring, so the direct guardrail is running the report script and validating the emitted artifacts/summary locally.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: The value here is report generation rather than product behavior. I verified the report output directly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

PRs now get a non-blocking `dependency-circular-report` job that publishes:

- a summary table of cycle-heavy scopes
- a sorted descending directory/module list
- a Mermaid visualization for scope distribution and largest SCC clusters
- uploaded artifacts with raw JSON and full cycle listings

Local development also gets:

- `pnpm deps:circular`
- `pnpm deps:circular:json`
- `pnpm deps:circular:report`

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR] -> [standard CI lanes only] -> [no circular-import visibility]

After:
[PR] -> [dependency-circular-report job] -> [step summary + artifacts]
     -> [sorted scopes/directories + SCC graphs]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: CI now runs `dependency-cruiser` against repo sources to generate reports. This is limited to checked-in code in the Actions workspace and does not expand runtime privileges beyond normal repo CI tooling.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js v25.8.1 local shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default repo config

### Steps

1. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular`
2. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular:report`
3. Inspect `.artifacts/dependency-cruiser/step-summary.md`, `report.md`, `summary.json`, and `cycle-violations.txt`

### Expected

- `dependency-cruiser` finds current runtime cycles using the repo config
- the report script emits a readable sorted summary plus raw artifacts

### Actual

- Current snapshot found `150` cycle violations, `24` cyclic SCCs, and `195` modules participating in cycles
- Top cycle-heavy scopes were `extensions/browser`, `src/agents`, `extensions/mattermost`, `extensions/zalouser`, and `src/cli`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: I ran the circular check locally, ran the report generator locally, and inspected the generated markdown/JSON/text artifacts for sorting, counts, and representative SCC output.
- Edge cases checked: I confirmed type-only imports are excluded, test files are excluded, the report groups by scope and exact directory, and the summary stays non-blocking.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not yet verify the rendered GitHub Actions step summary or artifact download UX inside a live PR run before opening this PR.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No

- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The report could be noisy or too broad for reviewers if the summary is not distilled enough.
  - Mitigation: The PR summary shows only top scopes/directories/SCCs, while the full raw listing stays in artifacts.
- Risk: The report job adds CI time.

target.title: fix(cron): apply defaultAgentId in normalizeCronJobInput applyDefaults
target.body:
## Summary

When cron jobs are created or loaded from SQLite without an explicit `agentId`, the `normalizeCronJobInput` function's `applyDefaults` block does not fill one in. This leaves the job without an agentId reference, causing `resolveEffectiveJobAgentId` to fall back to `defaultAgentId` or `DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` at runtime.

**Fix:** Add `defaultAgentId` to `NormalizeOptions` and apply it in the `applyDefaults` block when `agentId` is missing. Pass `resolveDefaultAgentId(cfg)` from the gateway `cron.add` handler.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/cron/normalize.ts:33` | Add `defaultAgentId` field to `NormalizeOptions` type |
| `src/cron/normalize.ts:578-580` | Apply `defaultAgentId` in `applyDefaults` block |
| `src/gateway/server-methods/cron.ts:427-429` | Pass `defaultAgentId` to `normalizeCronJobCreate` |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Cron jobs created via the Gateway RPC (`cron.add`) without an explicit `agentId` now get one set from the config's default agent.

**Real setup tested:**
- **Runtime**: Node v24.13.1 on Linux x86_64
- **Repository**: OpenClaw git worktree at commit `b0584263a3`
- **Verification method**: Imported the actual `normalizeCronJobInput()` function from `src/cron/normalize.ts` via `tsx` (TypeScript runtime) and verified three scenarios: defaultAgentId applied when agentId is null, explicit agentId preserved, and backward compatibility when no defaultAgentId is provided.

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```javascript
import { normalizeCronJobInput } from "./src/cron/normalize.ts";

// Test 1: No agentId + defaultAgentId ŌåÆ should apply default
normalizeCronJobInput({}, { applyDefaults: true, defaultAgentId: "agent-1" });
// ŌåÆ { agentId: "agent-1", ... }

// Test 2: Explicit agentId should NOT be overridden
normalizeCronJobInput({ agentId: "explicit" }, { applyDefaults: true, defaultAgentId: "agent-1" });
// ŌåÆ { agentId: "explicit", ... }

// Test 3: No defaultAgentId ŌåÆ backward compatible
normalizeCronJobInput({}, { applyDefaults: true });
// ŌåÆ { } (agentId null, unchanged from before fix)
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
$ npx tsx -e "
import { normalizeCronJobInput } from './src/cron/normalize.ts';

const r1 = normalizeCronJobInput({}, { applyDefaults: true, defaultAgentId: 'agent-1' });
console.log('Test 1 - No agentId + defaultAgentId:', r1.agentId === 'agent-1' ? 'Ō£ģ default applied' : 'ŌØī');
console.log('  agentId:', r1.agentId);

const r2 = normalizeCronJobInput({ agentId: 'explicit-agent' }, { applyDefaults: true, defaultAgentId: 'agent-1' });
console.log('Test 2 - Explicit agentId:', r2.agentId === 'explicit-agent' ? 'Ō£ģ preserved' : 'ŌØī');
console.log('  agentId:', r2.agentId);

const r3 = normalizeCronJobInput({}, { applyDefaults: true });
console.log('Test 3 - No defaultAgentId:', r3.agentId == null ? 'Ō£ģ null preserved' : 'ŌØī');
"

Test 1 - No agentId + defaultAgentId: Ō£ģ default applied
  agentId: agent-1
Test 2 - Explicit agentId: Ō£ģ preserved
  agentId: explicit-agent
Test 3 - No defaultAgentId: Ō£ģ null preserved
```

**Before/after comparison**:

| Scenario | Before fix | After fix |
|---|---|---|
| `normalizeCronJobInput({}, { applyDefaults: true, defaultAgentId: "main" })` | `{}` (no agentId) | `{ agentId: "main", ... }` Ō£ģ |
| `normalizeCronJobInput({ agentId: "explicit" }, ...)` | `{ agentId: "explicit", ... }` | `{ agentId: "explicit", ... }` Ō£ģ preserved |
| `normalizeCronJobInput({}, { applyDefaults: true })` | `{}` (no agentId) | `{}` (no agentId) Ō£ģ backward compatible |

**What was not tested**: End-to-end SQLite persistence verification (requires running Gateway with SQLite cron store). The normalization-level behavior is the authoritative validation point since `normalizeCronJobForSqlite` calls `normalizeCronJobInput` with `applyDefaults: true`.

## Test results

```
 PASS  src/cron/normalize.test.ts (53 tests)
 PASS  src/cron/service/ops.test.ts (19 tests)
```

Additionally verified by importing and executing `normalizeCronJobInput()` directly from OpenClaw source on Node v24.13.1.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No`)
- Cron jobs that already had an explicit `agentId` are unaffected
- Cron jobs created without `agentId` now get the default agent set at creation time

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- Jobs that deliberately omit `agentId` to inherit the runtime default might now get a specific agentId persisted

How is that risk mitigated?
- The `defaultAgentId` only applies when `applyDefaults` is true (create/new job paths)
- The `normalizeCronJobPatch` path explicitly uses `applyDefaults: false`
- All 72 relevant cron tests pass
- Direct runtime verification confirms backward compatibility (null defaultAgentId ŌåÆ unchanged behavior)

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- ClawSweeper: needs real behavior proof from a real setup

Closes #93031

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns, but only when OpenClaw can identify one of its bundled SDK tool-compat normalizers.
- Keep the cache internal and conservative: bundled DeepSeek, Gemini, and OpenAI compatibility helpers can reuse cloned normalized `parameters`; custom provider `normalizeToolSchemas` hooks bypass the cache.
- Avoid new public provider plugin contracts and avoid new operator-facing runtime flags. Tool objects, execute closures, approval state, guards, and runtime policy are still rebuilt per turn.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns can avoid redoing bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization when the provider/model/api/schema signature is unchanged. Unknown or custom provider normalizers still run normally and do not participate in the cache.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current PR head `dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a terminal proof with `node --import tsx --input-type=module` against the production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry. The script used the real bundled OpenAI tool-schema normalizer identity through a provider runtime handle, called normalization twice with the same provider/model/api/schema signature and different `execute` closures, then repeated the same pattern with a custom provider normalizer.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the current head:

```json
{
  "head": "dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 0, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "afterSecond": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 1, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": { "bypass": 2, "hit": 0, "miss": 0, "store": 0, "size": 0 },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The bundled OpenAI compatibility path produced one miss and store on the first normalization, then one cache hit on the second normalization while preserving the fresh second `execute` closure. The custom provider path bypassed the cache twice and invoked its normalizer twice, proving the cache remains internal to known bundled compatibility helpers.

What was not tested: Full live-provider latency and a remote LLM turn were not tested in this current-head refresh. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

## Additional validation

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/build.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime-errors.test.ts
[test] passed 4 Vitest shards in 10.92s
Test files: 6 passed
Tests: 124 passed

pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed files>
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD && git diff --check
exit 0
```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Repeated embedded-agent turns reuse bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization for the same provider/model/api/schema signature, while non-bundled/custom provider normalizers still bypass the cache so plugin-specific behavior is not hidden.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout at PR head `b183b3fbe3379f870b8a65e5bf2fe43c8e5aa2ca`; production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry point; real bundled OpenAI normalizer `normalizeOpenAIToolSchemas` via runtime handle; separate custom runtime handle for the non-bundled provider path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran this repo-local Node harness with `node --import tsx --input-type=module`; it imports `./src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts` and `./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts`, resets the provider tool schema cache, normalizes equivalent OpenAI tool schemas twice, then normalizes a custom provider twice.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "head": "b183b3fbe3379f870b8a65e5bf2fe43c8e5aa2ca",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": {
      "bypass": 0,
      "hit": 0,
      "miss": 1,
      "store": 1,
      "size": 1
    },
    "afterSecond": {
      "bypass": 0,
      "hit": 1,
      "miss": 1,
      "store": 1,
      "size": 1
    },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": {
      "bypass": 2,
      "hit": 0,
      "miss": 0,
      "store": 0,
      "size": 0
    },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The second bundled OpenAI normalization increments cache hits from 0 to 1 and preserves the second tool execute closure, while the custom provider path records two bypasses and zero hits/stores. That proves the cache applies only to bundled compatibility normalization and does not reuse plugin-specific custom output.
- What was not tested: Live external provider latency or a paid remote LLM turn. This proof targets the local embedded-agent normalization/cache behavior directly.

target.title: feat(plugins): add optional api.resetSession()
target.body:
## Summary

`main` already has gateway-side session reset support through `performGatewaySessionReset()`.

This PR adds optional plugin-side `api.resetSession(key, reason?)` support on top of the current mainline architecture.

- Adds `PluginResetSessionResult` type and optional `resetSession` on `OpenClawPluginApi`
- Wires `api.resetSession(...)` in the plugin registry only in full registration mode and only when the gateway exposes `sessions.reset`
- Reuses gateway-owned canonicalization and reset resolution (no plugin-side duplicate logic)
- Unifies config snapshot usage in `performGatewaySessionReset` to eliminate config-drift between target resolution and entry lookup
- Adds per-canonical-key in-flight guard in the reset service
- Exports `PluginResetSessionResult` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`

## Scope

This PR:
- adds `PluginResetSessionResult` to `src/plugins/types.ts`
- adds optional `resetSession` to `OpenClawPluginApi`
- adds builder support in `src/plugins/api-builder.ts`
- wires `api.resetSession(...)` in `src/plugins/registry.ts` (full-mode + capability gated)
- adds optional `existingCfg` param to `loadSessionEntry` in `src/gateway/session-utils.ts`
- adds in-flight guard and unified resolution in `src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts`
- exports the result type from `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`
- adds focused plugin contract tests (17 tests)
- refreshes generated plugin SDK API baselines

This PR does not:
- change gateway reset RPC behavior
- broaden session architecture scope beyond the plugin API surface
- revive any stale-branch refactor baggage from the closed #43375

## Behavior

`api.resetSession(key, reason?)`:
- accepts `"new"` or `"reset"` reason values (defaults to `"new"`)
- trims and validates the key
- delegates to gateway-owned `performGatewaySessionReset`
- resolves to a structured plugin result instead of rejecting for operational failures
- absent in setup-only, setup-runtime, and cli-metadata registration modes
- absent when gateway does not expose `sessions.reset`

Success: `{ ok: true, key, sessionId }`
Failure: `{ ok: false, key, code, message }`

## Local Verification

```
pnpm test -- src/plugins/reset-session.test.ts     # 17/17 pass
pnpm test -- src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts      # 77/77 pass
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check                           # OK
pnpm tsgo                                           # no new errors
```

Replaces #43375 (closed, stale branch).

target.title: UI: fix half-block art rendering for UTF-8 QR codes in web chat
target.body:
## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2ŌĆō5 bullets: - Problem: UTF-8 QR codes (using half-block characters Ō¢ĆŌ¢äŌ¢ł) render broken in the web chat dashboard ŌĆö default `
` line-height creates gaps between rows, background/foreground colors don't match the block-character encoding, and the characters don't tile into a scannable image.
- Why it matters: QR codes sent by tools (e.g. Algorand ARC-26 QR generator) are unreadable/unscannable in the web UI, forcing users to copy raw text and render elsewhere.
- What changed: Added `looksLikeHalfBlockArt()` detection in `markdown.ts` that identifies code blocks dominated by half-block Unicode characters, wraps them with a `.half-block-art` CSS class, and applies tight line-height (0.6), black background, white foreground, and slightly larger font-size so the blocks tile seamlessly. CSS added for both `.chat-text` (chat messages) and `.sidebar-markdown` (tool output sidebar), with both light and dark theme variants.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the markdown parsing pipeline, sanitization, or any other code block rendering. Only half-block-art detection and styling were added.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Code blocks containing UTF-8 half-block art (QR codes, box-drawing art) now render with black background, white text, and tight line-height so they display as intended scannable images.
- No change to normal code block rendering.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS, Ubuntu
- Runtime/container: TUI, Browser (web chat dashboard)
- Model/provider: Any (content is tool output)
- Integration/channel (if any): Web UI
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Open the web chat dashboard
2. Have a tool or assistant send a UTF-8 QR code (e.g. via Algorand `generate_algorand_qrcode` or any tool that outputs Unicode block-character QR codes)
3. Observe the code block rendering

### Expected

- QR code renders as a clean, scannable square image with no gaps between rows

### Actual

- Before: rows have visible gaps due to default line-height, background is gray instead of black, characters don't tile ŌĆö QR is unreadable
- After: QR code renders correctly and is scannable

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: UTF-8 QR codes built from half-block characters (Ō¢ĆŌ¢äŌ¢ł) rendered broken in the web chat dashboard ŌĆö the default `
` line-height left gaps between rows and the gray background/foreground did not match the block-character encoding, so the codes were unscannable. This patch detects half-block art, wraps it in a `.half-block-art` class, and applies tight line-height, black background, white foreground, and larger font-size so the blocks tile into a clean scannable image, across both the chat-text and sidebar surfaces in light and dark themes.

Real environment tested: OpenClaw web chat dashboard running locally on macOS and Ubuntu (Node 22), rendering live tool output that emits a UTF-8 QR code (e.g. the Algorand ARC-26 `generate_algorand_qrcode` tool).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Opened the web chat dashboard, had a tool emit a UTF-8 half-block QR code, then viewed it in both the chat message view and the tool-output sidebar, in light and dark themes, and scanned the rendered code with a phone camera.

Evidence after fix:

**Before**



**After**





Observed result after fix: The QR code renders as a clean, gap-free square and scans correctly with a phone camera in both the chat-text and sidebar surfaces; normal code blocks remain unaffected.

What was not tested: Terminal-only (TUI) rendering paths and exhaustive QR color combinations; the detection heuristic may not cover every possible block-art edge case.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: QR code rendering in chat messages and tool output sidebar, both light and dark themes
- Edge cases checked: Normal code blocks unaffected, short block-character snippets (< 10 lines) do not trigger detection, non-QR code blocks render as before
- What you did **not** verify: All possible QRCode coloring; detection heuristic may not cover all edge cases of block art

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the `.half-block-art` CSS rules in `text.css` and `sidebar.css`, and remove the `looksLikeHalfBlockArt()` branch in `markdown.ts`
- Files/config to restore: `ui/src/styles/chat/text.css`, `ui/src/styles/chat/sidebar.css`, `ui/src/ui/markdown.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Normal code blocks accidentally styled as half-block art (false positive detection), or QR codes still rendering with gaps (false negative)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: False positive detection on code blocks that happen to contain many block characters but aren't QR/art
  - Mitigation: Detection requires 60%+ of lines to contain half-block characters AND at least 10 lines ŌĆö unlikely for normal code. Worst case is cosmetic (black bg + tight line-height on a non-QR block).

target.title: fix(agents): hint when unknown tools are skills
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** When a model mistakenly calls a skill name as a tool (e.g. `my-skill`), the error message is a generic "Tool not found" with no guidance, so the model keeps retrying or gives up. This is especially common with weaker/smaller models that struggle to distinguish skills from tools in the system prompt. - **Why it matters:** Skills are invoked differently from tools; without a hint the model has no way to self-correct, leading to repeated failed tool calls that waste tokens and frustrate users ŌĆö particularly on cost-sensitive or lower-capability model configurations. - **What changed:** Register an `afterToolCall` hook in `attempt.ts` that intercepts error tool results, detects unknown-tool errors matching a registered skill name, and rewrites the message to `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` Also extracted `resolveUnknownToolName` helper in `errors.ts` (was inline regex) and exported `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` for the hook to call. - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** `formatAssistantErrorText` signature and all its callers are untouched. No new params threaded through `run.ts`, `payloads.ts`, `subscribe.types.ts`, or lifecycle handlers. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Skills / tool execution ## Linked Issue/PR None ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - When a model calls a skill name as a tool and the skill is registered, the error tool result now says: `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` - When the tool name does not match any registered skill, behavior is unchanged (generic "Tool not found"). ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64) - Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm - Model/provider: Claude (Anthropic) - Relevant config: workspace with registered skills (e.g. `my-skill`, `another-skill`) ### Steps 1. Start an OpenClaw agent session with skills registered 2. Prompt the model to call a skill name as if it were a tool (e.g. "use tool my-skill") 3. Observe the error tool result returned to the model ### Expected - Error message: `Tool "my-skill" not found. "my-skill" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` ### Actual - Confirmed: skill-matching errors get the hint; non-skill unknown tools get the generic error. ## Evidence - [x] Trace/log snippets Manually tested two scenarios: 1. Called skill names (`my-skill`, `another-skill`) as tools -> got skill hint message 2. Called nonexistent name (`banana123`) as tool -> got generic "Tool not found" ## Human Verification (required) - **Verified scenarios:** Both skill-match and non-match unknown tool errors in a live OpenClaw agent session. - **Edge cases checked:** Tool name that does not match any skill; empty skill snapshot; multiple registered skills. - **What you did not verify:** Behavior with the upstream pi-agent-core `immediate` branch change (afterToolCall on prepareToolCall); that path is covered by the pi-agent-core MR. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert commit `e527e3c111`; the `afterToolCall` hook is additive and removing it restores default unknown-tool errors. - Files/config to restore: `errors.ts`, `attempt.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If `setAfterToolCall` is called before the agent session is fully initialized, the hook may not fire; watch for missing skill hints in logs. ## Risks and Mitigations - **Risk:** `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` regex may not match all provider error formats. - **Mitigation:** Uses the same regex patterns already proven in `formatAssistantErrorText`; additional patterns can be added to `resolveUnknownToolName` without changing the hook wiring.

target.title: feat(session): add chat-channel session switching with LRU history retention
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2ŌĆō5 bullets:

- Problem: Chat channels did not support listing or switching between prior sessions under the same SessionKey, and follow-up fixes were needed to preserve switchable transcripts until LRU eviction and restore per-session usage/compaction snapshots correctly.
- Why it matters: Without in-chat session switching, users cannot navigate past conversations from Feishu/DingTalk/Telegram/Discord-style chat surfaces; incorrect transcript/archive or snapshot behavior would also make `/session back`, `/sessions`, and `/status`-adjacent state feel unreliable.
- What changed: Added `/sessions`, `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for chat channels; introduced per-SessionKey LRU `sessionHistory`; preserved switchable transcripts until history eviction; restored token/context/compaction snapshots on switch; documented matching/order/retention behavior; cleaned up reset semantics so `session.historyLimit=0` truly disables switchable history.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change SessionKey routing, does not add cross-SessionKey switching, does not add named sessions/UI picker flows, and does not change channel plugin-specific transport behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #9959
- Related #28168

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Added `/sessions` to list switchable sessions for the current chat SessionKey.
- Added `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for in-chat session switching.
- Current session is included in `/sessions`, marked as `[current]`.
- `/sessions` now shows compact single-line preview rows with session ID prefix and truncated preview text.
- Session switching now restores stored token/context/compaction snapshots for the target session.
- `session.historyLimit` now consistently supports `0` as ŌĆ£disable switchable session historyŌĆØ.
- Transcripts for sessions still in history remain switchable; transcripts are archived when evicted from the LRU history queue.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw dev runtime / source tree
- Model/provider: N/A for core session-switch logic
- Integration/channel (if any): chat-channel command flow; manually validated in a real chat session
- Relevant config (redacted):
  - `session.historyLimit: 5` (default path)
  - `session.historyLimit: 0` (disable-history regression path)

### Steps

1. Start a chat conversation and create multiple sessions with `/new`.
2. Run `/sessions`, then switch via `/session <number>` or `/session back`.
3. Continue creating sessions until the history limit is exceeded.

### Expected

- `/sessions` lists current + recent switchable sessions in LRU order.
- Switching restores the target sessionŌĆÖs stored metadata/snapshots.
- Sessions still present in history remain switchable.
- The oldest session is archived only after LRU eviction.
- With `session.historyLimit=0`, switchable history is disabled and the previous session is archived immediately on reset.

### Actual

- Behavior matches the expected results above in targeted tests and manual validation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `/sessions` output in chat
  - `/session back` restores the previous session
  - switching to a still-retained historical session restores expected context/state
  - LRU eviction archives only the oldest evicted session
  - daily reset path preserves switchable history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` disables switchable history and archives immediately on reset
- Edge cases checked:
  - history boundary behavior at the configured limit
  - selecting current session is a no-op
  - unique-prefix SessionId selection behavior
  - stale/daily-reset session rollover
- What you did **not** verify:
  - every individual chat provider/plugin surface
  - cross-SessionKey switching (out of scope)
  - any UI/card-based session picker (out of scope)

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - revert this PR, or set `session.historyLimit=0` to disable switchable session history
- Files/config to restore:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`
  - `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
  - `src/config/types.base.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - `/sessions` shows stale/incorrect ordering
  - switched sessions lose expected token/context/compaction snapshot state
  - transcripts are archived too early while still present in history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` does not disable switchable history

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: LRU eviction/archive timing could regress and archive a session that should still remain switchable.
  - Mitigation: Added/updated targeted tests covering retained-history vs evicted-history behavior.

- Risk: Snapshot restore could leave token/context/compaction state inconsistent after switching.
  - Mitigation: Added lifecycle tests covering restore and missing-metadata fallback behavior.

- Risk: Docs could drift from implementation again as follow-up fixes land.
  - Mitigation: Updated `docs/design/chat-session-switch.md` to match the current implementation semantics in this PR.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted: Yes

I used an AI assistant for code review, doc cleanup, and targeted test updates. I manually reviewed the final diff, understood the changes, and verified the session-switch flows myself.

target.title: feat(embedded-runner): expose prep stage timings
target.body:
## Summary
- Expose embedded runner prep stage snapshots on `EmbeddedRunAttemptResult`.
- Include the same prep stage summary on the `agent_end` hook event for plugin observability.
- Add focused coverage for attempt result and hook event prep stage propagation.
- Document the new optional hook field. No `CHANGELOG.md` change is kept.

## Current upstream status
Latest `origin/main` already has May embedded-runner/subagent performance work and trace logging for startup stages. It still does not expose the attempt prep stage summary to `agent_end` hook observers. This PR is narrowed to that missing diagnostics surface so plugins can collect stage timing evidence without patching core locally.

## Motivation
OpenClaw records detailed embedded runner prep stages internally, but that timing data is not available to plugin hooks. When embedded runs feel slow, plugin authors and operators cannot tell whether time is going into tool bundling, system prompt construction, session resource loading, or stream setup without adding local instrumentation.

Exposing the existing stage snapshot gives downstream integrations a low-risk way to collect latency evidence before proposing behavior-changing optimizations.

## Implementation notes
- Reuses the existing `createEmbeddedRunStageTracker()` / `prepStages.snapshot()` path.
- Keeps all new fields optional.
- Uses public structural plugin hook types instead of exporting runner internals through the plugin API.
- Does not change tool bundling, system prompt construction, stream setup, model calls, or runner behavior.
- Documents `agent_end.prepStages` as optional diagnostic telemetry whose stage labels may grow over time.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Embedded runner prep stage timings are visible to a real non-bundled plugin's `agent_end` hook without changing the embedded run outcome.

Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw source checkout, current PR head `8d7ef409272853778bd234da21e777ac880216a6`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, temporary local plugin loaded through `plugins.load.paths` with `hooks.allowConversationAccess=true`, typed lifecycle hook registration via `api.on("agent_end", ...)`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --local --json --session-key agent:default:prep-proof`, and the repo mock OpenAI-compatible server `scripts/e2e/mock-openai-server.mjs`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the mock OpenAI server with `SUCCESS_MARKER=OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, ran a local embedded agent turn against a temporary config, and had the temporary plugin write the real `agent_end` event to JSONL. The proof script asserted both the CLI result and plugin hook payload.

Fresh current-head verification:

```text
node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-embedded-agent.config.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts -t "exposes embedded prep stages" --reporter=dot
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 58 skipped (59)
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the current-head local OpenClaw CLI run and typed non-bundled plugin `agent_end` hook JSONL assertion.

```json
{
  "head": "8d7ef409272853778bd234da21e777ac880216a6",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK",
  "hookSuccess": true,
  "hookHasPrepStages": true,
  "hookTotalMs": 143,
  "hookStageNames": [
    "workspace-sandbox",
    "skills",
    "core-plugin-tools",
    "bootstrap-context",
    "bundle-tools",
    "system-prompt",
    "session-resource-loader",
    "agent-session",
    "stream-setup"
  ],
  "proofDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78381-proof-current.Xjp9Fj"
}
```

Observed result after fix: A standalone embedded `openclaw agent --local` run completed successfully against the mock OpenAI server, returned `OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, and the temporary plugin's real typed `agent_end` handler observed `event.prepStages` with total setup time and expected prep stages.

What was not tested: A live paid external model/provider run was not run. The proof used OpenClaw's deterministic local OpenAI-compatible response server to avoid provider latency/cost while still exercising the real embedded runner, local CLI, plugin loader, and typed `agent_end` hook dispatch.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- Risk: plugin authors might treat stage names as permanent.
- Mitigation: docs describe `prepStages` as optional diagnostics and allow stage labels to grow over time.

target.title: fix(memory): prove live manager recovery after CLI reindex
target.body:
Closes #91167
Related #90453
Related #92943

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes the proof gap for #91167 where a running gateway/default memory manager could be suspected of staying stuck on `index metadata is missing` after a separate CLI/status reindex rebuilt the same SQLite store.

## Why This Change Was Made

Current `main` already publishes full memory reindexes into the canonical SQLite database instead of swapping the whole DB file, and the live manager refreshes index identity from that handle on status/search reads. This PR keeps the source change intentionally small by adding the missing regression coverage for that manager-owned live/CLI publication contract, without duplicating the broader in-flight reindex barrier work in #90453.

## User Impact

Users and maintainers get a pinned regression proving that the live gateway manager can recover visibility after an external CLI reindex without requiring a gateway restart for this #91167 path.

## Evidence

Real behavior proof:

- Behavior addressed: A live default memory manager with a missing index identity observes a separate `purpose: "cli"` forced reindex on the same SQLite store, refreshes to a valid index identity, and returns the newly indexed memory content without reopening the live manager.
- Real environment tested: local source checkout at `541917a3fd90`, Node `v24.15.0`, macOS host, real `node:sqlite` manager path with temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, built-in memory backend, FTS-only provider mode (`provider: "none"`) so no external credentials are required.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts
pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
RUN  v4.1.8 /Users/psx849261680/Desktop/projects/openclaw-91167

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  3 passed (3)
Duration  35.12s
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 93.46s

git diff --check: clean
oxfmt --check: All matched files use the correct format.
oxlint: clean
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
```

- Observed result after fix: The new regression deletes `memory_index_meta_v1` from an already-open live manager, runs a separate CLI-purpose manager `sync({ reason: "cli", force: true })` after changing the memory note, then verifies the original live manager reports `indexIdentity.status === "valid"` and `search("beta repaired")` returns the newly indexed `Beta topic` snippet.
- What was not tested: full Gateway process with a real tool invocation, remote embedding provider, large-index latency, and concurrent stress during an in-flight reindex; #90453 owns the in-flight reindex race/barrier proof.

Regression coverage added:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts` now covers the live-manager plus separate CLI-manager recovery path for #91167.

target.title: fix(feishu): add pagination support for drive list and info
target.body:
Closes #93928

## What Problem This Solves

Feishu drive list and info lacked pagination support ŌĆö when a folder had more than 10 items, `list` only returned the first page and `info` returned `File not found` for items beyond the first page.

## Why This Change Was Made

Added `page_size`, `page_token`, and `all` parameters to the drive schema, and implemented pagination loops in `listFolder` and `getFileInfo` with infinite-loop protection (`MAX_LIST_PAGES = 100`).

## Evidence

```text
$ npx tsx -e 'import { FeishuDriveSchema } from "./extensions/feishu/src/drive-schema.ts"; ...'

schemas with pagination: 3
page_size: true  page_token: true  all: true
page_size: true  page_token: true  all: false
page_size: true  page_token: true  all: false
MAX_LIST_PAGES = 100 (infinite loop protection)
```

target.title: fix: use single-char ellipsis in commitments truncate() to prevent table misalignment
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

In `openclaw commitments`, the `truncate()` helper reserved only 1 character for the overflow indicator (`maxChars - 1`) but appended a 3-character ASCII `...`, making the truncated string `maxChars + 2` characters wide. This caused `padEnd`-based table columns to misalign ŌĆö truncated rows pushed past the column boundary, breaking visual alignment of the commitments table output.

**Root cause:** `src/commands/commitments.ts` ŌĆö `truncate()` appended 3 chars of ellipsis after slicing to `maxChars - 1`, producing `maxChars + 2` total width instead of the expected `maxChars`.

**Fix:** Replace `...` with single Unicode ellipsis `\u2026`, matching the identical pattern already used in `flows.ts`, `tasks.ts`, and `onboard-skills.ts`.

## Evidence

### Fix (commitments.ts, line 27)

```diff
 function truncate(value: string, maxChars: string): string {
-  return value.length <= maxChars ? value : `${value.slice(0, maxChars - 1)}...`;
+  return value.length <= maxChars ? value : `${value.slice(0, maxChars - 1)}\u2026`;
 }
```

### Consistency with existing codebase

The single-char Unicode ellipsis is already the repo convention for truncation helpers:

| File | Ellipsis |
|------|----------|
| `src/cli/flows.ts` | `\u2026` (single unicode) |
| `src/cli/tasks.ts` | `\u2026` (single unicode) |
| `src/cli/onboard-skills.ts` | `\u2026` (single unicode) |
| `src/commands/commitments.ts` (BEFORE) | `...` (3-char ASCII) |
| `src/commands/commitments.ts` (AFTER) | `\u2026` (single unicode) |

### Impact

- Single-character change; zero behavioral difference for text content
- `padEnd(maxChars)` now produces correctly aligned columns since truncated values are exactly `maxChars` wide
- Consistent with the existing truncation convention used across the CLI

­¤ż¢ Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(active-memory): filter assistant chitchat from recall summary (fixes #84034)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The active-memory recall subagent sometimes returns assistant-style chitchat (greetings, help-offer boilerplate, "message got cut off" text, date/time announcements) instead of `NONE` or a valid memory summary. This chitchat passes through `normalizeActiveSummary()` and gets injected into the runtime context via the `<active_memory_plugin>` tag, contaminating the main prompt with irrelevant assistant-language.

Observed contamination variants include:
- `µé©ÕźĮ’╝üĶ»ĘķŚ«µ£ēõ╗Ćõ╣łÕÅ»õ╗źÕĖ«ÕŖ®µé©ńÜäÕÉŚ’╝¤Õ”éµ×£µé©µ£ēõ╗╗õĮĢķŚ«ķóśµł¢ķ£ĆĶ”üÕĖ«ÕŖ®’╝īĶ»ĘķÜÅµŚČÕæŖĶ»ēµłæŃĆé`
- `It seems like your message got cut off. Could you please provide more details...`
- `Hello! It looks like your message didn't come through...`
- `ÕĮōÕēŹµŚźµ£¤ÕÆīµŚČķŚ┤µś»...Õ”éµ×£µé©ķ£ĆĶ”üõ╗╗õĮĢÕĖ«ÕŖ®...`
- `µé©ÕźĮÕāÅµ▓Īµ£ēĶ»┤Õ«īµé©ńÜäµČłµü»`
- `Õ”éµ×£ķ£ĆĶ”üÕĖ«ÕŖ®’╝īĶ»ĘĶüöń│╗Õ«óµ£Ź`

This is prompt contamination ŌĆö assistant-language enters untrusted runtime context and can confuse downstream reasoning.

## Changes

Add `CHITCHAT_PATTERNS` regex array and `isChitchatSummary()` function in `extensions/active-memory/index.ts`. The function requires at least 2 pattern matches to avoid false positives on legitimate memory content (e.g., a summary mentioning "help" in a factual context). Integrated into `normalizeActiveSummary()` so chitchat is filtered before reaching `buildMetadata()`.

Patterns detect:
- Greetings + help offers (EN/CN)
- Message-cut-off / didn't-come-through boilerplate
- Please-provide / please-tell requests
- Date/time announcements
- Looks-like / seems-like + negative constructions
- Current-model + help-offer (EN/CN)
- Unfinished message / didn't-finish variants
- If-need-help + contact-support requests
- Feel-free-to-ask boilerplate

- [x] AI-assisted (Hermes Agent)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Active-memory recall subagent returning assistant chitchat instead of NONE gets filtered before injection into `<active_memory_plugin>` context. Added missing patterns for date/time help-offer, Chinese no-question/request, and unfinished-message variants that were leaking through the 2-match threshold.
- **Environment tested:** macOS 26.4.1, Node v22.19, OpenClaw PR branch `fix/active-memory-chitchat-filter`
- **Steps run after the patch:** Exercised `isChitchatSummary()` from the production source with all observed contamination variants from the issue (including 5 new variants), plus legitimate memory content to verify no false positives. Ran full test suite (166 tests).
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```
  $ npx tsx -e "
  import { testing } from './extensions/active-memory/index.ts';
  const { isChitchatSummary } = testing;
  const cases = [
    { input: 'µé©ÕźĮ’╝üĶ»ĘķŚ«µ£ēõ╗Ćõ╣łÕÅ»õ╗źÕĖ«ÕŖ®µé©ńÜäÕÉŚ’╝¤Õ”éµ×£µé©µ£ēõ╗╗õĮĢķŚ«ķóśµł¢ķ£ĆĶ”üÕĖ«ÕŖ®’╝īĶ»ĘķÜÅµŚČÕæŖĶ»ēµłæŃĆé', expected: true, label: 'CN greeting+help offer' },
    { input: 'ÕĮōÕēŹµ©ĪÕ×ŗµś» ClaudeŃĆéÕ”éµ×£µé©ķ£ĆĶ”üÕĖ«ÕŖ®µł¢µ£ēÕģČõ╗¢ķŚ«ķóś’╝īĶ»ĘÕæŖĶ»ēµłæŃĆé', expected: true, label: 'CN model metadata help' },
    { input: 'Memory of user preferences: dark mode enabled, language Chinese.', expected: false, label: 'Legitimate memory summary' },
    { input: 'Looks like your message didn't come through. Could you please provide more details?', expected: true, label: 'EN message-cut-off+please-provide' },
    { input: 'The user prefers dark mode and uses Chinese language.', expected: false, label: 'Factual summary' },
    { input: 'Hello! How can I help you today?', expected: true, label: 'EN greeting+help' },
    { input: 'ÕĮōÕēŹµŚźµ£¤ÕÆīµŚČķŚ┤µś» 2026-06-23', expected: false, label: 'Date only, no help offer' },
    { input: 'ÕĮōÕēŹµŚźµ£¤ÕÆīµŚČķŚ┤µś» 2026-06-23’╝īÕ”éµ×£µé©ķ£ĆĶ”üõ╗╗õĮĢÕĖ«ÕŖ®’╝īĶ»ĘķÜÅµŚČÕæŖĶ»ēµłæŃĆé', expected: true, label: 'Date+time help-offer (new)' },
    { input: 'Ķ»ĘķÜÅµŚČÕæŖĶ»ēµłæµé©ńÜäÕüÅÕźĮÕÆīĶ«ŠńĮ«ŃĆé', expected: true, label: 'CN feel-free-to-ask (new)' },
    { input: 'µé©ÕźĮÕāÅµ▓Īµ£ēĶ»┤Õ«īµé©ńÜäµČłµü»ŃĆé', expected: true, label: 'CN unfinished message (new)' },
    { input: 'I didn't finish your message, could you repeat?', expected: true, label: 'EN unfinished message (new)' },
    { input: 'Õ”éµ×£ķ£ĆĶ”üÕĖ«ÕŖ®’╝īĶ»ĘĶüöń│╗Õ«óµ£ŹŃĆé', expected: true, label: 'CN if-need-help (new)' },
  ];
  let allPass = true;
  for (const c of cases) {
    const result = isChitchatSummary(c.input);
    const status = result === c.expected ? 'PASS' : 'FAIL';
    if (status === 'FAIL') allPass = false;
    console.log(status + ' ' + c.label + ' -> ' + result);
  }
  console.log(allPass ? 'ALL PASSED' : 'SOME FAILED');
  "
  PASS: CN greeting+help offer -> true
  PASS: CN model metadata help -> true
  PASS: Legitimate memory summary -> false
  PASS: EN message-cut-off+please-provide -> true
  PASS: Factual summary -> false
  PASS: EN greeting+help -> true
  PASS: Date only, no help offer -> false
  PASS: Date+time help-offer (new) -> true
  PASS: CN feel-free-to-ask (new) -> true
  PASS: CN unfinished message (new) -> true
  PASS: EN unfinished message (new) -> true
  PASS: CN if-need-help (new) -> true
  ALL PASSED

  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts
   Ō£ō  extension-active-memory  extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts (166 tests) 12516ms
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  166 passed (166)

  $ grep -n "CHITCHAT_PATTERNS\\|isChitchatSummary" extensions/active-memory/index.ts
  153:const CHITCHAT_PATTERNS = [
  2552:function isChitchatSummary(value: string): boolean {
  2556:    isChitchatSummary(singleLine)
  3767:  isChitchatSummary,
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** All 12 chitchat detection cases pass (7 original + 5 new variants). The production `isChitchatSummary()` function correctly distinguishes assistant chitchat (CN/EN greetings, help offers, message-cut-off boilerplate, model-metadata chatter, date/time help-offer, unfinished message, feel-free-to-ask, if-need-help+contact) from legitimate memory content. The 2-pattern minimum prevents false positives on summaries that mention "help" in a factual context. Full test suite (166 tests) passes.
- **What was not tested:** Live active-memory recall with real subagent ŌĆö the fix is purely in the post-processing filter, verified through source-level exercise of the production function.

target.title: # PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)
target.body:
# PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)

## Summary

This PR introduces a stricter, policy-driven filesystem access layer (**PathGuard**) for OpenClaw agent filesystem tools, including local media (image/pdf) loaders.

It closes key security and consistency gaps by:
- canonicalizing requested paths before evaluation (realpath-aware, ENOENT-safe),
- preventing traversal and symlink/junction escapes,
- enforcing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths` consistently for classic filesystem tools **and** local media loads,
- preserving **deny-overrides-allow** semantics,
- anchoring workspace-relative policy entries to the workspace root (and re-anchoring after realpath),
- treating `allowedPaths: []` as an explicit **deny-all**,
- supporting absolute glob policy entries (via minimatch) alongside canonical absolute literals,
- returning structured, remediation-focused policy denial tool results (`policy_denied`) when access is blocked,
- and enforcing a monotonic filesystem policy ceiling for spawned subagents (global + agent + subagent defaults + spawn-time tightening), plumbed end-to-end into the spawned run.

**Important scope note:**

R: ŌĆ£This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.ŌĆØ

Remote URLs (`http(s)://`) and inline `data:` inputs are not treated as filesystem paths and are outside this layer.

---

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Tests

---

## Linked Issues

- Closes #39672
- Related #8719

---

## Problem Statement (What was wrong before)

Before this PR, filesystem tool enforcement had multiple gaps:

1) **Path canonicalization and symlink escape handling were fragmented**
- Traversal protections and ŌĆ£what path are we actually touching?ŌĆØ logic were spread across tools/flows.
- Non-existent paths (ENOENT) were tricky: naive realpath calls fail and can allow later-creation escapes.

2) **Policy matching was inconsistent**
- Absolute vs relative entries could be evaluated in different ŌĆ£path spacesŌĆØ.
- Glob vs non-glob entries could diverge in behavior, especially when ŌĆ£rootsŌĆØ filtering was used as an approximation.

3) **Media tools could diverge from file tool policy**
- Local file references inside image/pdf flows could reopen outside-workspace reads if not enforced per resolved file.

4) **Sandbox and host flows lacked a unified enforcement story**
- Enforcement needed to be identical regardless of whether the run is sandboxed or host-based.

5) **Spawned subagents did not have a guaranteed filesystem policy ceiling**
- Subagents needed an explicit, monotonic ceiling that could not be loosened at spawn time.

---

## What Changed (Design + Implementation)

### 1) Core PathGuard enforcement (`src/security/path-guard.ts`)

PathGuard implements:

- **Canonical resolution for existing paths** via `fs.realpath`.
- **Nearest-existing-parent resolution** for ENOENT paths (prevents ŌĆ£create later via symlinkŌĆØ bypass).
- **Symlink/junction-aware comparisons** in canonical path space.
- **Deny precedence**: deny rules always override allow rules.
- **Workspace-only boundary**: `workspaceOnly` blocks outside-workspace access.
- **Relative entry anchoring**: workspace-relative policy entries are anchored to the workspace root, and re-anchored after realpath resolution.
- **Empty allowlists**: `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit deny-all.
- **Absolute policy entries**:
  - absolute **literals** are canonicalized before comparison,
  - absolute **globs** are supported (matched against canonical real paths) without attempting to realpath the pattern.
- **Glob detection** via `Minimatch(...).hasMagic()` (brace/extglob supported).

### 2) Filesystem tool integration (read/write/edit/apply_patch-style)

- Tool FS config is resolved through the tool/fs policy configuration and enforced using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`.
- Wrappers normalize args and run PathGuard checks on resolved paths.
- `apply_patch` is explicitly guarded by parsing patch hunks and enforcing FS policy on each patch target path (not just args.path/file_path).

### 3) Structured `policy_denied` tool result (model guidance)

When PathGuard denies access, tools return a structured tool result (instead of only throwing a raw error) that:
- states the restriction is system-enforced and cannot be bypassed,
- describes what the tool attempted to do,
- provides actionable remediation options,
- and explicitly points to config keys that can be changed to grant access.

This improves model behavior: fewer useless retries, more direct user remediation.

### 4) Media tool integration (image/pdf local paths)

Local media loads are now policy-checked on the resolved file path:
- `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`
- `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.ts`

Key invariant:
- enforcement is per resolved path using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`, not via root-prefix approximations.

### 5) Sandbox behavior

For local media loads in sandbox mode:
- PathGuard runs in sandbox flows too,
- policy root uses `sandbox.root` when sandboxed,
- otherwise `workspaceDir` is used.

### 6) Subagent filesystem policy ceiling (spawn-time tightening + runtime enforcement)

Goal: spawned subagents must never receive broader filesystem access than the configured ceiling.

Policy sources (monotonic tightening):
- Global: `tools.fs`
- Per-agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- Subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- Sessions-spawn defaults/ceiling: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (applied when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`)
- Spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge semantics:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: enforced as a ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

Persistence + plumbing:
- Effective policy is persisted on the spawned child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) via `sessions.patch`.
- Gateway plumbs it into the child run as ingress field `toolFsPolicy`.
- Gateway session resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve `spawnedToolFsPolicy` (no widening after reset).
- Embedded runner passes it into tool construction.
- `createOpenClawCodingTools` accepts an `fsPolicy` override and uses it as the effective policy for that run.
- Nested spawns inherit the parent sessionŌĆÖs `spawnedToolFsPolicy` ceiling before applying config/tool-call tightening.

---

## Configuration (for contributors)

### Filesystem policy knobs

- `tools.fs.workspaceOnly: boolean`
- `tools.fs.allowedPaths: string[] | undefined` (empty array means deny-all)
- `tools.fs.denyPaths: string[] | undefined`

Subagent ceiling:
- `tools.subagents.fs.{workspaceOnly, allowedPaths, denyPaths}`

Sessions-spawn defaults + spawn-time tightening:
- `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (config default ceiling/tightening)
- `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (tool arg tightening; can only further restrict)

Notes:
- Deny always wins.
- Relative entries are workspace-anchored.
- Policies are evaluated against canonicalized real paths.

---

## Explicit Non-Goals / Out of Scope

- Exec sandboxing is not implemented here.
  R: ŌĆ£This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.ŌĆØ
- No shell argument parsing.
- Remote source governance is out-of-scope.

---

## Implementation Notes (for contributors)

### Policy evaluation edge cases covered

- `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit **deny-all** (not ŌĆ£no restrictionŌĆØ).
- Relative literal entries are **re-anchored after realpath**: if a workspace-relative entry canonicalizes outside the workspace (e.g. it points at a symlink to an external dir), it **does not** match outside-workspace targets.
- Absolute glob entries support symlink-alias authoring by **canonicalizing the non-glob prefix** before matching.
  - When rebuilding the rewritten pattern, the implementation **normalizes slash joining** so root-anchored globs stay semantically equivalent (e.g. `"/**/*.pem"` must not become `"//**/*.pem"`).

### Tool behavior on denial (model guidance)

When a filesystem access is blocked by PathGuard, tools return a structured `policy_denied` result (English text + structured fields) so models:
- do not attempt ŌĆ£bypass retriesŌĆØ,
- clearly communicate what was attempted,
- present actionable remediation options,
- and point to config keys (`tools.fs.*`) for operators to change if access is intended.

### Subagent filesystem ceiling (monotonic tightening)

Effective subagent policy is computed as a ceiling and is monotonic (cannot be widened by spawn calls):
- global: `tools.fs`
- agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- sessions-spawn defaults: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`
- spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` / tool arg `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge intent:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

The effective ceiling is persisted on the child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) and forwarded into the embedded runner (`toolFsPolicy` ŌåÆ `createOpenClawCodingTools({ fsPolicy })`).

---

## Verification

- CI checks and tests pass (per latest runs).
- Added/maintained coverage ensuring `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` is applied as a default tightening when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`.
- Verified critical denial scenarios: traversal, symlink/junction escapes, ENOENT parent-walk, deny precedence, empty allowlist deny-all, sandbox media enforcement, apply_patch enforcement, and subagent ceiling propagation.
- Verified monotonic nested spawning: a tightened subagent cannot spawn a child with broader FS access (parent `spawnedToolFsPolicy` is inherited).
- Verified gateway resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve the spawned FS ceiling (no widening after reset).

---

## Reviewer / Contributor Notes (Do not regress these invariants)

- Do not replace per-path enforcement with root-prefix approximations for glob policies.
- Keep deny-overrides-allow.
- Keep relative policy entries anchored to workspace (including after realpath).
- Keep sandbox and host local-file enforcement aligned.
- Keep local filesystem policy and remote-source policy separate concepts.
- Keep subagent policy tightening monotonic (no privilege escalation vs ceiling).
- Remember: exec is outside this enforcement layer.

---

## Rollback / Recovery

- Disable restrictions by setting `workspaceOnly: false` and clearing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths`.
- No data migration required.
