target.title: fix(memory-core): honor private-network settings for OpenAI-compatible embeddings
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes the current review/test-only blockers on PR #94344 without changing the runtime SSRF contract from the PR head.
- Updates the added SSRF tests to match the exported `LookupFn` overload shape.
- Updates the memory config docs to describe exact configured `baseUrl` hostname trust, not origin trust.
- **Why it matters / User impact:** Operators using configured OpenAI-compatible memory embedding providers need the selected provider's private-endpoint policy to behave predictably: omitted policy preserves exact configured hostname compatibility, explicit `false` fails closed, and explicit `true` allows broader private-network access.
- **Scope boundary:** This author repair changes only tests, documentation wording, and PR evidence. It does not change schema, migrations, non-memory provider request paths, gateway/channel/session routing, global SSRF helpers, or the runtime behavior already present on this PR head.

Fixes #94166

## Origin / follow-up

N/A ŌĆö existing PR author repair for #94344.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

- **Linked PR(s):** #94180 (`feat(memory-core): allow private network endpoints for memory embeddings`). The public-contract helper also flagged #92307 through an environment/version term, but #92307 is about exec-approval startup warnings and is not the same embedding/private-network contract.
- **Gap in linked PR(s):** #94180 adds a memory-specific config surface, `memorySearch.remote.network.dangerouslyAllowPrivateNetwork`, for a trust decision that the selected model provider can already express with `models.providers.<id>.request.allowPrivateNetwork`.
- **Why this patch is better/canonical:** This PR reuses the existing provider transport-trust contract as the canonical source of truth for configured providers instead of introducing a second memory-only knob. This update only repairs the review findings on that existing approach.
- **Local real behavior proof advantage:** The existing local proof for this PR exercises the real `memory status --deep --json` CLI path against a loopback/private OpenAI-compatible endpoint in three states: omitted policy succeeds through exact-hostname trust with one `/v1/embeddings` request, explicit `request.allowPrivateNetwork: false` is blocked before the endpoint receives any request, and explicit `true` succeeds with one `/v1/embeddings` request.
- **Related open PR scan:** Current same-contract scan found #94180 as the linked alternative for memory private endpoint support; no broader canonical provider-inheritance PR was found besides this PR. #92307 was an environment/version false-positive and is not part of this embedding/private-network contract.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw memory status --deep` against a configured OpenAI-compatible embedding endpoint on a loopback/private address must preserve exact-hostname compatibility when provider private-network policy is omitted, must block when the selected provider explicitly sets `request.allowPrivateNetwork: false`, and must honor `request.allowPrivateNetwork: true` for explicit broader private-network trust.
- **Real environment tested:** Live CLI proof was captured in the local PR worktree on Linux, using the real source CLI, the real `memory-core` CLI command, isolated `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and a local OpenAI-compatible `/v1/embeddings` HTTP endpoint bound to a loopback/private address with host and port redacted below. The current author update did not change runtime source; current-head re-verification covered the changed test typing and docs wording.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** The live runtime proof command used for the PR behavior was:

```sh
node scripts/run-node.mjs memory status --agent proof --deep --json
```

The current author update was re-verified with these commands from `/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-94344`:

```sh
pnpm exec tsc --ignoreConfig --strict --module NodeNext --moduleResolution NodeNext --target ES2022 --types node --noEmit /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94344-evidence/lookupfn-overload-smoke.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.full-core-unit.config.ts src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts
pnpm check:test-types
git diff --check
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Exact-hostname compatibility result from the real CLI, with provider `request.allowPrivateNetwork` omitted:

```json
{
  "agentId": "proof",
  "status": {
    "provider": "openai-compatible",
    "requestedProvider": "private-embedding-proof",
    "model": "text-embedding-3-small"
  },
  "embeddingProbe": {
    "ok": true
  }
}
```

Exact-hostname compatibility summary:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "containsEmbeddingsReady": true,
  "serverRequestCount": 1
}
```

Explicit provider denial result from the real CLI:

```json
{
  "agentId": "proof",
  "status": {
    "provider": "openai-compatible",
    "requestedProvider": "private-embedding-proof",
    "model": "text-embedding-3-small"
  },
  "embeddingProbe": {
    "ok": false,
    "error": "Blocked hostname or private/internal/special-use IP address"
  }
}
```

Explicit provider denial summary:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "containsGuardBlock": true,
  "containsEmbeddingsReady": false,
  "serverRequestCount": 0
}
```

Provider opt-in overlay result from the real CLI:

```json
{
  "agentId": "proof",
  "status": {
    "provider": "openai-compatible",
    "requestedProvider": "private-embedding-proof",
    "model": "text-embedding-3-small"
  },
  "embeddingProbe": {
    "ok": true
  }
}
```

Provider opt-in overlay summary:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "containsEmbeddingsReady": true,
  "serverRequestCount": 1
}
```

Redacted endpoint request observed once in the omitted case and once in the opt-in case; the explicit false case observed zero endpoint requests:

```json
{
  "method": "POST",
  "url": "/v1/embeddings",
  "headers": {
    "host": "<loopback>:<redacted-port>",
    "authorization": "<redacted>",
    "content-type": "application/json",
    "x-proof": "openclaw-pr-94344"
  },
  "body": {
    "model": "text-embedding-3-small",
    "input": ["ping"],
    "dimensions": 3
  }
}
```

Current-head review repair verification:

```text
LookupFn overload smoke: passed with no TypeScript diagnostics.
openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts: Test Files 1 passed (1), Tests 30 passed (30).
pnpm check:test-types: completed with exit code 0 after running tsgo core and extension test typechecks.
git diff --check: passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The omitted configured provider alias reaches the loopback/private embedding endpoint successfully. The explicit false config is blocked by the guarded-fetch path before the endpoint sees a request. The explicit true config returns `embeddingProbe.ok: true` and reaches the endpoint once. The current update also resolves the `LookupFn` overload type issue and keeps the provider test passing under the owning Vitest config.
- **What was not tested:** I did not use a maintainer's real private LAN/VPN embedding service, third-party credentials, Mantis, Telegram, or Desktop proof infrastructure. This PR does not touch Telegram/Desktop/channel delivery; the local proof exercises the relevant memory CLI, configured-provider, and guarded-fetch runtime surface. I did not drive a live metadata/link-local endpoint for safety; metadata/link-local rebinding remains covered by the regression test that resolves an exact-hostname policy to `169.254.169.254` and expects private/internal blocking when broader opt-in is not set. I did not run `openclaw memory index --force`; `memory status --deep` is the requested memory runtime probe path and reaches the embedding availability check directly.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix plus author-side review repair.

## Review findings addressed

| Finding | Status | Resolution |
| --- | --- | --- |
| RF-001 | Addressed | This author update responds to the current `status: ŌÅ│ waiting on author` state. Label removal still depends on the next ClawSweeper/maintainer pass after submission. |
| RF-002 | Fixed/proven | The added SSRF tests now import `type LookupFn` and cast the async lookup fixtures through `unknown` before `LookupFn`, matching the overloaded `dns.promises.lookup` type shape. The isolated overload smoke and target provider test both pass. |
| RF-003 | Fixed/proven | `pnpm check:test-types` completed successfully with exit code 0 after the `LookupFn` overload repair. |
| RF-004 | Disclosed/maintainer decision | The provider-level `request.allowPrivateNetwork` inheritance remains an SSRF trust-boundary decision. This PR keeps the existing runtime behavior and explicitly calls out that maintainer/security acceptance is required. |
| RF-005 | Disclosed/maintainer decision | Explicit `request.allowPrivateNetwork: false` remains fail-closed for configured memory embedding setups that previously relied on exact-hostname compatibility. This update documents the hostname boundary and does not relax the current PR's runtime contract. |
| RF-006 | Addressed/disclosed | Contributor-side test and documentation findings are fixed; the remaining SSRF/security-boundary acceptance is intentionally left as maintainer/security judgment rather than hidden as an autonomous repair. |
| RF-007 | Fixed/proven | The specific line-level `LookupFn` overload issue is fixed by using `as unknown as LookupFn` for the lookup fixture functions. |
| RF-008 | Fixed | The trust note now says `hostname` and `exact-hostname` instead of `origin` and `exact-origin`. |

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts`
- **Target files:** `src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts`; `docs/reference/memory-config.md`
- **Scenario locked in:** Configured OpenAI-compatible memory providers use the selected provider request policy: omitted policy keeps exact configured `baseUrl` hostname trust, explicit `false` opts out of exact-hostname private-network trust, and explicit `true` allows broader private-network embedding requests. The test fixtures also keep private/internal resolution blocked when broader opt-in is not set.
- **Commands run after this author repair:**

```sh
pnpm exec tsc --ignoreConfig --strict --module NodeNext --moduleResolution NodeNext --target ES2022 --types node --noEmit /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-94344-evidence/lookupfn-overload-smoke.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.full-core-unit.config.ts src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts
pnpm check:test-types
git diff --check
```

- **Observed local result:** The `LookupFn` overload smoke passed without TypeScript diagnostics; `src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts` passed 30/30 tests under `test/vitest/vitest.full-core-unit.config.ts`; `pnpm check:test-types` exited 0; `git diff --check` exited 0.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The runtime source was already in place on the PR head. This update only repairs the review blockers around the added tests and docs wording, then verifies the owning test file and full test typecheck instead of expanding unrelated provider or gateway coverage.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** security-boundary, compatibility, auth-provider.
- **Risk explanation:** The PR touches the security boundary for private-network embedding fetches and the provider/config contract that decides when exact-hostname or broader private-network trust is allowed. A wrong change could relax SSRF blocking by default, break existing configured local/private aliases, or create a second incompatible memory-only trust surface.
- **Why acceptable:** This author update is limited to tests, docs wording, and PR evidence. The PR's runtime behavior remains constrained to the OpenAI-compatible embedding provider policy: omitted config preserves exact configured hostname trust, explicit false denies exact-hostname private trust, and explicit true is required for broader private-network access.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The original issue is that memory-core OpenAI-compatible embeddings did not consistently consume the selected configured provider's `request.allowPrivateNetwork` policy when building the embedding SSRF policy. The current review blocker was narrower: the added SSRF tests used lookup fixtures that did not satisfy the overloaded `LookupFn` type shape, and the docs described the trust boundary as an origin instead of a hostname.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The PR keeps the provider request policy as the source-of-truth for memory embedding SSRF policy and this update fixes the owner tests and docs language at that same boundary. It does not add downstream fetch fallbacks, a parallel memory-only knob, or unrelated runtime changes.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix with focused author repair.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The canonical contract surface is `models.providers.<id>.request.allowPrivateNetwork` for configured provider transport trust. Memory embedding SSRF policy consumes that provider-owned request setting; this PR does not add a parallel `memorySearch` private-network knob, does not change schema/default values, and does not alter unrelated provider, gateway, channel, or session contracts.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** Medium-high ŌĆö local CLI proof exists for the runtime path, the current author repair passes focused and full test-type verification, and the remaining SSRF/private-network acceptance is disclosed as maintainer/security judgment rather than presented as already externally approved.
- **Patch quality notes:** The `as unknown as LookupFn` casts are test-only fixtures for the overloaded `dns.promises.lookup` signature and match the existing SSRF pinning test pattern; the `EmbeddingProviderCreateOptions["config"]` casts are pre-existing partial config fixture casts in the task diff, not production type suppression. The runtime `return undefined` for explicit false is the intentional provider-denial path from the PR head. No skipped tests, `.only`, lint disables, runtime catch-all fallback, schema migration, or new public config knob were added in this author update.

target.title: feat(mattermost): add /model dialog picker
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Mattermost `/oc_model` and `/oc_models` still relied on the older message picker, so they could not use Mattermost's richer dialog flow.
- Why it matters: the Discord picker already feels native and multi-step; Mattermost needed a comparable native picker path instead of staying button-first.
- What changed: plain `/oc_model` and `/oc_models` now open a Mattermost dialog when `trigger_id` is available, dialog refresh/submit callbacks stay inside the Mattermost extension, and the final apply still routes through the existing shared `/model` command path.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no shared interactive-core expansion, no unrelated config/schema work, and the older message picker still remains as fallback when a dialog cannot be opened.
- Compatibility note: the provider -> model/runtime refresh path relies on Mattermost dynamic dialog refresh. Upstream documents that refresh path as a Mattermost Server `11.1+` capability, and older servers were not validated in this PR.

## Links

- Issue/PR refs: none

## Verification

- `CI=true PNPM_CONFIG_PM_ON_FAIL=ignore PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/model-picker.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.test.ts`
- `CI=true PNPM_CONFIG_PM_ON_FAIL=ignore PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/model-picker.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/model-picker.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts`
- `CI=true PNPM_CONFIG_PM_ON_FAIL=ignore PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `CI=true PNPM_CONFIG_PM_ON_FAIL=ignore PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm test src/infra/outbound/source-delivery-plan.test.ts`
- Rebased onto current `origin/main` and resolved the single overlapping `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/model-picker.ts` import conflict from current main.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost native `/oc_model` slash-command routing and dialog callback reachability on the live devbox-to-vp deployment that backs this plugin test setup.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw gateway running from this worktree on `devbox`, published through Tailscale Serve at `https://devbox.tail9cbe19.ts.net`; Mattermost running on `vp` and calling the gateway over that public tailnet URL.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Restart the gateway from the `codex-mm-model-dialog` worktree on `devbox`.
  2. Let Mattermost native `oc_*` commands register against `https://devbox.tail9cbe19.ts.net/api/channels/mattermost/command`.
  3. Probe the slash callback from the Mattermost host and verify it responds as a live POST-only endpoint.
  4. Run plain `/oc_model` in the Mattermost workspace tied to that deployment.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted runtime logs and copied live output from the real deployment.

```text
11:37:01 [mattermost] registered command /oc_model (id=rr9eyu8daif6p87czrtkwxb6fe)
11:37:02 [mattermost] slash commands activated for account default (8 commands)
11:37:02 [mattermost] slash commands registered (8 commands across 1 teams, callback=https://devbox.tail9cbe19.ts.net/api/channels/mattermost/command)
```

```text
$ curl -k -sS --connect-timeout 5 --max-time 10 -D - https://devbox.tail9cbe19.ts.net/api/channels/mattermost/command
HTTP/2 405
allow: POST
```

- Observed result after fix: The live deployment registered native Mattermost commands against the reachable devbox callback URL instead of the old `localhost` route, the callback was reachable from the Mattermost VPS, and the live tester on that deployment reported `/oc_model` working after the routing and channel-lookup fixes. This follow-up branch update also carries the same verified slash channel facts through dialog submit when later channel lookup is forbidden.
- What was not tested: A fresh screenshot or video of the post-rebase dialog submit flow is not attached here; the new submit-fallback behavior itself is locked by the added regression coverage in `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/model-picker.test.ts` and `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts`, and the broader rebased Mattermost shard including `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.test.ts` and `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.test.ts` stays green.

## Behavior Notes

- The new picker is Mattermost-owned UI: dialog open, refresh, and submit live in `extensions/mattermost/**`.
- Provider-dependent dialog refresh relies on Mattermost dynamic dialog refresh support, which upstream documents for Mattermost Server `11.1+`; this PR was validated only against the live server used in this branch testing, not against older Mattermost releases.
- If Mattermost does not provide a usable `trigger_id`, OpenClaw falls back to the existing message-based picker instead of breaking `/oc_model`.
- Slash-command authorization now falls back to channel information already present in the slash payload when `GET /channels/<id>` is forbidden, and dialog submit now reuses the signed fallback channel snapshot for the same restricted-channel case.

target.title: feat(feishu): add agent message delete and recall
target.body:
# PR 1 draft: Feishu agent message delete and recall

Title:

```text
feat(feishu): add agent message delete and recall actions
```

## Summary

- Add an agent-callable `feishu_message` tool for `list`, `delete`, `recall`, `read_receipts`, and `read_users`.
- Wire Feishu `messages` tool config through defaults, schema validation, plugin metadata, API exports, and runtime registration.
- Expose Telegram-like generic message actions for Feishu: `delete` and `unsend`.
- Track the latest bot-sent Feishu message per account/chat so natural-language requests like "delete your previous message" can work even when the model omits `messageId`.
- Document Feishu message-management permissions, Unix-second time filters, and delete/recall behavior.

## Related upstream work

- Closes / revives https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/51422
- Supersedes https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/52993
- Complements, but does not replace, inbound recall-event work in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/55225 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/55691
- Leaves Feishu reaction tool registration for a follow-up PR, because reaction fixes are already active in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/66478 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/77653

## Fixes compared with #52993

- `start_time` and `end_time` are documented and validated as Unix-second timestamp strings, matching Feishu's API, instead of ISO 8601 strings.
- `delete` and `recall` have distinct request paths and user-facing messages; delete no longer reuses recall semantics such as "cannot recall after two minutes".
- Missing Feishu scopes are surfaced with actionable messages, including `im:message.group_msg` for group-message deletion.

## Notes for reviewers

- Feishu delete behaves as a platform soft-delete/recall: follow-up reads can return `deleted: true`, `updated: true`, and empty content. Some clients may still show a deletion placeholder.
- The last-bot-message fallback is intentionally in-memory and scoped by account/chat with a 24-hour TTL. It only records messages sent by this bot process.
- The feature is gated by `tools.messages`, defaulting to `false` because message list/delete/recall/read-receipts are sensitive.

## Testing

```text
corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/message-tool.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.reply-fallback.test.ts
# 4 files, 104 tests passed

corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/outbound.test.ts
# 2 files, 84 tests passed

corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts
# 63 files, 786 tests passed

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions
# passed

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
# passed

corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check ...
# passed

corepack pnpm exec oxlint extensions/feishu/src/sent-message-cache.ts extensions/feishu/src/send-target.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.ts extensions/feishu/src/media.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
# passed, 0 warnings/errors

git diff --check
# passed
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu agents can delete or recall bot messages from a real group chat through the new `feishu_message` tool and generic `message(action="delete"|"unsend")` path.

Real environment tested: Windows 11 host, local OpenClaw gateway from this branch, enterprise self-built Feishu app using WebSocket mode, real Feishu group chat with the bot added, and Feishu app scopes including `im:message:update`, `im:message:readonly`, and `im:message.group_msg`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Enabled the opt-in message management gate for the Feishu test account, started the local gateway from this branch with `openclaw gateway run --force`, sent messages in the real Feishu group, first verified delete failed before `im:message.group_msg` was granted, granted the scope and restarted the gateway, sent a live bot reply, asked the bot to delete its previous message, then checked the deleted message through Feishu message read output.

Evidence after fix: Copied live output and redacted runtime notes from the real Feishu setup:

```text
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
Feishu WebSocket ready at 2026-05-18T15:43:04+08:00

Initial group delete attempt:
  Feishu API error code=230027
  msg="need scope: im:message.group_msg"
  log_id=20260518150333B37818A84E685EE4FE60

After granting im:message.group_msg:
  sent message_id=om_x100b6f9fbdc2c8e4c3bb0f1440e9c1d
  im.message.delete response={"code":0,"msg":"success"}
  follow-up message read:
    deleted=true
    updated=true
    content_length=0

Natural-language group flow after last-bot-message fallback:
  user: delete your previous message
  agent called message(action="delete") without messageId
  fallback resolved last bot message in chat oc_* to om_*
  Feishu delete returned deleted=true
```

Observed result after fix: The bot replied in the Feishu group, deleted its own previous message after the required group-message scope was enabled, and Feishu readback showed the target message was deleted with empty content. The natural-language "delete your previous message" case also worked without the model supplying a `messageId`.

What was not tested: No additional gaps

target.title: fix: skip TTS for explicit command replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: explicit slash/native command replies such as `/active-memory status` were still passed through final TTS when TTS was configured to synthesize final replies.
- Why it matters: command/status output should remain visible text only; speaking command output is noisy and can leak operational status into audio surfaces.
- What changed: explicit command turns now bypass final-reply TTS synthesis while preserving normal final reply delivery and media normalization.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): non-command assistant replies, streamed ACP block TTS, tool-result TTS handling, and TTS config defaults are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #82582
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: explicit command replies are delivered as text but are not sent to the TTS synthesizer.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.4.1, Node v23.11.0, pnpm v11.1.0, local OpenClaw checkout on `fix/command-replies-skip-tts`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Real OpenClaw dispatch smoke: `PATH=/opt/homebrew/Cellar/node/23.11.0/bin:$PATH OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 node --import tsx --input-type=module` with a local Telegram direct command context, `tts.mode: "always"`, and production `dispatchReplyFromConfig`.
  - `PATH=/opt/homebrew/Cellar/node/23.11.0/bin:$PATH pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts -t "does not synthesize TTS audio for explicit command replies"`
  - `PATH=/opt/homebrew/Cellar/node/23.11.0/bin:$PATH pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-tts.test.ts`
  - `PATH=/opt/homebrew/Cellar/node/23.11.0/bin:$PATH pnpm check:changed`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  - Copied live terminal output from the real OpenClaw dispatch smoke (no test runner):

```json
{
  "command": "/active-memory status",
  "ttsMode": "always",
  "result": {
    "queuedFinal": true,
    "counts": {
      "tool": 0,
      "block": 0,
      "final": 1
    }
  },
  "delivered": [
    [
      "final",
      {
        "text": "Active memory status: enabled"
      }
    ]
  ],
  "audioMediaDelivered": false
}
```

  - Supplemental focused regression: `Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 1 passed | 116 skipped (117)`
  - Supplemental focused suite: `Test Files 2 passed (2); Tests 128 passed (128)`
  - Supplemental changed-file gate: `pnpm check:changed` completed successfully, including core/core-test typecheck, oxlint, import-cycle, and guard checks.
- Observed result after fix: the explicit command reply is delivered once as final text, and no audio/media payload is delivered despite `tts.mode: "always"`.
- What was not tested: a live Telegram/real TTS provider run with actual synthesized media; the real dispatch smoke disables bundled plugins to keep the local proof deterministic.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the new regression test failed before the implementation change with `expected "vi.fn()" to not be called at all, but actually been called 1 times` for `maybeApplyTtsToPayload` on payload text `Active memory status: enabled`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: final reply dispatch applied TTS based only on TTS configuration and payload kind; it did not distinguish explicit command turns from normal assistant replies.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no dispatch-level regression test asserting that explicit command replies bypass TTS while still delivering visible text.
- Contributing context (if known): command reply visibility had dedicated group/channel coverage, but final-reply TTS coverage did not include command-origin replies.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: a direct explicit native command turn with TTS synthesis enabled delivers text but never calls `maybeApplyTtsToPayload`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the regression occurs at the dispatch boundary where command-origin metadata and final-reply TTS are both available.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Explicit slash/native command replies no longer generate TTS audio. Regular assistant final replies still follow configured TTS behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[/command] -> command text reply -> final TTS path -> spoken command output

After:
[/command] -> command text reply -> TTS bypass -> visible text only
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 26.4.1
- Runtime/container: Node v23.11.0, pnpm v11.1.0
- Model/provider: N/A for focused dispatch tests
- Integration/channel (if any): dispatch seam for native command turn; no live channel
- Relevant config (redacted): TTS mock configured to synthesize final audio

### Steps

1. Run the new focused regression test before the fix; observe `maybeApplyTtsToPayload` is called for an explicit command reply.
2. Apply the dispatch guard that bypasses final TTS for `isExplicitCommandTurn(...)`.
3. Re-run focused tests and changed-file checks.

### Expected

- Explicit command reply text is delivered.
- Final TTS is not attempted for that command reply.
- Non-command final reply TTS paths remain covered by existing tests.

### Actual

- Matches expected after the patch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - RED: the new command-reply TTS regression test failed on current `origin/main` behavior.
  - GREEN: the focused regression test passed after the dispatch guard.
  - Focused suite: `dispatch-from-config.test.ts` and `commands-tts.test.ts` passed together.
  - Changed-file gate: `pnpm check:changed` passed.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Existing final-mode TTS tests in `dispatch-from-config.test.ts` still pass.
  - Command text reply remains visible via `sendFinalReply`.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Live channel/audio-provider behavior with real synthesized media.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: command-origin final replies with intentionally requested audio would also bypass final TTS.
  - Mitigation: the issue describes slash/native command output as status-like text; normal non-command final replies and streamed ACP TTS behavior are unchanged.

target.title: fix(feishu): preserve ACP topic conversation bindings
target.body:
## Summary
- Refreshes #50943 onto current `openclaw/main` as a narrow Feishu plugin fix.
- Adds Feishu `messaging.resolveInboundConversation` so ACP thread binding uses plugin-owned topic conversation grammar.
- Covers topic, sender-scoped topic, and direct conversation resolution in Feishu channel tests.
- Adds ACP spawn integration coverage that imports the Feishu plugin through its public barrel and verifies topic binding plus bootstrap delivery target selection from a sender-scoped topic context.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts` ŌĆö 112/112 passed on `02113a7a`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts` ŌĆö 57/57 passed on `02113a7a`
- `git diff --check`
- `env PNPM_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pnpm-home pnpm exec oxfmt --write --no-error-on-unmatched-pattern src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: ACP session thread binding now resolves Feishu/Lark topic conversations through the Feishu plugin instead of generic fallback parsing, and the ACP bootstrap delivery target is normalized back to the intended topic thread instead of the sender-scoped target.
Real environment tested: local source checkout rebased on `upstream/main` `322f0bb7`, with dependencies refreshed by `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` using temporary `PNPM_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pnpm-home`; the ACP coverage imports the actual `feishuPlugin` through `extensions/feishu/channel-plugin-api.ts` and exercises `spawnAcpDirect` through the production conversation-resolution/session-binding path.

target.title: fix(auth): clean refresh contention diagnostics
target.body:
## Summary
- Keep OAuth `refresh_contention` diagnostics deterministic by avoiding nested file-lock cause flattening in user-facing refresh failures.
- Preserve programmatic `code` / `lockPath` on `OAuthManagerRefreshError` while redacting lock paths from messages and JSON-facing diagnostics, including structured non-`Error` causes.
- Add regression coverage for manager-level redaction and resolver-level single-message surfacing.

## Related PRs and Issues
- Follow-up to https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/82670, which landed the first auth refresh redaction/pass over the agents reliability slice.
- Related to https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/67876 and closed https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/26322, the original cross-agent OAuth refresh serialization/race fix.
- Adjacent to https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/82117 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80738 for Codex OAuth refresh fallback/forced-refresh behavior.
- Adjacent to https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/83312 for legacy Codex OAuth sidecar compatibility.
- Related but not closing https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/42541; that issue is already closed by the prior auth-refresh work.
- Closes: none. Supersedes: none.

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth-manager.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth.test.ts`: 4 files / 76 tests passed.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: OAuth refresh contention failures no longer expose file-lock paths in user-facing refresh diagnostics, and the contention text is surfaced once instead of duplicated through cause-chain flattening.

target.title: feat(browser): add browserbase profile driver with per-attach connectUrl resolution
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a new `browserbase` profile driver to the OpenClaw browser plugin. Profiles using this driver declare a Browserbase keep-alive session id and an env var name for the API key; on every CDP attach OpenClaw fetches a fresh `connectUrl` from `GET /v1/sessions/<id>` and hands it to the existing CDP pipeline. Eliminates the stale-signing-key fragility that breaks stateful Browserbase work today.

```jsonc
"browserbase-lexzur": {
  "driver": "browserbase",
  "browserbaseSessionId": "44589bac-33f0-4080-9eff-f1b3e3a0bf9c",
  "browserbaseApiKeyEnv": "BROWSERBASE_API_KEY",
  "attachOnly": true
}
```

## Why

The existing `cdpUrl: "wss://...?signingKey=..."` model puts a short-lived JWT into static config. Browserbase rotates the signing key well before the session itself expires ŌĆö verified empirically with two consecutive `GET /v1/sessions/<id>` calls returning different `signingKey` values for the same session. Once the embedded key ages out, every CDP attach gets `1008 unauthorized` and any multi-step stateful workflow (login ŌåÆ multi-step ŌåÆ submit) breaks mid-flow.

The fix is the documented Browserbase pattern: fetch a fresh `connectUrl` via the Sessions API on each attach. Bearer auth comes from the operator's own credentials, refreshed on each request, no embedded lifetime to track.

## What changed

### New module ŌĆö `extensions/browser/src/browser/browserbase-session.ts`

- `fetchBrowserbaseConnectUrl(sessionId, apiKeyEnv, opts)` ŌĆö issues `GET /v1/sessions/<id>` with `X-BB-API-Key`. 8s default timeout, combines internal AbortController with caller-supplied `signal`. Three named error classes: `BrowserbaseAuthConfigError`, `BrowserbaseSessionUnavailableError`, `BrowserbaseSessionMalformedError`.
- `withResolvedCdpUrl(profile, opts)` ŌĆö identity for non-browserbase drivers (same reference returned, zero allocations, no fetch); shallow-copy with freshly resolved `cdpUrl` / `cdpHost` / `cdpIsLoopback: false` for browserbase drivers.
- **No caching.** Two calls = two HTTP requests by design ŌĆö the whole point is signing-key rotation.

### Resolver branch ŌĆö `extensions/browser/src/browser/config.ts`

- `resolveProfile()` gains a `browserbase` branch that validates `browserbaseSessionId` is UUID-shaped and `browserbaseApiKeyEnv` matches `/^[A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*$/`. Returns `cdpUrl: ""` ŌĆö URL is resolved lazily at attach time.
- Operator-friendly fail-fast at config load (not on first hot-path attach) for missing/malformed fields.

### Capability branch ŌĆö `extensions/browser/src/browser/profile-capabilities.ts`

- Third branch for `driver === "browserbase"` ŌåÆ `mode: "remote-cdp"`, `isRemote: true`, `usesPersistentPlaywright: true`, all `/json/*` endpoint flags off (Browserbase doesn't expose JSON tab endpoints; everything goes through the signed WS connectUrl).

### Wired into CDP attach call sites

- `server-context.tab-ops.ts` ŌĆö `readTabs` + `openTab` Playwright legs.
- `server-context.selection.ts` ŌĆö `focusTab` + `closeTab` Playwright legs.
- `server-context.availability.ts` ŌĆö `isReachable` / `isHttpReachable` probe via cheap `fetchBrowserbaseConnectUrl` for browserbase profiles (instead of full CDP WS attach per status poll).

### Default-timeout bump (separate commit `700639f1df`)

Based on real-world transcontinental remote-CDP topology (SFO-hosted Browserbase + Linux gateway). Previous 1500/2000 defaults were too aggressive ŌĆö left zero margin on healthy attaches under transit jitter.

- `remoteCdpTimeoutMs`: 1500 ŌåÆ 3000
- `remoteCdpHandshakeTimeoutMs` floor: `max(remoteCdpTimeoutMs*2, 5000)` ŌåÆ resolves to 6000 by default. Preserves the `handshake Ōēź 2├Ś http` invariant for operators who customize one without bumping the other.

### Docs (separate commit `68f68af6f7`)

New subsection in `docs/tools/browser.md` covering keep-alive sessions, auto-create vs session-id distinction, the `POST /v1/sessions` curl example with `keepAlive: true`, the new profile shape, why static-URL signingKey rotation is fragile, and the SecretRef follow-up note.

## Tests

```
extensions/browser/src/browser/browserbase-session.test.ts     13 passed
extensions/browser/src/browser/config.browserbase.test.ts       8 passed
```

Full extension-browser suite: **103 files, 1099 passed, 1 pre-existing skip, 0 failed.**

## Live smoke test

Verified end-to-end against the production Browserbase API with a fresh keepAlive session:

| Test | Result |
|---|---|
| Happy path live `GET /v1/sessions/<id>` | Ō£ģ 584ms |
| No-caching contract (two calls = different signingKeys) | Ō£ģ confirmed signing-key rotation working |
| `BrowserbaseAuthConfigError` raised on missing env var | Ō£ģ |
| `withResolvedCdpUrl` shallow-copy preserves immutability | Ō£ģ |
| Identity passthrough for non-browserbase drivers | Ō£ģ same reference returned |

## Out of scope (deliberately)

- **SecretRef supported-surface expansion** for `browserbaseApiKeyEnv`. The new field is read from `process.env` at attach time, not via SecretRef resolution. Follow-up PR to add the path to the canonical credential surface. Operators who want a 1Password-only secret can inject the env var via `op run --env-file=...` at gateway start.
- **Auto-mint-on-stale-session fallback** ŌĆö when the configured session id is no longer `RUNNING`, this PR fails loudly with `BrowserbaseSessionUnavailableError`. Auto-minting would mean OpenClaw writes its own config at runtime; that's a separate design conversation.
- **Renaming the legacy `browserbase` auto-create profile** to `browserbase-ephemeral`. Follow-up release with migration tooling.

## Risk + rollback

Risk surface is narrow ŌĆö the new code path only activates when a profile declares `driver: "browserbase"`. Existing `openclaw` and `existing-session` profiles get an identity passthrough in `withResolvedCdpUrl` (same reference, zero allocations, no fetch). Default-timeout bump is conservative and only affects remote CDP profiles; loopback `openclaw` profiles are unaffected.

Rollback is a revert. Existing deployments without a `browserbase`-driver profile are bit-identical in behavior except for the +1.5s remote-CDP timeout bump.

## Lexzur audit context

Lexzur audit on 2026-05-17 burned 4-5 Browserbase sessions and as many OTPs trying to drive a multi-step SSO ticket form before identifying the static-signingKey-in-URL pattern as the root cause. This PR is the permanent fix for that class of failure ŌĆö multi-step Browserbase flows can now complete in a single keep-alive session with cookies persisting across tool calls.

target.title: fix(config): tolerate unknown metadata keys
target.body:
## Summary

OpenClaw currently treats `meta` as a strict object containing only `lastTouchedVersion` and `lastTouchedAt`. If a newer runtime, tool, or config writer adds another bookkeeping field under `meta`, current binaries reject the whole config with an error like:

```text
meta: Unrecognized key: "lastTouchedReason"
```

That can prevent the gateway from starting until the user manually edits `openclaw.json` or repairs the config.

This PR makes only the `meta` object forward-compatible by allowing additional metadata keys while preserving existing strict validation for operational config.

## Why this is intentionally narrow

This is not a general "ignore unknown config keys" change.

Previous broader attempts to tolerate or strip unknown keys at startup were discussed in related PRs such as #47654, #40198, and #65766. There is also an existing fail-closed posture for invalid operational config, e.g. #28205. This PR keeps that posture intact.

The change is limited to `meta`, which is runtime-managed bookkeeping data. Unknown keys under `gateway`, `channels`, providers, auth, plugins, agents, and other operational config still fail validation as before.

## User impact

We observed a real gateway startup failure caused by an extra metadata field:

```json
{
  "meta": {
    "lastTouchedVersion": "2026.5.12",
    "lastTouchedAt": "...",
    "lastTouchedReason": "..."
  }
}
```

The rest of the config was valid. The failure mode was operationally severe because the gateway could not start, but the offending field was metadata rather than executable, routing, provider, auth, or security-relevant configuration.

## What changed

- `meta` now accepts additional unknown metadata keys.
- `meta.lastTouchedVersion` and `meta.lastTouchedAt` remain typed as before.
- The TypeScript config type reflects forward-compatible metadata keys.
- A regression test verifies that unknown metadata keys are accepted and preserved.
- The generated config baseline hash is updated for the intentional schema surface change.

## What did not change

- Strict validation remains in place for the rest of `openclaw.json`.
- Unknown operational config keys still fail validation.
- No automatic config mutation or cleanup is introduced.
- No gateway, auth, provider, channel, or plugin behavior is relaxed.

## Related work

- #80856: related `meta.lastTouched*` handling, but for rejecting user writes to auto-managed metadata paths.
- #68664: broader issue where config validation can block recovery or migration paths.
- #57391: related crash-loop class caused by unrecognized config keys.
- #69631 / #69659: related doctor repair behavior around unknown keys.
- #77802: related doctor repair failure when multiple validation errors exist.
- #72527 / #72535: related version-mismatch config compatibility issue.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/config/config.meta-timestamp-coercion.test.ts` (7 tests)
- `corepack pnpm config:schema:check`
- `corepack pnpm config:docs:check`
- `corepack pnpm config:channels:check`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core --pretty false`
- `corepack pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`
- `git diff --check`
- `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-shims:$PATH corepack pnpm check:changed`

Additional note: while running a broader local config shard, `src/config/io.write-config.test.ts` currently fails one plugin warning assertion. The same failure reproduces on `HEAD~1` in a detached baseline worktree with the same dependencies, so it does not appear to be introduced by this PR.


## Real behavior proof

The proof below uses a temporary redacted `OPENCLAW_HOME` containing only a minimal `openclaw.json`; no real user secrets, channel IDs, tokens, or provider config are included.

### Before this PR: extra metadata key blocks config loading

Command run against `HEAD~1` in a detached baseline worktree:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp> node --import tsx --input-type=module <loadConfig proof snippet>
Invalid config at <tmp>/.openclaw/openclaw.json:\n- meta: Unrecognized key: "lastTouchedReason"
BEFORE loadConfig failed: - meta: Unrecognized key: "lastTouchedReason"
```

The redacted config used for that proof:

```json
{
  "meta": {
    "lastTouchedVersion": "2026.5.12",
    "lastTouchedAt": "2026-05-17T00:00:00.000Z",
    "lastTouchedReason": "redacted proof note"
  },
  "gateway": { "mode": "local" }
}
```

### After this PR: the same config loads and preserves metadata

Command run against this PR head (`c9b69198`):

```text
$ OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp> node --import tsx --input-type=module <loadConfig proof snippet>
AFTER loadConfig ok: true
AFTER preserved meta.lastTouchedReason: redacted proof note
```

This exercises the real `loadConfig()` path from `src/config/config.ts`, not just a unit-test assertion.

### Operational unknown keys still fail closed

Command run against this PR head (`c9b69198`) with an unknown key under `gateway`:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp> node --import tsx --input-type=module <loadConfig proof snippet>
Invalid config at <tmp>/.openclaw/openclaw.json:\n- gateway: Unrecognized key: "redactedUnknownKey"
AFTER operational unknown failed: - gateway: Unrecognized key: "redactedUnknownKey"
```

This confirms the change is limited to runtime metadata and does not broadly relax operational config validation.

target.title: fix(sessions): prune stale gateway model-run sessions
target.body:
## Summary
- add a model-run-specific session retention setting with a conservative default of 24h
- validate `session.maintenance.modelRunPruneAfter` in the strict session schema (`duration | number | false`)
- prune stale `agent:*:explicit:model-run-<uuid>` probe sessions before global age/cap maintenance during write, load-time enforcement, and `sessions cleanup`
- expose `modelRunPruned` / `prune-model-run` in cleanup summaries and dry-run plans

Fixes #88632

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: stale gateway `model-run-<uuid>` explicit probe sessions are reported and pruned by `openclaw sessions cleanup`, while recent model-run probes and durable non-model-run sessions remain.
- Real environment tested: local macOS checkout at PR head `0a76e16651686277dfe15b9e108252b9caf4a13c`, Node 26.0.0, built CLI via `node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup`, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and temp session store under `/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: built the CLI, created a redacted temp `sessions.json` with one stale model-run key, one recent model-run key, and one durable control key, then ran:

```console
$ OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/home \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/state \
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/openclaw.json \
  node openclaw.mjs sessions cleanup --dry-run --json \
  --store /tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/sessions.json

$ OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/home \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/state \
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/openclaw.json \
  node openclaw.mjs sessions cleanup --enforce --json \
  --store /tmp/openclaw-88632-proof/sessions.json
```

- Evidence after fix: copied terminal output from the real built CLI run:

```json
{
  "dryRun": {
    "beforeCount": 3,
    "afterCount": 2,
    "modelRunPruned": 1,
    "pruned": 0,
    "capped": 0,
    "wouldMutate": true
  },
  "enforce": {
    "beforeCount": 3,
    "afterCount": 2,
    "modelRunPruned": 1,
    "pruned": 0,
    "capped": 0,
    "wouldMutate": true
  },
  "remainingKeys": [
    "agent:main:explicit:durable-control",
    "agent:main:explicit:model-run-123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174001"
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: dry-run and enforce both reported `modelRunPruned: 1`; after enforce, only the stale gateway model-run probe was removed, and the recent model-run plus durable control session remained.
- What was not tested: a live remote gateway model inference call was not required for this proof; the after-fix cleanup behavior was exercised through the real built OpenClaw CLI against an isolated session store. No channel, credential, model provider, or gateway transport behavior is changed in this PR.

## Testing
- `node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup`
- `pnpm format:docs:check docs/cli/sessions.md docs/concepts/session.md docs/reference/session-management-compaction.md docs/gateway/config-agents.md`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `pnpm lint:docs -- docs/cli/sessions.md docs/concepts/session.md docs/reference/session-management-compaction.md docs/gateway/config-agents.md`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.commands.config.ts src/commands/sessions-cleanup.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/zod-schema.session-maintenance-extensions.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts src/config/schema.base.generated.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/config/zod-schema.session.ts src/config/zod-schema.session-maintenance-extensions.test.ts src/commands/sessions-cleanup.test.ts src/commands/sessions-cleanup.ts src/config/schema.help.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.ts src/config/sessions/runtime-types.ts src/config/sessions/store-load.ts src/config/sessions/store-maintenance.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.ts src/config/types.base.ts`

Note: full `pnpm format:check` still reports unrelated pre-existing formatting issues outside this PR's touched files.

## Current status (2026-06-23)

Rebased onto latest `main`; current head `fc0e07e` is `CLEAN` / `MERGEABLE` with all required CI green. The previously-noted `agentic-agents-core-models` / `-runtime` jobs (tracked separately by #92075 as main-side flakes) no longer appear red on this PR.

The `isGatewayModelRunSessionKey` predicate tightening from the 06-12 self-review pass (canonical-key-only, rejects unscoped/empty-agent/extra-segment/whitespace keys) lands after the manual proof head above and is covered by the negative + positive key tests in `src/config/sessions/store.pruning.test.ts`, which pass at the current head. `maintainerCanModify` is enabled, so this branch is takeover-ready.

target.title: feat(synology-chat): add configurable triggerWord to replace payload-based stripping
target.body:
## Problem

The channel currently strips `payload.trigger_word` (the field Synology
Chat populates on each webhook delivery) from the message text. This
field is not user-controlled and its value depends on the Synology Chat
configuration in unpredictable ways:

- When no trigger word is configured in Synology Chat, Synology
  populates `trigger_word` with the full message text for CJK input, and
  with the first word of the message for ASCII input. This means:
  - CJK messages are silently dropped (stripped text is empty ŌåÆ 204,
    no log entry, no agent delivery)
  - ASCII messages are delivered with the first word missing
- When a trigger word is configured, users expecting to send the trigger
  word alone (e.g. a one-word ping) get their message silently dropped

Both failures produce no log entry and no feedback to the user.

## Solution

Replace the implicit payload-based stripping with an explicit opt-in
config field: `channels.synology-chat.triggerWord`.

- **Default (unset)**: full message text is delivered unchanged.
  `payload.trigger_word` is ignored entirely.
- **When set**: the configured prefix is stripped from the start of the
  message, but only when the result is non-empty. A message consisting
  solely of the trigger word is delivered in full rather than dropped.

## Usage

```json
{
  "channels": {
    "synology-chat": {
      "triggerWord": "!bot"
    }
  }
}
```

Messages starting with `!bot ` are delivered with the prefix removed.
Messages that are exactly `!bot` are delivered as `!bot`. Messages with
no `!bot` prefix are delivered unchanged.

## Changes

- `types.ts`: add `triggerWord?: string` to config fields; `string | undefined` to resolved account
- `config-schema.ts`: add `z.string().optional()` validation
- `accounts.ts`: pass through `merged.triggerWord`
- `webhook-handler.ts`: use `account.triggerWord` instead of `payload.trigger_word`; add non-empty guard on stripped result
- `webhook-handler.test.ts`: update existing test; add cases for full-text preservation and no-config pass-through

## Real behavior proof

Tested on OpenClaw v2026.5.12, Ubuntu 22.04, live Synology Chat outgoing webhook.
No `triggerWord` configured.

Synology Chat's observed behavior for ASCII text when no trigger word is set in
Synology Chat:
- `text` field = full message text
- `trigger_word` field = first word of the message

For a single-word message this means `text == trigger_word`.

### Old code path (`payload.trigger_word`, before this PR)

```js
// sanitizeSynologyWebhookText ŌĆö old
if (payload.trigger_word && cleanText.startsWith(payload.trigger_word)) {
  const stripped = cleanText.slice(payload.trigger_word.length).trim();
  if (stripped) cleanText = stripped;
}
```

- `text="ping"`, `trigger_word="ping"` ŌåÆ `stripped = ""`, guard fails, `cleanText = ""`
  ŌåÆ early `respondNoContent(res)` + `return { ok: false }` ŌåÆ **204, no log entry, no delivery**
- `text="hello world"`, `trigger_word="hello"` ŌåÆ `cleanText = "world"`
  ŌåÆ delivered with first word missing

### New code path (`account.triggerWord`, this PR)

With no `triggerWord` in config, `account.triggerWord` is `undefined`.
The stripping block is skipped entirely. Both payloads pass through unchanged.

```
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" ŌĆ” \
  -d "token=<redacted>&user_id=99&username=testuser&text=ping&trigger_word=ping"
# ŌåÆ 204

curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" ŌĆ” \
  -d "token=<redacted>&user_id=99&username=testuser&text=hello+world&trigger_word=hello"
# ŌåÆ 204
```

Gateway log (both messages reached the agent):

```
[synology-chat] Message from testuser (99): ping
[synology-chat] Agent reply started for 99
[synology-chat] Message from testuser (99): hello world
[synology-chat] Agent reply started for 99
```

Note: the gateway always returns 204 to the outgoing webhook (including the silent-drop
path). The log entry `Message from ŌĆ” (99): <text>` is the proof of delivery ŌĆö the drop
path produces **no such log line**.

target.title: fix(env): propagate service-managed GH_TOKEN to trusted exec
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `~/.openclaw/.env` could not be used as a durable source for operator-curated tool secrets like `GH_TOKEN` because override-only-dangerous keys were stripped before the managed gateway service could expose them to trusted exec children. - Why it matters: operators can configure `gh` and similar CLI tools for OpenClaw, but after gateway install/restart those tools could not see `GH_TOKEN` through the managed service path, so authenticated CLI workflows failed unless the token was injected some other way. - What changed: allow `~/.openclaw/.env` to retain override-only-blocked secrets as managed service keys, add a trusted exec inherited-env allowlist sourced from `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS`, and document the durable service env behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): everywhere-dangerous env vars such as `LD_PRELOAD`, `NODE_OPTIONS`, `BASH_ENV`, `DYLD_*`, and `GIT_DIR` stay blocked. `OPENCLAW_*` keys still do not gain trusted exec inheritance. Untrusted exec postures do not receive these secrets. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: `GH_TOKEN` placed in `~/.openclaw/.env` should survive managed gateway install/restart and be available to trusted gateway exec children, so `gh` can authenticate without manually injecting the token into each command. - Real environment tested: macOS OpenClaw gateway installed as a managed service, local OpenClaw checkout from v2026.5.2 with this patch applied, `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`, Telegram/agent exec path using `host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```sh cp ~/.env ~/.openclaw/.env chmod 600 ~/.openclaw/.env openclaw gateway stop openclaw gateway install --force openclaw gateway start openclaw gateway status ``` then ask the agent to run `gh auth status` and report back - Evidence after fix (screenshot): - Telegram screenshot showing the live exec environment reporting `GH_TOKEN_present: true`, `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`, `gh auth status` logged in via `GH_TOKEN`, and `gh api user` returning the authenticated account JSON. Account details and token values are redacted. - Observed result after fix: trusted gateway exec can use `GH_TOKEN` inherited from the managed service env, and `gh` authenticates successfully from the OpenClaw exec environment. - What was not tested: Linux/systemd real-service behavior was not manually tested; the Linux path is covered by the shared service-env/unit tests. Non-trusted exec postures were verified by targeted unit tests, not by a second live service screenshot. The exec integration test (`bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts`) is skipped on Windows (shell-dependent); unit-level coverage runs on all platforms. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): - `before-1.jpg` and `before-2.jpg` ŌĆö terminal screenshots showing the pre-fix managed-service behavior where the gateway env path stripped `GH_TOKEN` before it could reach the exec child, so `gh` could not authenticate after service install/restart. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `~/.openclaw/.env` parsing treated keys that are dangerous as agent request overrides the same as keys that are dangerous everywhere. This stripped operator-curated tool tokens like `GH_TOKEN` before the managed service could record them as durable env. Separately, exec env sanitization had no trusted-mode inherited-key allowlist, so there was no narrow path for managed service keys to reach trusted tool children. - Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests covered dangerous env stripping and service env formatting, but did not cover the desired operator-curated secret path from `~/.openclaw/.env` through managed service metadata into trusted gateway exec. - Contributing context (if known): the security model already distinguished inherited/env override risk, but the durable service env path did not preserve that distinction. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts` - `src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts` - `src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts` - `src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `GH_TOKEN` and similar override-only-blocked keys can be retained from owner-controlled durable service env, become managed service keys, and reach exec children only in the trusted gateway posture (`host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`). Unsafe keys and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: unit tests cover each policy seam, and the exec integration test proves the env-composition wiring reaches the child process under the relevant exec postures without requiring live GitHub credentials in CI. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A, new tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Operators can place `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env`, reinstall/start the managed gateway, and use `gh` from trusted gateway exec. This does not grant those tokens to untrusted exec postures. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser strips override-only-dangerous key -> managed gateway service has no GH_TOKEN-managed allowlist entry -> trusted exec child cannot authenticate gh After: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser keeps operator-curated override-only key -> install records GH_TOKEN in OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS -> trusted gateway exec allowlist permits inherited GH_TOKEN -> gh authenticates from the exec child ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this intentionally allows operator-curated service env secrets like `GH_TOKEN` to reach trusted gateway exec children. The path is constrained to keys recorded by the managed service env metadata and to the trusted exec posture. Everywhere-dangerous keys are still stripped, `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded, request overrides remain sanitized, and non-trusted postures do not inherit the allowlisted secret. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local managed OpenClaw gateway service - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): OpenClaw agent exec path, GitHub CLI (`gh`) as the real CLI consumer - Relevant config (redacted): `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`; `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` included `GH_TOKEN`; token value redacted in all evidence. ### Steps 1. Put `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env` and set owner-only permissions. 2. Reinstall/restart the managed gateway service with this patch applied. 3. Run a trusted gateway exec env probe and `gh auth status` / `gh api user`. 4. Run the targeted regression tests: ```sh pnpm test src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts ``` ### Expected - `process.env.GH_TOKEN` is present inside trusted gateway exec. - `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` contains `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` reports authentication via `GH_TOKEN`. - Untrusted exec postures do not receive the fixture token in tests. - Everywhere-dangerous and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. ### Actual - Live evidence showed `GH_TOKEN_present: true` and `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`. - `gh auth status` authenticated via `GH_TOKEN` and `gh api user` succeeded with redacted account output. - Targeted tests covered the parser, managed service allowlist, host env sanitizer, daemon install plan, and exec wiring. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Evidence files prepared for upload: - `before-1.jpg` - `before-2.jpg` - `after-1.jpg` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Managed gateway install/restart picks up `GH_TOKEN` from `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Trusted gateway exec sees `GH_TOKEN` and `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` includes `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` and `gh api user` work from the OpenClaw exec environment using `GH_TOKEN`. - Targeted tests exercise trusted, allowlist, and prompted exec postures plus dangerous-key exclusions. - Edge cases checked: - `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded from trusted inherited allowlist. - Everywhere-dangerous keys remain blocked even if listed. - PATH override smuggling remains blocked. - Default sanitizer behavior remains strict without `allowInheritedKeys`. - What you did **not** verify: - Manual Linux/systemd live service verification. - Every provider token named in the override-only dangerous list; `GH_TOKEN` is the live proof case and tests use representative fixture keys. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) Yes, for operators who want this behavior. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: place `GH_TOKEN` or similar operator-curated tool secret in `~/.openclaw/.env`, ensure the file is owner-only, then reinstall/restart the managed gateway service so `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` is regenerated. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: secrets that were previously stripped from managed service env can now reach trusted exec children. - Mitigation: only operator-curated durable service env keys are considered, only trusted gateway exec posture receives the inherited allowlist, and dangerous-everywhere plus `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Risk: operators may assume all exec postures receive `GH_TOKEN` after placing it in `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Mitigation: docs state that the exec layer decides inheritance by posture and that these keys are not auto-propagated to untrusted commands.

target.title: feat(tools): introduce group:core for all built-in core tools
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `group:openclaw` is documented as "all built-in tools" but actually filters out filesystem and shell runtime tools, so there is no obvious `group:*` shorthand for "all built-in core tools." - Why it matters: operators writing `tools.allow: ["group:openclaw"]` expecting broad built-in access get a curated subset instead. A dedicated all-core group name removes the ambiguity. - What changed: added `group:core` as the all-built-in-core-tools shorthand; updated `group:openclaw` docs to accurately say "curated OpenClaw integration tools (excludes filesystem, shell runtime, and provider plugins)"; added `expandToolGroups` to the plugin allowlist normalizer so `group:*` entries work in plugin tool policy. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no profile semantics changed. `tools.profile: "full"` still means unrestricted, same as unset. Plugin tools are still not included by `group:core`. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: My OpenClaw runs in a non-admin account with it's own credentials. As such when configuring the browser plugin, I discovered that it had to be explicitly added to tools.allow and that I could allow all future configured plugins with `group:plugins` ```bash openclaw config set tools.allow '["group:plugins"]' --strict-json ``` However, when I did this my agent lost access to all core tools because if tools.allow is present it must specify everything allowed. So I went looking for a group similar to group:plugins and found it missing. Now, with this change full yolo looks like this. ```bash openclaw config set agents.defaults.sandbox.mode off openclaw config set tools.profile full openclaw config set tools.exec.ask off openclaw exec-policy preset yolo openclaw config set tools.allow '["group:core","group:plugins"]' --strict-json ``` - Real environment tested: Mac Mini with openclaw v2026.5.2 built from source ```bash git checkout v2026.5.2 git apply seank-group-core.patch pnpm install pnpm ui:build pnpm build ./scripts/package-mac-app.sh pnpm test src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts pnpm link --global ``` - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```bash openclaw config set tools.allow '["group:core","group:plugins"]' --strict-json openclaw gateway restart ``` - Evidence after fix (screenshot): - Observed result after fix: OpenClaw had access to all core tools as well as browser plugin - What was not tested: I only ran this live on the environment above, beside that just unittest suggested by OpenClaw and GitHub Copilot - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `group:openclaw` was both the only broad group name and a curated subset, creating a naming gap where no group meant "all built-in core tools." - Missing detection / guardrail: no test asserted the existence of an all-core group or verified that `group:openclaw` excluded fs/runtime tools. - Contributing context (if known): the naming collision was a design clarity problem, not a runtime regression. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts` - `src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts` - `src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: - `group:core` expands to built-in core tools. - Plugin optional tools are not enabled by `group:core` alone, but still work with `group:plugins`. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is pure policy expansion, so unit coverage around the catalog, global tool policy, and plugin allowlist boundary is sufficient. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing group expansion tests covered section groups and `group:openclaw`; this PR adds explicit coverage for `group:core`. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` now support `group:core` for all built-in core tools. - Docs now distinguish: - `tools.profile: "full"`: unrestricted profile, same as unset. - `group:core`: all built-in core tools, excluding plugin tools. - `group:openclaw`: curated OpenClaw integration tools, excluding filesystem, shell runtime, and plugin tools. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user wants broad built-in tool access] -> group:openclaw -> curated subset (misleading docs say "all") After: [user wants all built-in core tools] -> group:core -> all built-in core tools [user wants curated integration tools] -> group:openclaw -> curated subset (docs now accurate) [user wants unrestricted profile] -> tools.profile: full -> unrestricted baseline ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: `group:core` is an explicit allow/deny shorthand for existing built-in tools. It does not grant anything unless an operator opts into it in tool policy. Tests lock that it does not enable optional plugin tools by itself. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Windows 11 (rebase + verification); macOS Darwin 25.4.0 arm64 (original patch) - Runtime/container: Node 22, local OpenClaw repo - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): tool policy allowlist examples using `group:core` and `group:plugins` ### Steps 1. Configure `tools.allow: ["group:core"]`. 2. Resolve OpenClaw coding tools. 3. Verify built-in core tools such as `read`, `exec`, `canvas`, `message`, and `cron` are available. 4. Verify optional plugin tools are not enabled by `group:core` alone. ### Expected - `group:core` expands to built-in core tools. - Plugin tools still require explicit plugin/tool allowlist entries or `group:plugins`. ### Actual - Matches expected. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Tests after rebase onto current `upstream/main` (59326c8e3b): ```sh pnpm test src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts ``` ```text Ō£ō unit-fast src/agents/tool-policy.test.ts (32 tests) 23ms Ō£ō agents src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts (55 tests) 779ms Ō£ō plugins src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts (62 tests) 3773ms Test Files 3 passed (3) Tests 149 passed (149) ``` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Live gateway with `tools.allow: ["group:core"]` in agent config. - `group:core` is present in the core tool group map (`src/agents/tool-catalog.ts`). - Global allowlist expansion accepts `group:core`. - Optional plugin tools are not unlocked by `group:core` alone. - Docs list `group:core` and no longer describe `group:openclaw` as all built-in tools. - `expandToolGroups` correctly wired into plugin allowlist normalizer (`src/plugins/tools.ts`). - Edge cases checked: - `group:core` plus `group:plugins` still allows optional plugin tools. - Stale group naming references searched across `docs`, `src`, `packages`, and `README.md`. - `group:core` confirmed absent from `upstream/main` before this patch (not previously landed). - What you did **not** verify: - Full CI. - Rendered docs site. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. (`Yes/No`): No - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: users might assume `group:core` includes plugin tools. - Mitigation: docs state plugin tools are excluded, and tests verify optional plugins require `group:plugins` or explicit plugin/tool allowlist entries. - Risk: `group:openclaw` documentation could imply broader access than it actually provides. - Mitigation: docs now call it a curated integration group and explicitly distinguish it from `group:core`. ## Attribution - **OpenClaw (Codex 5.5)**: identified the naming gap, proposed the fix, authored the original commit and initial PR description. - **GitHub Copilot (Claude Opus 4.6)**: verified the fix was still viable on current `main`, rebased the commit (resolving 3 merge conflicts including an `expandToolGroups` import relocation to `tool-policy-shared.ts`), ran tests, and rewrote the PR description to match the current template.
